question: A polydisperse sample of polystyrene is prepared by mixing three monodisperse samples in the following proportions: $$\begin{array}{ll}1 \mathrm{~g} & 10,000 \text { molecular weight } \\ 2 \mathrm{~g} & 50,000 \text { molecular weight } \\ 2 \mathrm{~g} & 100,000 \text { molecular weight }\end{array}$$ Using this information, calculate the number-average molecular weight, weight-average molecular weight, and PDI of the mixture. Give your answer as a tuple (number-average molecular weight, weight-average molecular weight, PDI).

A polymer is fractionated and is found to have the continuous molecular-weight distribution shown below as a plot of the weight, $W$, of molecules having molecular weight, $M$, versus $M$. Given this molecular-weight distribution, calculate $\bar{M}_{\mathrm{n}}$ and $\bar{M}_{\mathrm{w}}$. Give your answer as a tuple ($\bar{M}_{\mathrm{n}}$, $\bar{M}_{\mathrm{w}}$).

The single-parameter Flory distribution is given as

$$
W(X)=X(\ln p)^{2} p^{X}
$$

where $X$ is the degree of polymerization and $p$ is the fractional monomer conversion in a step-growth polymerization. Using this equation, obtain expressions for the number-average and weight-average degrees of polymerization in terms of $X$ and $p$. Give your answer as a tuple (number-average degree of polymerization, weight-average degree of polymerization).

Using reactivity ratios of styrene (1) and 4-chlorostyrene (2) calculated from $Q-e$ values given in the table, determine the instantaneous copolymer composition (i.e., mole fraction of styrene) resulting from the free-radical copolymerization of an equimolar mixture of styrene and 4-chlorostyrene.

Calculate the number-average molecular weight of polystyrene obtained at the completion of an anionic (i.e., "living") polymerization in which 0.01 g of $n$-butyllithium and 10 g of styrene monomer are used. The molecular weights of butyllithium and styrene are 64.06 and 104.12 , respectively.

Consider the RAFT polymerization of 6.55 M methyl methacrylate (100.12 molecular weight) in benzene using a 1,1'-azobis(1-cyclohexanenitrile) $(0.0018 \mathrm{M})$ initiator and $2.48 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{M}$ of the RAFT agent $S$-dodecyl $S$-(2-cyano-4-carboxy)but-2-yl trithiocarbonate at $90^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. At a conversion of $92 \%$ of the monomer, estimate the number-average molecular weight.
Calculate the specific density of poly(2,6-dimethyl-1,4-phenylene oxide) using groupcontribution methods.
Calculate the fractional free volume (FFV) of atactic polystyrene (aPS) using only group contributions.
Calculate the $T_{\mathrm{g}}$ of bisphenol-A polycarbonate (PC) using van Krevelen's parameters. The repeating unit structure of PC is shown in the figure.
Calculate the oxygen permeability of polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) showing in the figure using the molar Permachor values listed in the Table.

Using MD solutions, calculate the diffusion coefficient of $\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{O}=\mathrm{O})$ in polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) at 298 K.

Chemical analysis in materials science laboratories is frequently done by means of the scanning electron microscope. In this instrument, an electron beam generates characteristic x-rays that can be used to identify chemical elements. This instrument samples a roughly cylindrical volume at the surface of a solid material. Calculate the number of atoms sampled in a $1-\mu \mathrm{m}$-diameter by $1-\mu \mathrm{m}$-deep cylinder in the surface of solid copper. Assuming $\text { density of copper }=8.93 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{cm}^{3}$ and $\text { atomic mass of copper }=63.55 \mathrm{amu}$.

One mole of solid MgO occupies a cube 22.37 mm on a side. Calculate the density of $\mathrm{MgO}$ (in $\mathrm{g} / \mathrm{cm}^{3}$). $ \text { atomic mass of } \mathrm{Mg}(\text { in } \mathrm{g}) = 24.31g$, $\text { atomic mass of } \mathrm{O}(\text { in } \mathrm{g}) = 16.00g$.

Calculate the coulombic force of attraction between $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ and $\mathrm{O}^{2-}$ in $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ and determine the repulsive force in this case. 

$$
r_{\mathrm{Na}^{+}}=0.098 \mathrm{~nm}
$$
$$
r_{\mathrm{O}^{2-}}=0.132 \mathrm{~nm}
$$

Give your answer as a tuple (coulombic force, repulsive force).

(a) Assuming that the ionic radii are $r_{\mathrm{Na}^{+}}=0.098 \mathrm{~nm}$ and $r_{\mathrm{Cl}^{-}}=0.181 \mathrm{~nm}$., calculate the coulombic force of attraction between $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ and $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ in NaCl. (b) What is the repulsive force in this case? 

Give your answer as a tuple (coulombic force of attraction, repulsive force).
Calculate the minimum radius ratio for a coordination number of 8.

Estimate the coordination number for the cation in each of these ceramic oxides: $\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}, \mathrm{~B}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}, \mathrm{CaO}, \mathrm{MgO}, \mathrm{SiO}_{2}$, and $\mathrm{TiO}_{2}$. 

$r_{\mathrm{Al}^{3+}}=0.057 \mathrm{~nm}, r_{\mathrm{B}^{3+}}=0.02 \mathrm{~nm}, r_{\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}}=0.106 \mathrm{~nm}$, $r_{\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}}=0.078 \mathrm{~nm}, r_{\mathrm{Si}^{4+}}=0.039 \mathrm{~nm}, r_{\mathrm{Ti}^{4+}}=0.064 \mathrm{~nm}$, and $r_{\mathrm{O}^{2-}}=$ 0.132 nm .

Give your answer as a tuple (Al2O3, B2O3, CaO, MgO, SiO2, TiO2).

Calculate the length of a polyethylene molecule, $\left\langle\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{4}\right\rangle_{n}$, where $n=500$.

Given the bonding energy curve for secondary bonding described by the "6-12" potential: $$E=-\frac{K_{A}}{a^{6}}+\frac{K_{R}}{a^{12}}$$ where $K_{A}=10.37 \times 10^{-78} \mathrm{~J} \cdot \mathrm{~m}^{6}$ and $K_{R}=16.16 \times 10^{-135} \mathrm{~J} \cdot \mathrm{~m}^{12}$, calculate the bond energy and bond length for argon. Give your answer as a tuple (bond length, bond energy).
Calculate the density of copper, given that copper is an fcc metal with an atomic radius of 0.128 nm and a molar mass of 63.55 g/mol.
Calculate the Ionic Packing Factor (IPF) of MgO, which has the NaCl structure.(Figure 3.9) Ionic radius of \mathrm{Mg}^{2+} is 0.078nm, Ionic radius of \mathrm{O}^{2-} is 0.132nm.
Given $a=0.420 \mathrm{~nm}$, calculate the density of MgO.

Calculate the number of C and H atoms in the polyethylene unit cell, given a density of $0.9979 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{cm}^{3}$ and unit-cell dimensions of $0.741 \mathrm{~nm} \times 0.494 \mathrm{~nm} \times 0.255 \mathrm{~nm}$. Give your answer as a tuple (number of C atoms, number of H atoms).
Calculate the APF for the diamond cubic structure. (Figure 3.20)
Given the atom radius is $0.117 \mathrm{~nm}$, calculate the density of silicon. Given V_{\text {unit cell }} & =98.5 r_{\mathrm{Si}}^{3}.
Which lattice points lie on the [110] direction in the fcc and fco unit cells? Give your answer as a tuple (X, Y, ...) where X, Y, ... are the lattice points in the [110] direction.
What is the angle between the [110] and [111] directions in the cubic system?
Identify the axial intercepts for the $(3 \overline{1} 1)$ plane. Give your answer as a tuple (X, Y, Z) where X, Y, Z represent the intercepts on the x, y, and z axes respectively.

Given $r_{W atom}=0.137nm$ and $r_{AI atom} = 0.143 nm$, calculate the linear density of atoms along the [111] direction in (a) bcc W and (b) fcc Al. 

Give your answer as a tuple (linear density of bcc tungsten, linear density of fcc aluminum).

Given rW = 0.137 nm for bcc W and rAl = 0.143 nm for fcc Al, calculate the planar density of atoms in the (111) plane of (a) bcc tungsten and (b) fcc aluminum.  

Give your answer as a tuple: (bcc_tungsten_planar_density, fcc_aluminum_planar_density).
Calculate the planar density of ions in the (111) plane of MgO. Given the lattice constant $a=0.420 \mathrm{~nm}$.
Given r silicon = 0.117 nm, calculate the linear density of atoms along the [111] direction in silicon.
Given r silicon = 0.117 nm, calculate the planar density of atoms in the (111) plane of silicon.

Using Bragg's law, calculate the diffraction angles (2θ) for the first three peaks in the aluminum powder pattern, given that the first three peaks correspond to the (111), (200), and (220) planes, the lattice parameter $a=0.404 \mathrm{~nm}$, and the wavelength of the x-ray used is $\lambda=0.1542 \mathrm{~nm}$. 

Give your answer as a tuple (diffraction angle for (111) plane, diffraction angle for (200) plane, diffraction angle for (220) plane).

How much "oversize" is the C atom in $\alpha-\mathrm{Fe}$ ? Given the radius of the iron item is 0.124 nm and the radius carbon is 0.077 nm. Give your answer as a tuple: (ideal_interstitial_radius, carbon_radius_ratio). Refer to Figure 4.4

The fraction of vacant lattice sites in a crystal is typically small. For example, the fraction of aluminum sites vacant at $400^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is $2.29 \times 10^{-5}$. Calculate the density of these sites (in units of $\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ ). Given the density of aluminum to be $2.70 \mathrm{Mg} / \mathrm{m}^{3}$ and its atomic mass to be 26.98 amu .

Using the atomic radii RFe=0.124 nm for bcc Fe and RAl=0.143 nm for fcc Al, calculate the magnitude of the Burgers vector for (a) α-Fe and (b) Al.

Give your answer as a tuple (magnitude for α-Fe, magnitude for Al).
Given that Al is fcc with lattice parameter a = 0.405 nm, calculate the separation distance of dislocations in a low-angle $\left(\theta=2^{\circ}\right)$ tilt boundary in aluminum.
Calculate the grain-size number, $G$, for a micrograph for which you measure $8.04 \frac{\text { grains }}{\text { in. } .^{2}}$ at $100 \times$.

Randomization of atomic packing in amorphous metals (e.g., Figure 4.20) generally causes no more than a $1 \%$ drop in density compared with the crystalline structure of the same composition. Given the normal density for nickel (which would be in the crystalline state) is $8.91 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{cm}^{3}$ and the APF for the fcc metal structure is 0.74. Calculate the APF of an amorphous, thin film of nickel whose density is $8.84 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{cm}^{3}$.

The rate at which a metal alloy oxidizes in an oxygen-containing atmosphere is a typical example of the practical utility of the Arrhenius equation (rate $=C e^{-Q / R T}$). For example, the rate of oxidation of a magnesium alloy is represented by a rate constant, $k$. The value of $k$ at $300^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is $1.05 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{~kg} /\left(\mathrm{m}^{4} \cdot \mathrm{~s}\right)$. At $400^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, the value of $k$ rises to $2.95 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~kg} /\left(\mathrm{m}^{4} \cdot \mathrm{~s}\right)$. Calculate the activation energy, $Q$, for this oxidation process (in units of $\mathrm{kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$ ).

At $400^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, the fraction of aluminum lattice sites vacant is $2.29 \times 10^{-5}$. Calculate the fraction at $660^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ (just below its melting point). From the text discussion relative to Figure 5.4, we have $E_{V}=0.76 \mathrm{eV}$.

Steel surfaces can be hardened by carburization, as discussed relative to Figure. During one such treatment at $1,000^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, there is a drop in carbon concentration from 5 to 4 at $\%$ carbon between 1 and 2 mm from the surface of the steel. Estimate the flux of carbon atoms into the steel in this near-surface region. (The density of $\gamma$-Fe at $1,000^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is $7.63 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{cm}^{3}$.)

A 5-mm-thick sheet of palladium with a cross-sectional area of $0.2 \mathrm{~m}^{2}$ is used as a steady-state diffusional membrane for purifying hydrogen. If the hydrogen concentration on the high-pressure (impure gas) side of the sheet is $0.3 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^{3}$ and the diffusion coefficient for hydrogen in Pd is $1.0 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}$, calculate the mass of hydrogen being purified per hour. Assume the hydrogen concentration on the low-pressure side is maintained at 0 kg/m³ (e.g. by vacuum).

(b) Calculate the diameter if this rod is subjected to a $6-\mathrm{kN}$ compressive load. Give your answer as a tuple (resulting diameter under tensile load, resulting diameter under compressive load).

A glass plate contains an atomic-scale surface crack. (Take the cracktip radius $\simeq$ diameter of an $\mathrm{O}^{2-}$ ion.) Given that the crack is $1-\mu \mathrm{m}$ long, the theoretical strength of the defect-free glass is 7.0 GPa , and $r_{\mathrm{O}^{2-}}=0.132 \mathrm{~nm}$. Calculate the breaking strength of the plate.

The following data are collected in a flexural test of a nylon to be used in the fabrication of lightweight gears:
Test piece geometry: $7 \mathrm{~mm} \times 13 \mathrm{~mm} \times 100 \mathrm{~mm}$,
Distance between supports $=L=50 \mathrm{~mm}$,
and
Initial slope of load-deflection curve $=404 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}$.

A small, uniaxial stress of $1 \mathrm{MPa}(145 \mathrm{psi})$ is applied to a rod of high-density polyethylene. (a) Given $E=830 \mathrm{MPa}$, what is the resulting strain? In the absence of stress, the center-to-center atomic separation distance of two Fe atoms is 0.2480 nm (along a $\langle 111\rangle$ direction). How much $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}_2$ must be added to ethylene to yield an average degree of polymerization of (a) 500 and (b) 1,000 ? Give your answer as a tuple: (H2O2_wt_percent_a, H2O2_wt_percent_b).
Calculate the degree of polymerization for a polyacetal molecule with a molecular weight of 25,000 amu. Give your answer as the value of degree_of_polymerization.
Calculate the density of a particulate composite containing 50 vol \% W particles in a copper matrix. Give your answer as the value of density.
Calculate the composite modulus for a composite with 50 vol \% E-glass in a polyester matrix. Give your answer as the value of composite_modulus.
How many free electrons would there be in a spool of high-purity copper wire ( 1 mm diameter $\times 10 \mathrm{~m}$ long)? Give your answer as the value of free_electrons.
What is the probability of an electron's being promoted to the conduction band in silicon at $50^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ? Give your answer as the value of probability.
Calculate the conductivity at $200^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ of (a) copper (annealed standard) and (b) tungsten. Give your answer as a tuple: (copper_conductivity, tungsten_conductivity).
Estimate the resistivity of a copper $-0.06 \mathrm{wt} \%$ phosphorus alloy at $200^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Give your answer as the value of resistivity.

Using the data in Table, calculate (a) the total conductivity and (b) the resistivity of Si at room temperature. Give your answer as a tuple: (total_conductivity, resistivity).

(a) Calculate the conductivity of germanium at $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, and (b) plot the conductivity over the range of 27 to $200^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ as an Arrhenius-type plot similar to that shown in Figure. Give your answer as the value of conductivity.
Calculate the intrinsic conductivity of $\operatorname{InSb}$ at $50^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$.
For intrinsic InSb , calculate the fraction of the current carried by electrons and the fraction carried by electron holes. Give your answer as a tuple: (fraction_electrons, fraction_holes).
Calculate the energy of a single photon from the long wavelength (red) end of the visible spectrum (at 700 nm ). Give your answer as the value of energy.

Using Fresnel's formula, calculate the reflectance, $R$, of (a) a sheet of polypropylene and (b) a sheet of polytetrafluoroethylene (with an average refractive index of 1.35). Give your answer as a tuple: (reflectance_polypropylene, reflectance_polytetrafluoroethylene).

What is the reflectance of single-crystal sapphire, which is widely used as an optical and electronic material? (Sapphire is nearly pure $\mathrm{Al}_2 \mathrm{O}_3$.) Give your answer as the value of reflectance.
Calculate the magnetic moment of a unit cell of copper ferrite. Give your answer as the value of magnetic_moment.

(a) Calculate the magnetic moment of a unit cell of $\mathrm{MgFe}_2 \mathrm{O}_4$ in an inverse spinel structure. (b) Repeat part (a) for the case of a normal spinel structure. (c) Given that the experimental value of the unitcell moment for $\mathrm{MgFe}_2 \mathrm{O}_4$ is $8.8 \mu_B$, estimate the fraction of the ferrite in the inverse spinel structure. Give your answer as a tuple: (magnetic_moment_inverse_spinel, magnetic_moment_normal_spinel, inverse_spinel_fraction).

Calculate the volume of $\mathrm{O}_2$ gas consumed (at STP) by the corrosion of 100 g of chromium. (In this case, trivalent $\mathrm{Cr}^{3+}$ ions are found at the anode.) Give your answer as the value of volume.

A gold O-ring is used to form a gastight seal in a high-vacuum chamber. The ring is formed from a $100-\mathrm{mm}$ length of $1.5-\mathrm{mm}$-diameter wire. Calculate the number of gold atoms in the O-ring. Give your answer as the value of number_of_gold_atoms.

In a metal-oxide-semiconductor (MOS) device, a thin layer of $\mathrm{SiO}_2$ (density $=2.20 \mathrm{Mg} / \mathrm{m}^3$ ) is grown on a single crystal chip of silicon. How many Si atoms and how many O atoms are present per square millimeter of the oxide layer? Assume that the layer thickness is 150 nm . Give your answer as a tuple: (Si_atoms_per_mm2, O_atoms_per_mm2).

$\mathrm{An} \mathrm{Al}_2 \mathrm{O}_3$ whisker is a small single crystal used to reinforce metal-matrix composites. Given a cylindrical shape, calculate the number of Al atoms and the number of O atoms in a whisker with a diameter of $1 \mu \mathrm{~m}$ and a length of $25 \mu \mathrm{~m}$. (The density of $\mathrm{Al}_2 \mathrm{O}_3$ is $3.97 \mathrm{Mg} / \mathrm{m}^3$.) Give your answer as a tuple: (number_of_Al_atoms, number_of_O_atoms).

Thirty grams of magnesium filings are to be oxidized in a laboratory demonstration. (a) How many $\mathrm{O}_2$ molecules would be consumed in this demonstration? (b) How many moles of $\mathrm{O}_2$ does this represent? Give your answer as a tuple: (O2_molecules, O2_moles).

A copper penny has a mass of 2.60 g . Assuming pure copper, how much of this mass is contributed by (a) the neutrons in the copper nuclei and (b) electrons? Give your answer as a tuple: (neutron_mass, electron_mass).

So far, we have concentrated on the coulombic force of attraction between ions. But like ions repel each other. A nearest neighbor pair of $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ions in Figure 2.5 are separated by a distance of $\sqrt{2} a_0$, where $a_0$ is defined in Figure 2.7. Calculate the coulombic force of repulsion between such a pair of like ions. Give your answer as the value of coulombic_force.

A common feature in high-temperature ceramic superconductors is a $\mathrm{Cu}-\mathrm{O}$ sheet that serves as a superconducting plane. Calculate the coulombic force of attraction between a $\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}$ and an $\mathrm{O}^{2-}$ within one of these sheets. Give your answer as the value of coulombic_force.

For an ionic crystal, such as NaCl , the net coulombic bonding force is a simple multiple of the force of attraction between an adjacent ion pair. To demonstrate this concept, consider the hypothetical, onedimensional "crystal" shown in fugure: (a) Show that the net coulombic force of attraction between the reference ion and all other ions in the crystal is $F=A F_c$ where $F_c$ is the force of attraction between an adjacent ion pair (see Equation($F_c=\frac{-K}{a^2}$)) and $A$ is a series expansion. (b) Determine the value of $A$. Give your answer as the value of A.

Neoprene is a synthetic rubber, polychloroprene, with a chemical structure similar to that of natural rubber , except that it contains a Cl atom in place of the $\mathrm{CH}_3$ group of the isoprene molecule. (a) Sketch the polymerization reaction for neoprene, and (b) calculate the reaction energy (per mole) for this polymerization. (c) Calculate the total energy released during the polymerization of 1 kg of chloroprene. Give your answer as a tuple: (reaction_energy_per_mole, total_energy_released).

The first step in the formation of phenolformaldehyde, a common phenolic polymer, is shown in Figure. Calculate the net reaction energy (per mole) for this step in the overall polymerization reaction.

The monomer upon which a common acrylic polymer, polymethyl methacrylate, is based is as figure. Calculate the molecular weight of a polymethyl methacrylate molecule with $n=600$. Give your answer as the value of molecular_weight.

The secondary bonding of gas molecules to a solid surface is a common mechanism for measuring the surface area of porous materials. By lowering the temperature of a solid well below room temperature, a measured volume of gas will condense to form a monolayer coating of molecules on the porous surface. For a 100-g sample of fused copper catalyst, a volume of $9 \times 10^3 \mathrm{~mm}^3$ of nitrogen (measured at standard temperature and pressure, $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and 1 atm ) is required to form a monolayer upon condensation. Calculate the surface area of the catalyst in units of $\mathrm{m}^2 / \mathrm{kg}$. (Take the area covered by a nitrogen molecule as $0.162 \mathrm{~nm}^2$ and recall that, for an ideal gas, $p V=n R T$, where $n$ is the number of moles of the gas.) Give your answer as the value of surface_area.

Small-diameter noble gas atoms, such as helium, can dissolve in the relatively open network structure of silicate glasses. (See Figure for a schematic of glass structure.) The secondary bonding of helium in vitreous silica is represented by a heat of solution, $\Delta H_s$, of $-3.96 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$. The relationship between solubility, $S$, and the heat of solution is $$ S=S_0 e^{-\Delta H s /(R T)} $$ where $S_0$ is a constant, $R$ is the gas constant, and $T$ is the absolute temperature (in K ). If the solubility of helium in vitreous silica is $5.51 \times 10^{23}$ atoms $/\left(\mathrm{m}^3 \cdot \mathrm{~atm}\right)$ at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, calculate the solubility at $250^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Give your answer as the value of solubility.
Calculate the density of $\mathrm{UO}_2$. Give your answer as the value of density.
Calculate the IPF for cristobalite (see Figure). Give your answer as the value of IPF.
Calculate the reaction energy involved in forming a single unit cell of polyethylene.
Calculate the APF for polyethylene. Give your answer as the value of APF.

(a) Sketch, in a cubic unit cell, [100] and [210] directions. (b) Use a trigonometric calculation to determine the angle between these directions. (c) Use Equation($\cos \delta=\frac{\mathbf{D} \cdot \mathbf{D}^{\prime}}{\left|\mathbf{D} \| \mathbf{D}^{\prime}\right|}=\frac{u u^{\prime}+v v^{\prime}+w w^{\prime}}{\sqrt{u^2+v^2+w^2} \sqrt{\left(u^{\prime}\right)^2+\left(v^{\prime}\right)^2+\left(w^{\prime}\right)^2}}$) to determine this angle. Give your answer as a tuple: (angle_trigonometric, angle_equation).

What $[h k l]$ direction connects the adjacent face-centered positions $\frac{1}{2} \frac{1}{2} 0$ and $\frac{1}{2} 0 \frac{1}{2}$ ? Illustrate your answer with a sketch. Give your answer as the value of [hkl_direction].

In Figures 3.4 (b) and $3.5(\mathrm{~b})$, we show atoms and fractional atoms making up a unit cell. An alternative convention is to describe the unit cell in terms of "equivalent points." For example, the two atoms in the bcc unit cell can be considered to be one corner atom at 000 and one body-centered atom at $\frac{1}{2} \frac{1}{2}$. The one corner atom is equivalent to the eight $\frac{1}{8}$ atoms shown in Figure 3.4(b). In a similar way, identify the four atoms associated with equivalent points in the fcc structure. Give your answer as a tuple: (equivalent_point_1, equivalent_point_2, equivalent_point_3, equivalent_point_4).
Calculate the planar density of ions along the (111) plane in zinc blende (see Figure). Give your answer as the value of planar_density.
What would be the $(h k l)$ indices for the three lowest diffraction-angle peaks for an hcp metal? Give your answer as a tuple: (hkl_index_1, hkl_index_2, hkl_index_3).

Although the Hume-Rothery rules apply strictly only to metals, the concept of similarity of cations corresponds to the complete solubility of NiO in MgO (Figure). Calculate the percent difference between cation sizes in this case. Give your answer as the value of percent_difference.
Calculate the number of $\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$ vacancies produced by the solubility of 1 mol of $\mathrm{Al}_2 \mathrm{O}_3$ in 99 mol of MgO (see Figure). Give your answer as the value of Mg_vacancies.

We shall be especially interested in "doped" semiconductors, in which small levels of impurities are added to an essentially pure semiconductor in order to produce desirable electrical properties. For silicon with $4 \times 10^{21}$ aluminum atoms per cubic meter in solid solution, calculate (a) the atomic percent of aluminum atoms and (b) the weight percent of aluminum atoms. Give your answer as a tuple: (atomic_percent, weight_percent).

For $4 \times 10^{21}$ phosphorus atoms $/ \mathrm{m}^3$ in solid solution in silicon, calculate (a) the atomic percent of phosphorous atoms and (b) the weight percent of phosphorous atoms. Give your answer as a tuple: (atomic_percent, weight_percent).

Calculate the density of vacant sites (in $\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ ) in a single crystal of silicon if the fraction of vacant lattice sites is $1 \times 10^{-7}$. Give your answer as the value of density_of_vacant_sites.

Calculate the density of Schottky pairs (in $\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ ) in MgO if the fraction of vacant lattice sites is $5 \times 10^{-6}$. (The density of MgO is $3.60 \mathrm{Mg} / \mathrm{m}^3$.) Give your answer as the value of density_of_Schottky_pairs.

The energy necessary to generate a dislocation is proportional to the square of the length of the Burgers vector, $|\mathbf{b}|^2$. This relationship means that the most stable (lowest energy) dislocations have the minimum length, $|\mathbf{b}|$. For the bcc metal structure, calculate (relative to $E_{\mathrm{b}}=[111]$ ) the dislocation energies for (a) $E_{\mathrm{b}}=[110]$ and (b) $E_{\mathrm{b}}=[100]$. Give your answer as a tuple: (dislocation_energy_110, dislocation_energy_100).

Determine the grain-size number, $G$, for the microstructure shown in Figure. (Keep in mind that the precise answer will depend on your choice of an area of sampling.) Give your answer as the value of $G$.

In steel-making furnaces, temperature-resistant ceramic bricks (refractories) are used as linings to contain the molten metal. A common byproduct of the steel-making process is a calcium aluminosilicate (slag) that is chemically corrosive to the refractories. For alumina refractories, the corrosion rate is $2.0 \times$ $10^{-8} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ at $1,425^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $8.95 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ at $1,500^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Calculate the activation energy for the corrosion of these alumina refractories. Give your answer as the value of activation_energy.

Manufacturing traditional clayware ceramics typically involves driving off the water of hydration in the clay minerals. The rate constant for the dehydration of kaolinite, a common clay mineral, is $1.0 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$ at $485^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $1.0 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$ at $525^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Calculate the activation energy for the dehydration of kaolinite. Give your answer as the value of activation_energy.

Using data in Table, calculate the self-diffusivity for iron in bec iron at $900^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Give your answer as the value of self_diffusivity.

The diffusion coefficient of nickel in an austenitic (fcc structure) stainless steel is $10^{-22} \mathrm{~m}^2 / \mathrm{s}$ at $500^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $10^{-15} \mathrm{~m}^2 / \mathrm{s}$ at $1,000^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Calculate the activation energy for the diffusion of nickel in this alloy over this temperature range. Give your answer as the value of activation_energy.

A differential nitrogen pressure exists across a $2-\mathrm{mm}$ thick steel furnace wall. After some time, steadystate diffusion of the nitrogen is established across the wall. Given that the nitrogen concentration on the high-pressure surface of the wall is $2 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^3$ and on the low-pressure surface is $0.2 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^3$, calculate the flow of nitrogen through the wall (in $\mathrm{kg} / \mathrm{m}^2 \cdot \mathrm{~h}$ ) if the diffusion coefficient for nitrogen in this steel is $1.0 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{~m}^2 / \mathrm{s}$ at the furnace operating temperature. Give your answer as the value of nitrogen_flow.

The endpoints of the Arrhenius plot of $D_{\text {grain boundary }}$ in Figure are $D_{\text {grain boundary }}=3.2 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{~m}^2 / \mathrm{s}$ at a temperature of $457^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and D_{\text {grain boundary }}=1.0 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{~m}^2 / \mathrm{s} at a temperature of $689^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Using these data, calculate the activation energy for grain-boundary diffusion in silver. Give your answer as the value of activation_energy.

The following three $\sigma-\epsilon$ data points are provided for a titanium alloy for aerospace applications: $\epsilon=0.002778$ (at $\sigma=300 \mathrm{MPa}), 0.005556(600 \mathrm{MPa})$, and $0.009897(900 \mathrm{MPa})$. Calculate $E$ for this alloy. Give your answer as the value of $E$.

In normal motion, the load exerted on the hip joint is 2.5 times body weight. (a) Calculate the corresponding stress (in MPa) on an artificial hip implant with a cross-sectional area of $5.64 \mathrm{~cm}^2$ in a patient weighing $150 \mathrm{lb}_{f .}$ (b) Calculate the corresponding strain if the implant is made of $\mathrm{Ti}-6 \mathrm{Al}-4 \mathrm{~V}$, which has an elastic modulus of 124 GPa . Give your answer as a tuple: (stress, strain).

In analyzing residual stress by x-ray diffraction, the stress constant, $K_1$, is used, where $$ K_1=\frac{E \cot \theta}{2(1+\nu) \sin ^2 \psi} $$ and where $E$ and $\nu$ are the elastic constants, $\theta$ is a Bragg angle, and $\psi$ is an angle of rotation of the sample during the x-ray diffraction experiment (generally $\psi=45^{\circ}$ ). To maximize experimental accuracy, one prefers to use the largest possible Bragg angle, $\theta$. However, hardware configuration (see Figure) prevents $\theta$ from being greater than $80^{\circ}$. (a) Calculate the maximum $\theta$ for a 1040 carbon steel using $\mathrm{CrK}_\alpha$ radiation ( $\lambda=0.2291 \mathrm{~nm}$ ). (Note that 1040 steel is nearly pure iron, which is a bcc metal, and that the reflection rules for a bcc metal are given in Table.) (b) Calculate the value of the stress constant for 1040 steel. Give your answer as a tuple: (maximum_theta, stress_constant).
(Reference to Table:
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|}
\hline \multicolumn{3}{|l|}{Reflection Rules of X-Ray Diffraction for the Common Metal Structures} \\
\hline Crystal structure & Diffraction does not occur when & Diffraction occurs when \\
\hline Body-centered cubic (bcc) & $h+k+l=$ odd number & $h+k+l=$ even number \\
\hline Face-centered cubic (fcc) & $h, k, l$ mixed (i.e., both even and odd numbers) & $h, k, l$ unmixed (i.e., are all even numbers or all are odd numbers) \\
\hline Hexagonal close packed (hcp) & $(h+2 k)=3 n, l$ odd ( $n$ is an integer) & All other cases \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
)

(a) The following data are collected for a modulus of rupture test on an MgO refractory brick (refer to Equation $\mathrm{MOR}=\frac{3 F L}{2 b h^2}$ and Figure): $$ \begin{aligned} F & =7.0 \times 10^4 \mathrm{~N} \\ L & =175 \mathrm{~mm} \\ b & =110 \mathrm{~mm} \end{aligned} $$ and $$ h=75 \mathrm{~mm} $$ Calculate the modulus of rupture. (b) Suppose that you are given a similar MgO refractory with the same strength and same dimensions except that its height, $h$, is only 64 mm . What would be the load ( $F$ ) necessary to break this thinner refractory? Give your answer as a tuple: (modulus_of_rupture, load).

A freshly drawn glass fiber $(100 \mu \mathrm{~m}$ diameter $)$ breaks under a tensile load of 40 N . After subsequent handling, a similar fiber breaks under a tensile load of 0.15 N . Assuming the first fiber was free of defects and that the second fiber broke due to an atomically sharp surface crack, calculate the length of that crack.

The following data are collected in a flexural test of a polyester to be used in the exterior trim of an automobile: Test piece geometry: $6 \mathrm{~mm} \times 15 \mathrm{~mm} \times 50 \mathrm{~mm}$, Distance between supports $=L=60 \mathrm{~mm}$, and Initial slope of load-deflection curve $=538 \times 10^3 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}$. Calculate the flexural modulus of this engineering polymer. Give your answer as the value of flexural_modulus.

Figure illustrates the effect of humidity on stress-versus-strain behavior for a nylon 66. In addition, the distinction between tensile and compressive behavior is shown. Approximating the data between 0 and 20 MPa as a straight line, calculate (a) the initial elastic modulus in tension and (b) the initial elastic modulus in compression for the nylon at $60 \%$ relative humidity. Give your answer as a tuple: (initial_elastic_modulus_tension, initial_elastic_modulus_compression).

The maximum modulus of elasticity for a copper crystal is 195 GPa . What tensile stress is required along the corresponding crystallographic direction in order to increase the interatomic separation distance by $0.05 \%$ ? Give your answer as the value of tensile_stress.

Consider the slip systems for aluminum shown in Figure. For an applied tensile stress in the [111] direction, which slip system(s) would be most likely to operate? Give your answer as a tuple: (slip_systems).

You are provided with an unknown alloy with a measured BHN of 100. Having no other information than the data of Figure, estimate the tensile strength of the alloy. (Express your answer in the form.) Give your answer as the value of tensile_strength.

You are asked to measure nondestructively the yield strength and tensile strength of an annealed 65-45-12 cast iron structural member. Fortunately, a small hardness indentation in this structural design will not impair its future usefulness, which is a working definition of nondestructive. A $10-\mathrm{mm}$-diameter tungsten carbide sphere creates a $4.26-\mathrm{mm}$-diameter impression under a $3,000-\mathrm{kg}$ load. What are the yield and tensile strengths? Give your answer as a tuple: (yield_strength, tensile_strength).

An alloy is evaluated for potential creep deformation in a short-term laboratory experiment. The creep rate ( $\dot{\epsilon}$ ) is found to be $1 \%$ per hour at $800^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $5.5 \times 10^{-2} \%$ per hour at $700^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. (a) Calculate the activation energy for creep in this temperature range. (b) Estimate the creep rate to be expected at a service temperature of $500^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. (c) What important assumption underlies the validity of your answer in part (b)? 

Give your answer as a tuple: (activation_energy, creep_rate, assumption).

The stress on a rubber disk is seen to relax from 0.75 to 0.5 MPa in 100 days. (a) What is the relaxation time, $\tau$, for this material? (b) What will be the stress on the disk after (i) 50 days, (ii) 200 days, or (iii) 365 days? (Consider time $=0$ to be at the stress level of 0.75 MPa .) Give your answer as a tuple: (relaxation_time, stress_after_50_days, stress_after_200_days, stress_after_365_days).

A borosilicate glass used for sealed-beam headlights has an annealing point of $544^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and a softening point of $780^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Calculate (a) the activation energy for viscous deformation in this glass, (b) its working range, and (c) its melting range. Give your answer as a tuple: (activation_energy, working_range, melting_range).

Estimate the amount of heat (in J) required to raise 2 kg of (a) $\alpha$-iron, (b) graphite, and (c) polypropylene from room temperature $\left(25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)$ to $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Give your answer as a tuple: (heat_alpha_iron, heat_graphite, heat_polypropylene).

A house designed for passive solar heating has a substantial amount of brickwork in its interior to serve as a heat absorber. Each brick weighs 2.0 kg and has a specific heat of $850 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{kg} \cdot \mathrm{K}$. How many bricks are needed to absorb $5.0 \times 10^4 \mathrm{~kJ}$ of heat by a temperature increase of $10^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ? Give your answer as the value of number_of_bricks.

At room temperature $\left(25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)$, a 5.000 -mm-diameter tungsten pin is too large for a 4.999 -mm-diameter hole in a nickel bar. To what temperature must these two parts be heated in order for the pin to just fit? Give your answer as the value of temperature.

Calculate the rate of heat loss per square meter through the fireclay refractory wall of a furnace operated at $1,000^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The external face of the furnace wall is at $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, and the wall is 10 cm thick. Give your answer as the value of heat_loss_rate.
What would be the stress developed in a mullite furnace tube constrained in the way illustrated in Figure if it were heated to $1,000^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ?

As pointed out in the Feature Box in this chapter, the thermal protection system for NASA's Space Shuttle Orbiter was effective due to its exceptional thermalinsulation properties. Calculate the heat flow (per square meter) across a 50-mm-thick ceramic tile with an outer surface temperature of $700^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and an inner surface temperature of $30^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, given a thermal conductivity of $0.0837 \mathrm{~J} /(\mathrm{s} \cdot \mathrm{m} \cdot \mathrm{K})$.
A silicon-nitride turbine rotor fractures at a stress level of 300 MPa . Estimate the flaw size responsible for this failure. Give your answer as the value of flaw_size.
Estimate the flaw size responsible for the failure of a turbine rotor made from partially stabilized zirconia that fractures at a stress level of 300 MPa .
Calculate the breaking stress for a rod of ABS with a surface flaw size of $100 \mu \mathrm{~m}$.

A structural steel with a fracture toughness of $60 \mathrm{MPa} \sqrt{\mathrm{m}}$ has no surface crack larger than 3 mm in length. By how much (in \%) would this largest surface crack have to grow before the system would experience fast fracture under an applied stress of 500 MPa ?

The application of a C11000 copper wire in a con-trol-circuit design will involve cyclic loading for extended periods at the elevated temperatures of a production plant. Use the data of Figure to specify an upper temperature limit to ensure a fatigue strength of at least 100 MPa for a stress life of $10^7$ cycles.

A small pressure vessel is fabricated from an acetal polymer. The stress in the vessel wall is $$ \sigma=\frac{p r}{2 t} $$ where $p$ is the internal pressure, $r$ is the outer radius of the sphere, and $t$ is the wall thickness. For the vessel in question, $r=30 \mathrm{~mm}$ and $t=2 \mathrm{~mm}$. What is the maximum permissible internal pressure for this design if the application is at room temperature and the wall stress is only tensile (due to internal pressurizations that will occur no more than $10^6$ times)? (See Figure for relevant data.)

In doing x-radiography of steel, assume the film can detect a variation in radiation intensity represented by $\Delta I / I_0=0.001$. What thickness variation could be detected using this system for the inspection of a $12.5-\mathrm{mm}$-thick plate of steel using a $100-\mathrm{keV}$ beam? Give your answer as a tuple: (thickness_variation_positive, thickness_variation_negative).

For an $x$-radiographic inspection of the plate in Figure using a $100-\mathrm{keV}$ beam, calculate the $\%$ change in $I / I_0$ between the flawed and flaw-free areas given that the effect of the flaw is to reduce the effective thickness of the plate by $10 \mu \mathrm{~m}$. Assume the sample is aluminum with a flaw depth at 10 mm and an overall sample thickness of 20 mm. Give your answer as the value of %_change_in_I_I0.

Calculate the degrees of freedom for a $50: 50$ copper-nickel alloy at (a) $1,400^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, where it exists as a single, liquid phase; (b) $1,300^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, where it exists as a two-phase mixture of liquid and solid solutions; and (c) $1,200^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, where it exists as a single, solid-solution phase. Assume a constant pressure of 1 atm above the alloy in each case. Give your answer as a tuple: (degrees_of_freedom_a, degrees_of_freedom_b, degrees_of_freedom_c).

Calculate the amount of each phase present in 1 kg of a $50 \mathrm{wt} \% \mathrm{Ni}-50 \mathrm{wt} \% \mathrm{Cu}$ alloy at (a) $1,400^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, (b) $1,300^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, and (c) $1,200^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ (see Figure). Give your answer as a tuple: (m_L_1400, m_alpha_1400, m_L_1300, m_alpha_1300, m_L_1200, m_alpha_1200).

Calculate the amount of proeutectoid $\alpha$ present at the grain boundaries in 1 kg of a common 1020 structural steel ( $0.20 \mathrm{wt} \% \mathrm{C}$ ). (See Figure.) Give your answer as the value of proeutectoid_alpha_amount.

Suppose that you have a crucible containing 1 kg of an alloy of composition $90 \mathrm{wt} \% \mathrm{Sn}-10 \mathrm{wt} \% \mathrm{~Pb}$ at a temperature of $184^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. How much Sn would you have to add to the crucible to completely solidify the alloy without changing the system temperature? (See Figure.) Give your answer as the value of Sn_to_add.

In a test laboratory, quantitative x-ray diffraction determines that a refractory brick has $25 \mathrm{wt} \%$ alumina phase and $75 \mathrm{wt} \%$ mullite solid solution. What is the overall $\mathrm{SiO}_2$ content (in wt \%) of this material? (See Figure.)

Calculate the weight fraction of mullite that is proeutectic in a slowly cooled $30 \mathrm{~mol} \% \mathrm{Al}_2 \mathrm{O}_3-70 \mathrm{~mol}$ $\% \mathrm{SiO}_2$ refractory cooled to room temperature. Give your answer as the value of weight_fraction.

A solder batch is made by melting together 64 g of a $40: 60 \mathrm{~Pb}-\mathrm{Sn}$ alloy with 53 g of a $60: 40 \mathrm{~Pb}-\mathrm{Sn}$ alloy. Calculate the amounts of $\alpha$ and $\beta$ phase that would be present in the overall alloy, assuming it is slowly cooled to room temperature, $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Give your answer as a tuple: (alpha_phase_amount, beta_phase_amount).

For an aluminum alloy, the activation energy for crystal growth is $120 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$. By what factor would the rate of crystal growth change by dropping the alloy temperature from $500^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to room temperature $\left(25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)$ ? Give your answer as the value of growth_rate_factor.

Relative to Figure, derive an expression for $r_c$ as a function of $\sigma$, the surface energy per unit area of the nucleus, and $\Delta G_v$, the volume energy reduction per unit volume. Recall that the area of a sphere is $4 \pi r^2$, and its volume is $\left(\frac{4}{3}\right) \pi r^3$. Give your answer as the value of $r_c$.
(a) A eutectoid steel is cooled at a steady rate from 727 to $200^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ in exactly 1 day, determine at what temperature a phase transformation would first be observed.

(a) Specify a quench rate necessary to ensure a hardness of at least Rockwell C40 in a 4140 steel. (b) Specify a quench rate necessary to ensure a hardness of no more than Rockwell C40 in the same alloy. Give your answer as a tuple: (minimum_quench_rate, maximum_quench_rate).

A flywheel shaft is made of 8640 steel. The surface hardness is found to be Rockwell C35. By what percentage would the cooling rate at the point in question have to change in order for the hardness to be increased to a more desirable value of Rockwell C45?

Quenching a bar of 4140 steel at $700^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ into a stirred water bath produces an instantaneous quench rate at the surface of $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C} / \mathrm{s}$. Use Jominy test data to predict the surface hardness resulting from this quench. (Note: The quench described is not in the Jominy configuration. Nonetheless, Jominy data provide general information on hardness as a function of quench rate.) Give your answer as the value of surface_hardness.

In heat-treating a complex-shaped part made from a 5140 steel, a final quench in stirred oil leads to a hardness of Rockwell C30 at 3 mm beneath the surface. This hardness is unacceptable, as design specifications require a hardness of Rockwell C45 at that point. Select an alloy substitution to provide this hardness, assuming the heat treatment must remain the same. Give your answer as a tuple: (alloy_substitution1, alloy_substitution2).
Specify an aging temperature for a $95 \mathrm{Al}-5 \mathrm{Cu}$ alloy that will produce a maximum of $5 \mathrm{wt} \% \theta$ precipitate. Give your answer as the value of aging_temperature.

As second-phase precipitation is a thermally activated process, an Arrhenius expression can be used to estimate the time required to reach maximum hardness (see Figure). As a first approximation, you can treat $t_{\max }^{-1}$ as a "rate," where $t_{\max }$ is the time to reach maximum hardness. For a given aluminum alloy, $t_{\text {max }}$ is 40 hours at $160^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and only 4 hours at $200^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Use Equation(rate $=C e^{-Q / R T}$) to calculate the activation energy for this precipitation process. Give your answer as the value of activation_energy.
A 12 -mm-diameter rod of steel is drawn through a $10-\mathrm{mm}$-diameter die. What is the resulting percentage of cold work? Give your answer as the value of percentage_cold_work.

The sintering of ceramic powders is a thermally activated process and shares the "rule of thumb" about temperature with diffusion and recrystallization. Estimate a minimum sintering temperature for (a) pure $\mathrm{Al}_2 \mathrm{O}_3$, (b) pure mullite, and (c) pure spinel. (See Figures) Give your answer as a tuple: (sintering_temperature_Al2O3, sintering_temperature_mullite, sintering_temperature_spinel).

The total sintering rate for $\mathrm{BaTiO}_3$ increases by a factor of 10 between $750^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $794^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Calculate the activation energy for sintering in $\mathrm{BaTiO}_3$. Give your answer as the value of activation_energy.

(a) Estimate the density of 1040 carbon steel as the weighted average of the densities of constituent elements. (b) The density of 1040 steel is what percentage of the density of pure Fe ? Give your answer as a tuple: (density_Mg_per_m3, density_percentage).

A prototype $\mathrm{Al}-\mathrm{Li}$ alloy is being considered for replacement of a 7075 alloy in a commercial aircraft. The compositions are compared in the following table. (a) Assuming that the same volume of material is used, what percentage reduction in density would occur by this material substitution? (b) If a total mass of $70,000 \mathrm{~kg}$ of 7075 alloy is currently used on the aircraft, what net mass reduction would result from the substitution of the $\mathrm{Al}-\mathrm{Li}$ alloy? 
Give your answer as a tuple: (density_reduction_percentage, mass_reduction_kg).

Between 1975 and 1985 , the volume of all iron and steel in a given automobile model decreased from $0.162 \mathrm{~m}^3$ to $0.116 \mathrm{~m}^3$. In the same time frame, the volume of all aluminum alloys increased from $0.012 \mathrm{~m}^3$ to $0.023 \mathrm{~m}^3$. Using the densities of pure Fe and Al , estimate the mass reduction resulting from this trend in materials substitution. Give your answer as the value of mass_reduction.

Estimate the density of a cobalt-chrome alloy used for artificial-hip joints as a weighted average of the densities of constituent elements: $50 \mathrm{wt} \% \mathrm{Co}$, $20 \mathrm{wt} \% \mathrm{Cr}, 15 \mathrm{wt} \% \mathrm{~W}$, and $15 \mathrm{wt} \% \mathrm{Ni}$. Give your answer as the value of density.

As pointed out in the discussion relative to the $\mathrm{Al}_2 \mathrm{O}_3-\mathrm{SiO}_2$ phase diagram (Figure), an alumina-rich mullite is desirable to ensure a more refractory (temperature-resistant) product. Calculate the composition of a refractory made by adding 2.0 kg of $\mathrm{Al}_2 \mathrm{O}_3$ to 100 kg of stoichiometric mullite. Give your answer as a tuple: (wt_percent_Al2O3, wt_percent_SiO2).

The primary reason for introducing ceramic components in automotive engine designs is the possibility of higher operating temperatures and, therefore, improved efficiencies. A by-product of this substitution, however, is mass reduction. For the case of 1.5 kg of cast iron (density $=7.15 \mathrm{Mg} / \mathrm{m}^3$ ) being replaced by an equivalent volume of PSZ (density $=5.50 \mathrm{Mg} / \mathrm{m}^3$ ), calculate the mass reduction. Give your answer as the value of mass_reduction.

A batch formula for a window glass contains 400 kg $\mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{CO}_3, 300 \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{CaCO}_3$, and $1,300 \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{SiO}_2$. Calculate the resulting glass formula. Give your answer as a tuple: (na2o_wt_percent, cao_wt_percent, sio2_wt_percent).

An economical substitute for vitreous silica is a highsilica glass made by leaching the $\mathrm{B}_2 \mathrm{O}_3$-rich phase from a two-phase borosilicate glass. (The resulting porous microstructure is densified by heating.) A typical starting composition is $81 \mathrm{wt} \% \mathrm{SiO}_2, 4 \mathrm{wt} \% \mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{O}, 2 \mathrm{wt} \%$ $\mathrm{Al}_2 \mathrm{O}_3$, and $13 \mathrm{wt} \% \mathrm{~B}_2 \mathrm{O}_3$. A typical final composition is $96 \mathrm{wt} \% \mathrm{SiO}_2, 1 \mathrm{wt} \% \mathrm{Al}_2 \mathrm{O}_3$, and $3 \mathrm{wt} \% \mathrm{~B}_2 \mathrm{O}_3$. How much product (in kilograms) would be produced from 50 kg of starting material, assuming no $\mathrm{SiO}_2$ is lost by leaching? Give your answer as the value of product_mass.

A novel electronic material involves the dispersion of small silicon particles in a glass matrix. If $4.85 \times 10^{16}$ particles of Si are dispersed per $\mathrm{mm}^3$ of glass corresponding to a total content of $5 \mathrm{wt} \%$, calculate the average particle size of the quantum dots. (Assume spherical particles and note that the density of the silicate glass matrix is $2.60 \mathrm{Mg} / \mathrm{m}^3$.)

Assuming the $\mathrm{TiO}_2$ in a $\mathrm{Li}_2 \mathrm{O}-\mathrm{Al}_2 \mathrm{O}_3-\mathrm{SiO}_2$ glassceramic is uniformly distributed with a dispersion of $10^{12}$ particles per cubic millimeter and a total amount of $6 \mathrm{wt} \%$, what is the average particle size of the $\mathrm{TiO}_2$ particles? (Assume spherical particles. The density of the glass-ceramic is $2.85 \mathrm{Mg} / \mathrm{m}^3$, and the density of the $\mathrm{TiO}_2$ is $4.26 \mathrm{Mg} / \mathrm{m}^3$.) Give your answer as the value of average_particle_size.

A bar of annealed $85 \mathrm{Cu}-15 \mathrm{Zn}$ (12-mm diameter) is cold-drawn through a die with a diameter of 10 mm . What are (a) the tensile strength and (b) the ductility of the resulting bar? Give your answer as a tuple: (tensile_strength, ductility).

For simplified processing calculations, glass-bottle shaping temperature can be taken as the softening point (at which $\eta=10^{7.6} \mathrm{P}$ ), and the subsequent annealing temperature can be taken as the annealing point (at which $\eta=10^{13.4} \mathrm{P}$ ). If the glass-bottle processing sequence involves a shaping temperature of $690^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ for a glass with an activation energy for viscous deformation of $470 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$, calculate the appropriate annealing temperature. Give your answer as the value of annealing_temperature.
How many grams of $\mathrm{N}_2$ gas are consumed in forming 100 g of TiN by SHS? Give your answer as the value of grams_of_N2.

What is the average molecular weight of a polypropylene, $\left(\mathrm{C}_3 \mathrm{H}_6\right)_n$, with a degree of polymerization of 500 ? Give your answer as the value of average_molecular_weight.
A blend of polyethylene and PVC (see Figure) contains $20 \mathrm{~mol} \%$ PVC. What is the weight percentage of PVC? Give your answer as the value of weight_percentage.

A simplified mer formula for natural rubber (isoprene) is $\mathrm{C}_5 \mathrm{H}_8$. Calculate the molecular weight for a molecule of isoprene with a degree of polymerization of 500 . Give your answer as the value of molecular_weight.

Calculate the average degree of polymerization for this system.

What would be the maximum fraction of cross-link sites that would be connected in 1 kg of chloroprene, $\mathrm{C}_4 \mathrm{H}_5 \mathrm{Cl}$, with the addition of 200 g of sulfur? Give your answer as the value of maximum_fraction.
Calculate the average molecular length (extended) for PVC with a molecular weight of $25,000 \mathrm{amu}$. Give your answer as the value of average_molecular_length.

Calculate (a) the molecular weight, (b) coiled molecular length, and (c) extended molecular length for a polytetrafluoroethylene polymer (mer formula $\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{~F}_4$ ) with a degree of polymerization of 500. Give your answer as a tuple: (molecular_weight, coiled_molecular_length, extended_molecular_length).

Calculate the degree of polymerization of a polycarbonate polymer (mer formula $\mathrm{C}_{16} \mathrm{H}_{14} \mathrm{O}_3$ ) with a molecular weight of $100,000 \mathrm{amu}$. Give your answer as the value of degree_of_polymerization.
What would be the molecular weight of a $40,000 \mathrm{~mm}^3$ plate made from urea-formaldehyde? (The density of the urea-formaldehyde is $1.50 \mathrm{Mg} / \mathrm{m}^3$.)

Polyisoprene loses its elastic properties with $1 \mathrm{wt} \% \mathrm{O}_2$ addition. If we assume that this loss is due to a cross-linking mechanism similar to that for sulfur, what fraction of the cross-link sites are occupied in this case? Give your answer as the value of fraction_of_cross_link_sites.

An epoxy (density $=1.1 \mathrm{Mg} / \mathrm{m}^3$ ) is reinforced with $30 \mathrm{vol} \mathrm{\%} \mathrm{E-glass} \mathrm{fibers} \mathrm{(density}=2.54 \mathrm{Mg} / \mathrm{m}^3$ ). Calculate (a) the weight percent of E-glass fibers and (b) the density of the reinforced polymer. Give your answer as a tuple: (weight_percent, density).

Bearings and other parts requiring exceptionally low friction and wear can be fabricated from an acetal polymer with an addition of polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) fibers. The densities of acetal and PTFE are $1.42 \mathrm{Mg} / \mathrm{m}^3$ and $2.15 \mathrm{Mg} / \mathrm{m}^3$, respectively. If the density of the polymer with additive is $1.54 \mathrm{Mg} / \mathrm{m}^3$, calculate the weight percent of the PTFE addition. Give your answer as the value of weight_percent_PTFE.

Calculate the density of a fiber-reinforced composite composed of $14 \mathrm{vol} \% \mathrm{Al}_2 \mathrm{O}_3$ whiskers in a matrix of epoxy. The density of $\mathrm{Al}_2 \mathrm{O}_3$ is $3.97 \mathrm{Mg} / \mathrm{m}^3$ and of epoxy is $1.1 \mathrm{Mg} / \mathrm{m}^3$. Give your answer as the value of density.
Calculate the density of a particulate composite composed of $50 \mathrm{vol} \%$ Mo in a copper matrix. Give your answer as the value of density.
Calculate the composite modulus for epoxy reinforced with $70 \mathrm{vol} \%$ boron filaments under isostrain conditions. Give your answer as the value of composite_modulus.

The Feature Box in this chapter introduced spider silk as an impressive natural material with a specific strength greater than structural steel. Given a tensile strength of a particular spider silk as $1.2 \times 10^3 \mathrm{MPa}$ with a density of $1.30 \mathrm{Mg} / \mathrm{m}^3$, calculate its specific strength and compare that result with the value for 1040 steel in Table. The experimental value of the dielectric constant ($\varepsilon_{\mathrm{r}, \text{exp}}$) for $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{SiO}_{3}$ between 1 kHz and 10 MHz is 6.7. How does the calculated value of the dielectric constant compare with the value estimated using Shannon's approach?

The dielectric constant of MgO is 9.83, and its molar volume $(V_{\mathrm{m}})$ is $18.69 \AA^{3}$. The dielectric constant of $\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ is estimated to be 10.126, and its molar volume $(V_{\mathrm{m}})$ is $42.45 \AA^{3}$. What is the dielectric constant of $\mathrm{MgAl}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$? The molar volume $(V_{\mathrm{m}})$ of $\mathrm{MgAl}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ is $66.00 \AA^{3}$.

The refractive index ($n$) of a silicate glass is 1.5. What is the high-frequency dielectric constant ($k_{\infty}$) of this glass? The dielectric constant of this glass at 1 kHz is $k_{\mathrm{S}}=7.6$. Based on the relaxation of the dielectric constant, approximately what fraction of the low-frequency dielectric constant ($k_{\mathrm{S}}$) can be attributed to ionic polarization? Reference the Lorentz-Lorenz equation and the relationship between the high-frequency dielectric constant and the refractive index, $k_{\infty}=n^{2}$.

The static dielectric constant $(\varepsilon_{\mathrm{s}})$ of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ is 78.4 at 298.15 K (Fernandez et al. 1995). It decreases to about $\varepsilon_{\mathrm{r}}(\omega)=$ 20 at a frequency of $f=40 \mathrm{GHz}$. If the high-frequency (optical) dielectric constant $(\varepsilon_{\infty})$ is 5, what is the relaxation time $\tau$ for $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ molecule dipoles?
What is the magnitude of the capacitive reactance $\left(X_{\mathrm{C}}\right)$ of a $200-\mathrm{pF}$ capacitor at a frequency of 10 MHz?

A $10-\mathrm{V}$ variable-frequency AC supply is connected to a $200-\mu \mathrm{F}$ capacitor. What is the value of capacitive reactance for frequencies of (a) 0 Hz, (b) 60 Hz, and (c) 1 kHz? What is the value of the current (I) flowing through the circuit for each frequency? Give your answer as a tuple (capacitive reactance at 0 Hz, capacitive reactance at 60 Hz, capacitive reactance at 1 kHz, current at 0 Hz, current at 60 Hz, current at 1 kHz).

Both sides of an alumina plate are coated with a metal electrode. The surface area of the electrode is $5 \mathrm{~mm}^{2}$ and the relative dielectric constant and dielectric strength of the alumina are 9.1 and 17 volt $/ \mathrm{mm}$, respectively. What is the maximum amount of electric charge that can be stored on the alumina plate's surface?

Relative permittivity of water at $10^{8} \mathrm{~Hz}$ and $10^{11} \mathrm{~Hz}$ is $\sim 90$ and $\sim 6$, respectively. Estimate the relative contribution of dipolar polarization, ionic polarization, and electronic polarization to the permittivity of water. The refractive index of water is 1.33 at room temperature. Give your answer as a tuple (dipolar polarization, ionic polarization, electronic polarization).
Estimate the plasma frequency \(\left(v_{p}\right)\) of lithium metal using the atomic density of lithium metal.

Green light with a wavelength of 530 nm travels in a waveguide. The refractive index of the core is 1.45 and that of the cladding is 1.43. What is the critical angle for total internal reflection at the core-cladding interface?

1. Sunlight is incident through a glass window. If there is no antireflection layer on the glass surface, how much radiation energy is transmitted through the glass for blue ray $(\lambda=450 \mathrm{~nm}, n=1.47)$ and infrared ray $(\lambda=3 \mu \mathrm{~m}, n=1.42)$? Assume that absorption is negligible. Give your answer as a tuple (percentage of transmitted energy for blue ray, percentage of transmitted energy for infrared ray).

If the AC conductivity ($\sigma$) and the real refractive index ($n$) of Cu are $3.1 \times 10^{2}(\Omega \cdot \mathrm{cm})^{-1}$ and 0.38 at $\lambda=1 \mu \mathrm{m}$, respectively, what are the extinction coefficient ($k$) and absorption coefficient ($\alpha$)? Give your answer as a tuple (k, $\alpha$).

Let us say that you want to install solar panels for your house in City A where the yearly solar radiation is 1,600 $\mathrm{kWh} / \mathrm{m}^{2}$. Each panel costs $\$ 550$, and the efficiency and active area of each panel is $16 \%$ and $2.5 \mathrm{~m}^{2}$.

1. In 2013, the average annual electricity consumption for a U.S. residential utility customer was $10,908 \mathrm{kWh}$. How many solar panels do you need to cover the electricity demand of your house?
2. The price of electricity in City A is 15 cents per kWh . What is your payback period (in other words, how many years do you need to use the solar panels to get back your investment)? Assume that costs of installation and other auxiliary parts are the same as the panel price.

Give your answer as a tuple (the number of solar panels, the payback period).

You are trying to connect an external load to a solar cell. This solar cell exhibits the current density $(J)$ and power $(P)$ versus voltage relations in Figure. What is the resistance of the external load that allows you to pull out a maximum power from this solar cell? Active area (the area of the p-n junction semiconductor exposed to illuminating light) is $100 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$.

An indium gallium arsenide, (In,Ga)As, photodiode is irradiated by IR light with the wavelength $1 \mu \mathrm{m}$ and the power density $1.5 \mathrm{mW} / \mathrm{cm}^{2}$. Assume that the incident photon-to-current efficiency (IPCE or external quantum efficiency) of the photodiode is $90\%$ over IR light, and the light receiving area is $2.5 \mathrm{cm}^{2}$. What is the electric current coming out of the photodiode?

Question: A poled PZT type I piezoelectric ceramic disk (length $l_{0}=2 \mathrm{~mm}$ and poled in Direction 3) of a certain composition has $d_{33}=289 \mathrm{pC} / \mathrm{N}$, and its dielectric constant is 1300.

1. What is the value of the piezoelectric voltage constant or the $g_{33}$ coefficient?
2. What is the voltage applied across the thickness of this material if the electric field $(E)$ is $250 \mathrm{kV} / \mathrm{m}$?
3. How much strain $(X)$ will this voltage generate along Direction 3?
4. What will be the increase in length, in micrometers?

Give your answer as a tuple (piezoelectric voltage constant, voltage applied, strain generated, increase in length).

A poled PZT ceramic plate, 50 mm long, 5 mm wide, and 2 mm thick, is used as a micropositioner device. Assume that the dielectric constant $\left(\varepsilon_{\mathrm{r} 33}^{\mathrm{X}}\right)$ is 1200 and $g_{31}=10.5 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~V} \cdot \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{N}$. 1. What will be the value of $d_{31}$? 2. What will be the change in the length of this plate if a potential of 100 V is applied across its thickness? Give your answer as a tuple (d31, change in length).

A commercially available poled PVDF film, electroded using silver metallic ink, is $110 \mu \mathrm{~m}$ thick. A stress of $20,000 \mathrm{~Pa}$ is applied to this film over a 1 - in. ${ }^2$ area.
1. Assuming that the film has a rigid backing and deforms only in the thickness direction, what will be the open-circuit voltage generated?
2. If the film is now subjected to the same level of force as in part (a) but the film has a compliant backing, with a force acting on the cross section of the film such that the film stretches in the length direction, what will be the voltage generated across the thickness of the film? Assume that $g_{33}=350 \times 10^{-3}$ and $g_{31}$ $=220 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~V} \cdot \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{N}$.

Give your answer as a tuple (voltage generated in part 1, voltage generated in part 2)

A poled PZT cylinder with a diameter of 2 mm and a thickness of 5 mm is to be used as a spark igniter. What voltage is generated by applying a compressive force of 100 N to the circular cross section of this disk? Assume that the $d_{33}$ for this PZT is $289 \mathrm{pC} / \mathrm{N}$ and the dielectric constant in a free (unclamped) state $\left(\varepsilon_{\mathrm{r} 33}^{X}\right)$ is 1300.

From the magnetic moments of the ferrous ($\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}$) and ferric ($\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$) ions, calculate the net magnetic moment per formula unit in ferrimagnetic iron oxide ($\mathrm{Fe}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$).

The applied magnetic field $(H)$ for a toroidal solenoid is given by $N \times I$, where $N$ is the number of turns per meter and $I$ is the current in amperes. (a) What is the flux density (B) created for a current of 0.5 A if the toroidal solenoid has an air core? (b) What is the flux density (B) if the core is filled with iron? Assume that the relative magnetic permeability of iron is 900 for the field generated using 0.5 A. The average circumference of the solenoid is 75 cm, and there are 1000 turns. Give your answer as a tuple (flux density with air core, flux density with iron core).

(a) Calculate the saturation magnetization ($\mu_{0} M_{\mathrm{s}}$) for $\mathrm{Fe}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ if the lattice constant ($a_{0}$) of the larger unit cell, which consists of eight smaller unit cells, is $8.37 \AA$.

The maximum $d_{33}$ magnetostriction coefficient for Terfenol-D is $57 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{A}$ (du Trémolet de Lacheisserie et al. 2002). A bar made from this material is 10 cm long and is exposed to a magnetic field $(H)$ of $5 \mathrm{~A} / \mathrm{m}$. What will be the elongation produced in this bar?

The coercivity $\left(H_{\mathrm{C}}\right)$ of a sample of a material known as grain-oriented steel is $300 \mathrm{~A} / \mathrm{m}$. The saturation magnetization ( $\mu_0 M_{\mathrm{S}}$ ) of this material is 2.1 T . A hysteresis loop for this material was measured to determine the properties.

1. If the coercivity of this material is also expressed in Tesla, how does the $y$-axis value of saturation magnetization compare with the $x$-axis value of coercivity? 
2. What is the value of the coercivity of this material in Oersteds? 
3. What is the value of the saturation magnetization of this grain-oriented steel in Gauss? 
4. Repeat this calculation for a sample of a neodymium-iron-boron $(\mathrm{Nd}_{2} \mathrm{Fe}_{14} \mathrm{~B})$ permanent magnet, whose coercive field is $700,000 \mathrm{~A} / \mathrm{m}$, if the saturation magnetization is 1.2 T. 

Give your answer as a tuple (coercivity in Tesla, coercivity in Oersteds, saturation magnetization in Gauss, coercivity in Tesla for Nd2Fe14B).

Silicon has three naturally occurring isotopes: $92.23 \%$ of ${ }^{28} \mathrm{Si}$, with an atomic weight of 27.9769 amu, $4.68 \%$ of ${ }^{29} \mathrm{Si}$, with an atomic weight of 28.9765 amu, and $3.09 \%$ of ${ }^{30} \mathrm{Si}$, with an atomic weight of 29.9738 amu. On the basis of these data, calculate the average atomic weight of Si.

Zinc has five naturally occurring isotopes: $48.63 \%$ of ${ }^{64} \mathrm{Zn}$ with an atomic weight of 63.929 amu; $27.90 \%$ of ${ }^{66} \mathrm{Zn}$ with an atomic weight of 65.926 amu; $4.10 \%$ of ${ }^{67} \mathrm{Zn}$ with an atomic weight of 66.927 amu; $18.75 \%$ of ${ }^{68} \mathrm{Zn}$ with an atomic weight of 67.925 amu; and $0.62 \%$ of ${ }^{70} \mathrm{Zn}$ with an atomic weight of 69.925 amu. Calculate the average atomic weight of Zn .

Indium has two naturally occurring isotopes: ${ }^{113}$ In with an atomic weight of 112.904 amu, and ${ }^{115}$ In with an atomic weight of 114.904 amu. If the average atomic weight for In is 114.818 amu, calculate the fraction of occurrences of these two isotopes.

Give your answer as a tuple (${ }^{113}$, ${ }^{115}$).

(a) How many grams are there in one amu of a material?
(b) Mole, in the context of this book, is taken in units of gram-mole. On this basis, how many atoms are there in a pound-mole of a substance?

Give your answer as a tuple (answer to (a), answer to (b)).

Calculate the force of attraction between a $\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}$ and an $O^{2-}$ ion whose centers are separated by a distance of 1.25 nm .

The atomic radii of $\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$ and $F^{-}$ions are 0.072 and 0.133 nm , respectively. Use k = 8.99×10^9 N·m²/C² (or equivalently k e² = 2.31×10^–28 N·m²).
(a) Calculate the force of attraction between these two ions at their equilibrium interionic separation (i.e., when the ions just touch one another).
(b) What is the force of repulsion at this same separation distance?

Give your answer as a tuple (force of attraction, force of repulsion)
The force of attraction between a divalent cation and a divalent anion is $1.67 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{~N}$. If the ionic radius of the cation is 0.080 nm , what is the anion radius?

The net potential energy between two adjacent ions, $E{N}$, may be represented by the sum of Equations ($E_A=-\frac{A}{r}$) and ($E_R=\frac{B}{r^n}$); that is,

$$
E{N}=-\frac{A}{r}+\frac{B}{r^{n}}
$$

Calculate the bonding energy $E{0}$ in terms of the parameters $A, B$, and $n$ using the following procedure:

1. Differentiate $E{N}$ with respect to $r$, and then set the resulting expression equal to zero, because the curve of $E{N}$ versus $r$ is a minimum at $E{0}$.
2. Solve for $r$ in terms of $A, B$, and $n$, which yields $r{0}$, the equilibrium interionic spacing.
3. Determine the expression for $E{0}$ by substitution of $r{0}$ into Equation ($E_N=-\frac{A}{r}+\frac{B}{r^n}$).

The net potential energy $E{N}$ between two adjacent ions is sometimes represented by the expression

$$
E{N}=-\frac{C}{r}+D \exp \left(-\frac{r}{\rho}\right)
$$

in which $r$ is the interionic separation and $C, D$, and $\rho$ are constants whose values depend on the specific material.
(a) Derive an expression for the bonding energy $E{0}$ in terms of the equilibrium interionic separation $r{0}$ and the constants $D$ and $\rho$ using the following procedure:
(i) Differentiate $E{N}$ with respect to $r$, and set the resulting expression equal to zero.
(ii) Solve for $C$ in terms of $D, \rho$, and $r{0}$.
(iii) Determine the expression for $E{0}$ by substitution for $C$ in Equation ($E_N=-\frac{C}{r}+D \exp \left(-\frac{r}{\rho}\right)$).
(b) Derive another expression for $E{0}$ in terms of $r{0}, C$, and $\rho$ using a procedure analogous to the one outlined in part (a).

Give your answer as a tuple (expression for the bonding energy in (a), expression for the bonding energy in (b)).

Compute the \%IC of the interatomic bond for each of the following compounds: $\mathrm{MgO}, \mathrm{GaP}, \mathrm{CsF}, \mathrm{CdS}$, and FeO.

Give your answer as a tuple (MgO, GaP, CsF, CdS, FeO).

If the atomic radius of lead is 0.175 nm, calculate the volume of its unit cell in cubic meters.

Strontium (Sr) has an FCC crystal structure, an atomic radius of 0.215 nm and an atomic weight of 87.62 $\mathrm{g} / \mathrm{mol}$. Calculate the theoretical density for Sr .

Calculate the radius of a tantalum (Ta) atom, given that Ta has a BCC crystal structure, a density of 16.6 $\mathrm{g} / \mathrm{cm}^{3}$, and an atomic weight of $180.9 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}$.

(a) What is the volume of its unit cell in cubic meters?
(b) If the c/a ratio is 1.58, compute the values of $c$.

Magnesium (Mg) has an HCP crystal structure and a density of $1.74 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{cm}^{3}$. For HCP, $n=6$ \frac{atoms}{unit} cell, and the atomic weight for $\mathrm{Mg}, A{\mathrm{Mg}}=24.31 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}$.

(b) If the c/a ratio is 1.624 , compute the values of $c$.

Niobium ( Nb ) has an atomic radius of 0.1430 nm and a density of $8.57 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{cm}^{3}$. Determine whether it has an FCC or a BCC crystal structure.

The unit cell for uranium (U) has orthorhombic symmetry, with $a, b$, and c lattice parameters of 0.286 , 0.587 , and 0.495 nm , respectively. If its density, atomic weight, and atomic radius are $19.05 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{cm}^{3}, 238.03 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}$, and 0.1385 nm , respectively, compute the atomic packing factor.

Indium (In) has a tetragonal unit cell for which the a and c lattice parameters are 0.459 and 0.495 nm , respectively.

(b) The atomic weight of indium is $114.82 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}$; compute its theoretical density.

Consider a diffusion couple composed of two semi-infinite solids of the same metal and that each side of the diffusion couple has a different concentration of the same elemental impurity; furthermore, assume each impurity level is constant throughout its side of the diffusion couple. For this situation, the solution to Fick's second law (assuming that the diffusion coefficient for the impurity is independent of concentration) is as follows:

$$
C{x}=C{2}+\left(\frac{C{1}-C{2}}{2}\right)\left[1-\operatorname{erf}\left(\frac{x}{2 \sqrt{D t}}\right)\right]
$$

The schematic diffusion profile in  figure 6.13  shows these concentration parameters as well as concentration profiles at times $t=0$ and $t>0$. Please note that at $t=0$, the $x=0$ position is taken as the initial diffusion couple interface, whereas $C{1}$ is the impurity concentration for $x<0$, and $C{2}$ is the impurity content for $x>0$.

A copper-nickel diffusion couple similar to that shown in  figure 6.1a  is fashioned. After a 500-h heat treatment at $1000^{\circ} \mathrm{C}(1273 \mathrm{~K})$ the concentration of Ni is $3.0 \mathrm{wt} \%$ at the $1.0-\mathrm{mm}$ position within the copper. At what temperature should the diffusion couple be heated to produce this same concentration (i.e., $3.0 \mathrm{wt} \% \mathrm{Ni}$ ) at a $2.0-\mathrm{mm}$ position after 500 h ? The preexponential and activation energy for the diffusion of Ni in Cu are $1.9 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}$ and $230,000 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol}$, respectively.

A diffusion couple similar to that shown in  figure 6.1a  is prepared using two hypothetical metals $A$ and $B$. After a 20-h heat treatment at $800^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ (and subsequently cooling to room temperature) the concentration of $B$ in $A$ is $2.5 \mathrm{wt} \%$ at the $5.0-\mathrm{mm}$ position within metal $A$. If another heat treatment is conducted on an identical diffusion couple, but at $1000^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ for 20 h , at what position will the composition be $2.5 \mathrm{wt} \% \mathrm{~B}$ ? Assume that the preexponential and activation energy for the diffusion coefficient are $1.5 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}$ and $125,000 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol}$, respectively.

Consider the diffusion of some hypothetical metal $Y$ into another hypothetical metal $Z$ at $950^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$; after 10 h the concentration at the 0.5 mm position (in metal $Z$ ) is $2.0 \mathrm{wt} \% Y$. At what position will the concentration also be $2.0 \mathrm{wt} \% Y$ after a 17.5 h heat treatment again at $950^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ? Assume preexponential and activation energy values of 4.3 $\times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}$ and $180,000 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol}$, respectively, for this diffusion system.

A diffusion couple is prepared using two hypothetical metals $R$ and $S$. After a 2.5-h heat treatment at $750^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ the concentration of $R$ is $4 \mathrm{at} \%$ at the $4-\mathrm{mm}$ position within $S$. Another heat treatment is conducted on an identical diffusion couple at $900^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and the time required to produce this same diffusion result (viz., $4 \mathrm{at} \% R$ at the $4-\mathrm{mm}$ position within $S$ ) is 0.4 h . If it is known that the diffusion coefficient at $750^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is $2.6 \times 10^{-17} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}$ determine the activation energy for the diffusion of $R$ in $S$.

A gas mixture is found to contain two diatomic $A$ and $B$ species ( $A_{2}$ and $B_{2}$ ), the partial pressures of both of which are 0.1013 MPa ( 1 atm ). This mixture is to be enriched in the partial pressure of the $A$ species by passing both gases through a thin sheet of some metal at an elevated temperature. The resulting enriched mixture is to have a partial pressure of 0.051 MPa ( 0.5 atm ) for gas $A$ and 0.0203 MPa ( 0.2 atm ) for gas $B$. The concentrations of $A$ and $B\left(C_{A}\right.$ and $C_{B}$, in $\left.mol / m^{3}\right)$ are functions of gas partial pressures $\left(p_{A_{2}}\right.$ and $p_{B_{2}}$, in MPa) and absolute temperature according to the following expressions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& C_{\mathrm{A}}=1.5 \times 10^{3} \sqrt{p_{\mathrm{A}_{2}}} \exp \left(-\frac{20,000 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol}}{R T}\right) \\
& C_{\mathrm{B}}=2.0 \times 10^{3} \sqrt{p_{\mathrm{B}_{2}}} \exp \left(-\frac{27,000 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol}}{R T}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Furthermore, the diffusion coefficients for the diffusion of these gases in the metal are functions of the absolute temperature as follows:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& D_{\mathrm{A}}\left(\mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}\right)=5.0 \times 10^{-7} \exp \left(-\frac{13,000 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol}}{R T}\right) \\
& D_{\mathrm{B}}\left(\mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}\right)=3.0 \times 10^{-6} \exp \left(-\frac{21,000 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol}}{R T}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Consider a cylindrical specimen of a steel alloy ( figure 7.34 ) 8.5 mm (0.33 in.) in diameter and 80 mm (3.15 in.) long that is pulled in tension. Determine its elongation when a load of $65,250 \mathrm{~N}(14,500 \mathrm{lb})$ is applied.

The net bonding energy $E{N}$ between two isolated positive and negative ions is a function of interionic distance $r$ as follows:

$$
E{N}=-\frac{A}{r}+\frac{B}{r^{n}}
$$

where $A, B$, and $n$ are constants for the particular ion pair. equation (E_N=-\frac{A}{r}+\frac{B}{r^n}) is also valid for the bonding energy between adjacent ions in solid materials. The modulus of elasticity $E$ is proportional to the slope of the interionic force-separation curve at the equilibrium interionic separation; that is,

$$
E \propto\left(\frac{d F}{d r}\right){r{0}}
$$

Derive an expression for the dependence of the modulus of elasticity on these $A, B$, and $n$ parameters (for the twoion system), using the following procedure:

1. Establish a relationship for the force $F$ as a function of $r$, realizing that

$$
F=\frac{d E{N}}{d r}
$$

2. Now take the derivative $d F / d r$.
3. Develop an expression for $r{0}$, the equilibrium separation. Because $r{0}$ corresponds to the value of $r$ at the minimum of the $E{N}$-versus-r curve ( figure 2.10b ), take the derivative $d E{N} / d r$, set it equal to zero, and solve for $r$, which corresponds to $r{0}$.
4. Finally, substitute this expression for $r{0}$ into the relationship obtained by taking $d F / d r$.

A cylindrical specimen of a brass alloy having a length of 100 mm (4 in.) must elongate only 5 mm ( 0.2 in.) when a tensile load of $100,000 \mathrm{~N}(22,500 \mathrm{lb})$ is applied. Under these circumstances, what must be the radius of the specimen? Consider this brass alloy to have the stress-strain behavior shown in  figure 7.12 .

A load of $140,000 \mathrm{~N}(31,500 \mathrm{lb})$ is applied to a cylindrical specimen of a steel alloy (displaying the stressstrain behavior shown in  figure 7.34 ) that has a cross-sectional diameter of 10 mm ( 0.40 in.). If the original specimen length is 500 mm (20 in.), how much will it increase in length when this load is applied?

A bar of a steel alloy that exhibits the stress-strain behavior shown in  figure 7.34  is subjected to a tensile load; the specimen is 375 mm (14.8 in.) long and has a square cross section 5.5 mm ( 0.22 in.) on a side.
Compute the magnitude of the load necessary to produce an elongation of 2.25 mm ( 0.088 in.).

A cylindrical metal specimen having an original diameter of 12.8 mm ( 0.505 in.) and gauge length of 50.80 mm (2.000 in.) is pulled in tension until fracture occurs. The diameter at the point of fracture is 8.13 mm ( 0.320 in.), and the fractured gauge length is 74.17 mm (2.920 in.). Calculate the ductility in terms of percent reduction in area and percent elongation. Give your answer as a tuple: (percent reduction, percent elongation).
