      • Steel alloy:  σy = 830 MPa,  E = 207 GPa  
      • Brass alloy:  σy = 380 MPa,  E = 97 GPa

A steel alloy to be used for a spring application must have a modulus of resilience of at least 2.07 MPa (300 psi).  If the Young’s modulus of the steel is 207 GPa, what minimum yield strength must it have?

Using data(The modulus of elasticity for 17-4PH stainless steel is 196 GPa ( $196 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{MPa}$ ). Furthermore, the yield strength for this annealed stainless steel is 760 MPa.), estimate the modulus of restlience (in MPa) of annealed 17-4PH stainless steel.

A tensile test is performed on a metal specimen, and it is found that a true plastic strain of 0.16 is produced when a true stress of 500 MPa (72,500 psi) is applied; for the same metal, the value of $K$ is 825 MPa (120,000 psi). Calculate the true strain that results from the application of a true stress of 600 MPa (87,000 psi).

For some metal alloy, a true stress of 345 MPa (50,000 psi) produces a plastic true strain of 0.02 . How much does a specimen of this material elongate when a true stress of 415 MPa (60,000 psi) is applied if the original length is 500 mm (20 in.)? Assume a value of 0.22 for the strain-hardening exponent, $n$.

The following true stresses produce the corresponding true plastic strains for a brass alloy:

| True Stress (psi) | True Strain |
| :--: | :--: |
| 60,000 | 0.15 |
| 70,000 | 0.25 |

What true stress is necessary to produce a true plastic strain of 0.21 ?

For a brass alloy, the following engineering stresses produce the corresponding plastic engineering strains prior to necking:

| Engineering Stress (MPa) | Engineering Strain |
| :--: | :--: |
| 315 | 0.105 |
| 340 | 0.220 |

On the basis of this information, compute the engineering stress necessary to produce an engineering strain of 0.28 .

Find the toughness (or energy to cause fracture) for a metal that experiences both elastic and plastic deformation. Assume equation (\sigma=E \varepsilon) for elastic deformation, that the modulus of elasticity is 103 GPa ( $15 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{psi}$ ), and that elastic deformation terminates at a strain of 0.007 . For plastic deformation, assume that the relationship between stress and strain is described by equation (\sigma_T=K \varepsilon_T^n), in which the values for $K$ and $n$ are 1520 MPa (221,000 psi) and 0.15 , respectively. Furthermore, plastic deformation occurs between strain values of 0.007 and 0.60 , at which point fracture occurs.

A cylindrical specimen of a brass alloy 10.0 mm (0.39 in.) in diameter and 120.0 mm (4.72 in.) long is pulled in tension with a force of $11,750 \mathrm{~N}(2640 \mathrm{lb})$; the force is subsequently released.
(a) Compute the final length of the specimen at this time. The tensile stress-strain behavior for this alloy is shown in  figure 7.12 .
(b) Compute the final specimen length when the load is increased to 23,500 N (5280 lb) and then released.

A circular specimen of MgO is loaded using a three-point bending mode. Compute the minimum possible radius of the specimen without fracture, given that the applied load is $5560 \mathrm{~N}\left(1250 \mathrm{lb}{\mathrm{f}}\right)$, the flexural strength is 105 MPa (15,000 psi), and the separation between load points is 45 mm (1.75 in.).

A three-point bending test was performed on an aluminum oxide specimen having a circular cross section of radius 5.0 mm ( 0.20 in.); the specimen fractured at a load of $3000 \mathrm{~N}\left(675 \mathrm{lb}\right.$ ) when the distance between the support points was 40 mm (1.6 in.). Another test is to be performed on a specimen of this same material, but one that has a square cross section of 15 mm ( 0.6 in.) length on each edge. At what load would you expect this specimen to fracture if the support point separation is maintained at 40 mm (1.6 in.)?

A three-point transverse bending test is conducted on a cylindrical specimen of aluminum oxide having a reported flexural strength of 300 MPa (43,500 psi). If the specimen radius is 5.0 mm ( 0.20 in .) and the support point separation distance is 15.0 mm ( 0.61 in .), would you expect the specimen to fracture when a load of 7500 N (1690 lb) is applied? Justify your answer.

Use the empirical relation  
 E = E₀·(1 – 1.9 P + 0.9 P²)  
to relate the Young’s modulus E of a ceramic with porosity P (vol fraction) to the modulus E₀ of the fully dense (nonporous) material. For spinel (MgAl₂O₄) with 5 vol % porosity (P=0.05) the measured modulus is 240 GPa.  
(a) Compute the nonporous modulus E₀.  
(b) Compute the modulus E when P=0.15 (15 vol %). 

Give your answer as a tuple (answer to (a), answer to (b)).

The modulus of elasticity for titanium carbide (TiC) having 5 vol\% porosity is 310 GPa ( $45 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{psi}$ ).
(a) Compute the modulus of elasticity for the nonporous material.
(b) At what volume percent porosity will the modulus of elasticity be $240 \mathrm{GPa}\left(35 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{psi}\right)$ ?

Give your answer a s tuple: (answer to (a), answer to (b)).

The flexural strength and associated volume fraction porosity for two specimens of the same ceramic material are as follows:

| $\sigma{f s}(\mathrm{MPa})$ | $\boldsymbol{P}$ |
| :-- | :-- |
| 70 | 0.10 |
| 60 | 0.15 |

(a) Compute the flexural strength for a completely nonporous specimen of this material.
(b) Compute the flexural strength for a 0.20 volume fraction porosity.

Give your answer as a tuple (answer to (a), answer to (b)).
From the stress-strain data for poly(methyl methacrylate) shown in  figure 7.24 , determine the modulus of elasticity at room temperature $\left[20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right]$.

For some viscoelastic polymers that are subjected to stress relaxation tests, the stress decays with time according to

$$
\sigma(t)=\sigma(0) \exp \left(-\frac{t}{\tau}\right)
$$

where $\sigma(t)$ and $\sigma(0)$ represent the time-dependent and initial (i.e., time $=0$ ) stresses, respectively, and $t$ and $\tau$ denote elapsed time and the relaxation time, respectively; $\tau$ is a time-independent constant characteristic of the material. A specimen of a viscoelastic polymer whose stress relaxation obeys equation (\sigma(t)=\sigma(0) \exp \left(-\frac{t}{\tau}\right)) was suddenly pulled in tension to a measured strain of 0.5 ; the stress necessary to maintain this constant strain was measured as a function of time. Determine $E_{r}(10)$ for this material if the initial stress level was 3.5 MPa ( 500 psi ), which dropped to 0.5 MPa ( 70 psi) after 30 s.

(a) A 10-mm-diameter Brinell hardness indenter produced an indentation 2.50 mm in diameter in a steel alloy when a load of 1000 kg was used. Compute the HB of this material.
(b) What will be the diameter of an indentation to yield a hardness of 300 HB when a $500-\mathrm{kg}$ load is used?

Give your answer as a tuple (HB of this material in (a), the diameter in (b).

(a) Calculate the Knoop hardness when a 500-g load yields an indentation diagonal length of $100 \mu \mathrm{~m}$.
(b) The measured HK of some material is 200. Compute the applied load if the indentation diagonal length is 0.25 mm .

(a) What is the indentation diagonal length when a load of 0.60 kg produces a Vickers HV of 400?
(b) Calculate the Vickers hardness when a 700-g load yields an indentation diagonal length of 0.050 mm .

Give your answer as a tuple (the indentation diagonal length in (a), the Vickers hardness in (b)).

Sometimes $\cos \phi \cos \lambda$ in equation (\tau_R=\sigma \cos \phi \cos \lambda) is termed the Schmid factor. Determine the magnitude of the Schmid factor for an FCC single crystal oriented with its [120] direction parallel to the loading axis.

Consider a metal single crystal oriented such that the normal to the slip plane and the slip direction are at angles of $60^{\circ}$ and $35^{\circ}$, respectively, with the tensile axis. If the critical resolved shear stress is 6.2 MPa ( 900 psi ), will an applied stress of 12 MPa ( 1750 psi ) cause the single crystal to yield? If not, what stress will be necessary?

A single crystal of zinc is oriented for a tensile test such that its slip plane normal makes an angle of $65^{\circ}$ with the tensile axis. Three possible slip directions make angles of $30^{\circ}, 48^{\circ}$, and $78^{\circ}$ with the same tensile axis. If plastic deformation begins at a tensile stress of 2.5 MPa ( 355 psi ), determine the critical resolved shear stress for zinc.

Consider a single crystal of nickel oriented such that a tensile stress is applied along a [001] direction. If slip occurs on a (111) plane and in a [ $\overline{1} 01]$ direction and is initiated at an applied tensile stress of 13.9 MPa (2020 psi), compute the critical resolved shear stress.

A single crystal of a metal that has the FCC crystal structure is oriented such that a tensile stress is applied parallel to the [100] direction. If the critical resolved shear stress for this material is 0.5 MPa , calculate the magnitude(s) of applied stress(es) necessary to cause slip to occur on the (111) plane in each of the [1 $\overline{1} 0$ ]. [10 $\overline{1}]$, and $[0 \overline{1} 1]$ directions.

A single crystal of a metal that has the BCC crystal structure is oriented such that a tensile stress is applied in the [100] direction. If the magnitude of this stress is 4.0 MPa , compute the resolved shear stress in the [1 $\overline{1} 1]$ direction on each of the (110), (011), and (101) planes.

Give your answer as a tuple (resolved shear stress on (110), on (011), on (101))

Consider a single crystal of some hypothetical metal that has the BCC crystal structure and is oriented such that a tensile stress is applied along a [121] direction. $$

if there is no change in specimen volume during the deformation process (i.e., $A{0} I{0}=A{d} I{d}$ ).
(b) Using the result of part (a), compute the percent cold work experienced by naval brass (for which the stress-strain behavior is shown in  figure 7.12 ) when a stress of 415 MPa (60,000 psi) is applied.

Two previously undeformed cylindrical specimens of an alloy are to be strain hardened by reducing their cross-sectional areas (while maintaining their circular cross sections). For one specimen, the initial and deformed radii are 15 and 12 mm , respectively. The second specimen, with an initial radius of 11 mm , must have the same deformed hardness as the first specimen; compute the second specimen's radius after deformation.
A cylindrical specimen of cold-worked copper has a ductility (\%EL) of $15 \%$. If its cold-worked radius is 6.4 $\mathrm{mm}(0.25 \mathrm{in}$.) , what was its radius before deformation?

Experimentally, it has been observed for single crystals of a number of metals that the critical resolved shear stress $\tau{\text {crss }}$ is a function of the dislocation density $\rho{D}$ as

$$
\tau_{\text {crss }}=\tau{0}+A \sqrt{\rho{\mathrm{D}}}
$$

where $\tau{0}$ and $A$ are constants. For copper, the critical resolved shear stress is 0.69 MPa (100 psi) at a dislocation density of $10^{4} \mathrm{~mm}^{-2}$. If it is known that the value of $\tau{0}$ for copper is 0.069 MPa (10 psi), compute $\tau_{\text {crss }}$ at a dislocation density of $10^{6} \mathrm{~mm}^{-2}$.

(a) From  figure 8.25 , compute the length of time required for the average grain diameter to increase from 0.03 to 0.3 mm at $600^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ for this brass material.
(b) Repeat the calculation, this time using $700^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$.

Give your answer as a tuple (length of time in (a), length of time in (b)).

Consider a hypothetical material that has a grain diameter of $2.1 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~mm}$. After a heat treatment at $600^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ for 3 h , the grain diameter has increased to $7.2 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~mm}$. Compute the grain diameter when a specimen of this same original material (i.e., $d{0}=2.1 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~mm}$ ) is heated for 1.7 h at $600^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Assume the $n$ grain diameter exponent has a value of 2 .

A hypothetical metal alloy has a grain diameter of $1.7 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~mm}$. After a heat treatment at $450^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ for 250 min the grain diameter has increased to $4.5 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~mm}$. Compute the time required for a specimen of this same material (i.e., $d{0}=1.7 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~mm}$ ) to achieve a grain diameter of $8.7 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~mm}$ while being heated at $450^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Assume the $n$ grain diameter exponent has a value of 2.1.

Assume parabolic grain growth so that d² – d₀² = K t. The average grain diameter for a brass material was measured as a function of time at $650^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, which is shown in the following table at two different times:

| Time (min) | Grain Diameter (mm) |
| :--: | :--: |
| 40 | $5.6 \times 10^{-2}$ |
| 100 | $8.0 \times 10^{-2}$ |

(a) What was the original grain diameter?
(b) What grain diameter would you predict after 200 min at $650^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$?

Give your answer as a tuple (the original grain diameter, grain diameter after 200 min).

If the friction stress σ₀ for this alloy is 25 MPa, estimate the new yield strength using the Hall–Petch equation.

Fifteen kilograms of polychloroprene is vulcanized with 5.2 kg of sulfur. What fraction of the possible crosslink sites is bonded to sulfur crosslinks, assuming that, on the average, 5.5 sulfur atoms participate in each crosslink?

For the vulcanization of polyisoprene, compute the weight percent of sulfur that must be added to ensure that $10 \%$ of possible sites will be crosslinked; assume that, on the average, 3.5 sulfur atoms are associated with each crosslink.

A specimen of a 4340 steel alloy with a plane strain fracture toughness of $54.8 \mathrm{MPa} \sqrt{\mathrm{~m}}(50 \mathrm{ksi} \sqrt{\mathrm{in}}$. ) is exposed to a stress of 1030 MPa (150,000 psi). Will this specimen experience fracture if the largest surface crack is $0.5 \mathrm{~mm}(0.02 \mathrm{in}$.) long? Why or why not? Assume that the parameter Y has a value of 1.0.

An aircraft component is fabricated from an aluminum alloy that has a plane strain fracture toughness of $40 \mathrm{MPa} \sqrt{\mathrm{m}}$ ( $36.4 \mathrm{ksi} \sqrt{\mathrm{in}}$.). It has been determined that fracture results at a stress of 300 MPa (43,500 psi) when the maximum (or critical) internal crack length is 4.0 mm ( 0.16 in .). For this same component and alloy, will fracture occur at a stress level of 260 MPa (38,000 psi) when the maximum internal crack length is 6.0 mm (0.24 in.)? Why or why not?

Suppose that a wing component on an aircraft is fabricated from an aluminum alloy that has a plane-strain fracture toughness of $26.0 \mathrm{MPa} \sqrt{\mathrm{m}}(23.7 \mathrm{ksi} \sqrt{\text { in. }}$ ). It has been determined that fracture results at a stress of 112 MPa (16,240 psi) when the maximum internal crack length is 8.6 mm ( 0.34 in .). For this same component and alloy, compute the stress level at which fracture will occur for a critical internal crack length of 6.0 mm ( 0.24 in.).

It has been determined that this component fails at a stress of 250 MPa when the maximum length of a surface crack is 1.6 mm . A hypothetical metal is known to have an electrical resistivity of $3.3 \times 10^{-8}(\Omega-\mathrm{m})$. A current of 25 A is passed through a specimen of this metal 15 mm thick. When a magnetic field of 0.95 tesla is simultaneously imposed in a direction perpendicular to that of the current, a Hall voltage of $-2.4 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~V}$ is measured. Compute the following:
(a) the electron mobility for this metal
(b) the number of free electrons per cubic meter.
Give your answer a tuple: (answer to (a), answer to (b)).

We noted in figure 5.4  that in FeO (wüstite), the iron ions can exist in both $\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}$ and $\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$ states. The number of each of these ion types depends on temperature and the ambient oxygen pressure. Furthermore, we also noted that in order to retain electroneutrality, one $\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}$ vacancy is created for every two $\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$ ions that are formed; consequently, in order to reflect the existence of these vacancies the formula for wüstite is often represented as $\mathrm{Fe}{(1-x)} \mathrm{O}$, where $x$ is some small fraction less than unity.

In this nonstoichiometric $\mathrm{Fe}{(1-x)} \mathrm{O}$ material, conduction is electronic, and, in fact, it behaves as a p-type semiconductor-that is, the $\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$ ions act as electron acceptors, and it is relatively easy to excite an electron from the valence band into an $\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$ acceptor state, with the formation of a hole. Determine the electrical conductivity of a specimen of wüstite that has a hole mobility of $1.0 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{V}$-s and for which the value of $x$ is 0.040 . Assume that the acceptor states are saturated (i.e., one hole exists for every $\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$ ion). Wüstite has the sodium chloride crystal structure with a unit cell edge length of 0.437 nm .

At temperatures between $540^{\circ} \mathrm{C}(813 \mathrm{~K})$ and $727^{\circ} \mathrm{C}(1000 \mathrm{~K})$, the activation energy and preexponential for the diffusion coefficient of $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$in NaCl are 173,000 J/mol and $4.0 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}$, respectively. Compute the mobility for an $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ion at $600^{\circ} \mathrm{C}(873 \mathrm{~K})$.

A charge of $2.0 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{C}$ is to be stored on each plate of a parallel-plate capacitor having an area of 650 $\mathrm{mm}^{2}\left(1.0 \mathrm{in} .^{2}\right.$ ) and a plate separation of 4.0 mm ( 0.16 in.).

Give your answer as a tuple (the rate of corrosion in mpy, the rate of corrosion in mm/yr).

(a) Demonstrate that the CPR is related to the corrosion current density $i\left(A / \mathrm{cm}^{2}\right)$ through the expression

$$
\mathrm{CPR}=\frac{K A i}{n \rho}
$$

where $K$ is a constant, $A$ is the atomic weight of the metal experiencing corrosion, $n$ is the number of electrons associated with the ionization of each metal atom, and $\rho$ is the density of the metal.
(b) Calculate the value of the constant $K$ for the CPR in mpy and $i$ in $\mu A / \mathrm{cm}^{2}\left(10^{-6} \mathrm{~A} / \mathrm{cm}^{2}\right)$.

$$
\begin{aligned}

i{0}=10^{-8} \mathrm{~A} / \mathrm{cm}^{2} & i{0}=6 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~A} / \mathrm{cm}^{2} \\
\beta=+0.12 & \beta=-0.10
\end{aligned}
$$

It is possible to express the magnetic susceptibility $\chi{m}$ in several different units. For the discussion of this chapter, $\chi{m}$ was used to designate the volume susceptibility in SI units-that is, the quantity that gives the magnetization per unit volume $\left(m^{3}\right)$ of material when multiplied by $H$. The mass susceptibility $\chi{m}(\mathrm{~kg})$ yields the magnetic moment (or magnetization) per kilogram of material when multiplied by $H$; similarly, the atomic susceptibility $\chi{m}(a)$ gives the magnetization per kilogram-mole. The last two quantities are related to $\chi{m}$ through the following relationships:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \chi{m}=\chi{m}(k g) \times \text { mass density }\left(\text { in } \mathrm{kg} / \mathrm{m}^{3}\right) \\
& \chi{m}(a)=\chi{m}(k g) \times \text { atomic weight (in } \mathrm{kg})
\end{aligned}
$$

When using the cgs-emu system, comparable parameters exist, which may be designated by $\chi{m}^{\prime}, \chi{m}^{\prime}(g)$, and $\chi{m}^{\prime}(a)$; the $\chi{m}$ and $\chi{m}^{\prime}$ are related in accordance with  table 18.1 . From  table 18.2 , $\chi{m}$ for copper is $-0.96 \times 10^{-5}$; convert this value into the other five susceptibilities. Give your answer as a tuple: ($\chi_m(\mathrm{~kg})$, $\chi_m(a), \chi_m^{\prime}, \chi_m^{\prime}(\mathrm{g})$, $\chi_m^{\prime}(\mathrm{a})$)

\hline Magnetic field strength & H & Amp-turn/m & $\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{m} \cdot \mathrm{s}$ & Oersted & 1 amp-turn/m = $4 \pi \times 10^{-3}$ oersted \\

a Assuming that the ends of the compression element are not constrained, the Euler formula can be used to calculate the minimum diameter that will allow safe use of the compression member without buckling. )

Aluminum AA7075-T651 and titanium $\mathrm{Ti}-6 \mathrm{Al}-4 \mathrm{~V}$ are being considered for making a 1 m long tie-rod that will carry a tensile load of 50 kN . If the available NDT equipment can only detect flaws larger than 3 mm in length, which of these two materials can be used to make a lighter member? Give your answer as a tuple: (AA7075_fracture_stress, Ti6Al4V_fracture_stress, AA7075_cross_section, Ti6Al4V_cross_section, AA7075_weight, Ti6Al4V_weight).

Ignore the factor of safety in this example and assume the following:

AA7075: yield strength $\sigma_{\mathrm{y}}=495 \mathrm{MPa}, K_{\mathrm{IC}}=24 \mathrm{MPa} \mathrm{m}{ }^{1 / 2}$, specific gravity $=2.7$
Ti-6Al-4V: yield strength $\sigma_{\mathrm{y}}=830 \mathrm{MPa}, K_{\mathrm{IC}}=60 \mathrm{MPa} \mathrm{m}{ }^{1 / 2}$, specific gravity $=4.5$

Aluminum alloy AA 6061-T6, steel AISI 4340 oil Q and tempered, and polyester $-30 \%$ glass fibers are being considered as a replacement for steel AISI 1025 normalized, in manufacturing a connecting rod to save weight. The connecting rod has a circular cross section and a length of 300 mm and is subjected to an alternating tensile load of 60 kN . Given the following information and assuming a derating factor of 0.4 on the fatigue strength for all alternatives, select the most suitable material:

AISI 1025: Tensile strength $=440 \mathrm{MPa}$, endurance ratio $=0.41$, specific gravity $=7.8$
AA 6061-T6: Tensile strength $=314 \mathrm{MPa}$, endurance ratio $=0.31$, specific gravity $=2.7$
AISI 4340: Tensile strength $=952 \mathrm{MPa}$, endurance ratio $=0.56$, specific gravity $=7.8$
Polyester $-30 \%$ glass: Tensile strength $=123 \mathrm{MPa}$, endurance ratio $=0.68$, specific gravity $=1.45$

Give your answer as a tuple: (AISI_1025_cross_section, AISI_1025_weight, AA_6061_T6_cross_section, AA_6061_T6_weight, AISI_4340_cross_section, AISI_4340_weight, polyester_30_glass_cross_section, polyester_30_glass_weight).

A structural element is made of a material with an average tensile strength of 2100 MPa . The element is subjected to a static tensile stress of an average value of 1600 MPa . If the variations in material quality and load cause the strength and stress to vary according to normal distributions with standard deviations of $\sigma_S=400$ and $\sigma_L=300$, respectively, what is the probability of failure of the structural element? Give your answer as the value of probability_of_failure.

If the range of strength of an alloy is given as $800-1200 \mathrm{MPa}$, what is the mean strength, the standard deviation, and coefficient of variation? Give your answer as a tuple: (mean_strength, standard_deviation, coefficient_of_variation).

Determine the dimensions of a cantilever beam of length 1 m and rectangular cross section of depth-to-width ratio $2: 1$. The cantilever is expected not to deflect more than 50 mm for every 1000 N increment of load at its tip. The material used in making the beam is steel AISI 4340 with a yield strength of 1420 MPa and UTS 1800 MPa . What is the maximum permissible load? Assume a suitable factor of safety. Give your answer as a tuple: (beam_width, maximum_permissible_load).

Assume the narrowest prong of a stainless‐steel picnic fork has a rectangular cross section 5 mm wide by 0.6 mm thick.  If you replace the steel with HDPE (EHDPE=1.1 GPa, Ess=210 GPa, ρHDPE=0.96 g/cm³, ρss=7.8 g/cm³) but keep the same width (5 mm) and want to maintain the same bending stiffness (EI), what thickness h of HDPE is required?  What is the new HDPE cross‐sectional area, and what is the HDPE fork’s weight relative to the steel fork?

Give your answer as a tuple (required moment of inertia, HDPE section area, relative weight ratio).

A truck has a fully loaded gross mass of $6000\;\mathrm{kg}$. Two-thirds of this mass is carried by the two rear axle shafts, which are identical. Each shaft may be modelled as a cantilever beam $1\;\mathrm{m}$ long that supports the wheel load applied at the free end. The shafts are made from heat-treated AISI 4340 steel whose ultimate tensile strength is $S_{\mathrm{ut}} = 952\;\mathrm{MPa}$. Because of the need to fit bearings, a step in diameter is required, and the fillet at this step gives an effective fatigue stress-concentration factor of $k_{f}=0.7$.

* For fatigue design, use the following data:


* fully reversed bending,


* endurance limit before modifying factors: $S_{e}' = 0.56\,S_{\mathrm{ut}} = 532\;\mathrm{MPa}$,


* surface-finish factor: $k_{a} = 0.68$,


* size factor: $k_{b} = 0.80$,


* reliability factor: $k_{c} = 0.752$,


* loading factor: $k_{e} = 1.0$,

assume other Marin factors are unity.



Assume gravitational acceleration $g = 9.8\;\mathrm{m/s^2}$. Using the distortion-energy criterion for completely reversed bending, and taking the section modulus for a solid circular shaft as $Z = \pi D^{3}/32$, determine the required common diameter $D$ (in millimetres, rounded to the nearest millimetre) so that each rear axle shaft has infinite fatigue life. Report your answer as \texttt{shaft\_diameter = \_\_\_ mm}.

A cantilever beam of length \(L = 1.0\,\text{m}\) (\(1000\,\text{mm}\)) has a section modulus  
\(Z = 2148\,\text{mm}^{3}\).  
The beam is made from heat-treated AISI 4340 steel with an ultimate tensile strength  
\(S_{\mathrm{u}} = 1800\,\text{MPa}\).  
Its fully corrected endurance limit may be taken as  
\(S_{e}=0.56\,S_{\mathrm{u}}\).

A mean load \(F_{\text{mean}} = 1000\,\text{N}\) is applied at the free end and fluctuates symmetrically about this value.  
Determine the largest alternating load amplitude \(F_{\text{alt}}\) that can be applied without causing fatigue failure, using

\[
n_{\text{s}} = 1.5 \quad\text{(static safety factor)}, \qquad
n_{\text{d}} = 3 \quad\text{(dynamic safety factor)},
\]

and the Soderberg fatigue criterion.

Give your name as a tuple (the Soderberg fatigue criterion, the largest alternating load amplitude \(F_{\text{alt}}\)).

Assuming that a turbine blade made of Nimonic 105 alloy had a life of $10,000 \mathrm{~h}$ at 150 MPa with a service temperature of $810^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, what is the expected life at the same stress but with a service temperature of $750^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ?  Give your answer as the value of expected_life.

A fastener is made of a given polymer with a relaxation time of one year at $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. What is the time needed to reduce the original stress exerted by the fastener by $25 \% ?$ Give your answer as the value of time_needed.

A steel plate of thickness \( t_s = 10\,\mathrm{mm} \) is to be replaced by a steel–polyethylene sandwich panel of the same width and bending stiffness in order to reduce weight.  
The sandwich panel consists of two outer steel facings each of thickness \( t_f = 0.5\,\mathrm{mm} \), and a polyethylene foam core of negligible stiffness.

Given material properties:
\[
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{steel}} &= 210\,\mathrm{GPa}, \\
\rho_{\text{steel}} &= 7800\,\mathrm{kg/m^3}, \\
\rho_{\text{core}} &= 200\,\mathrm{kg/m^3},
\end{aligned}
\]

Calculate the total thickness of the laminate and the mass ratio.

Give your answer as a tuple: (total thickness, mass ratio).

An AISI 1020 steel angle of dimensions $150 \mathrm{~mm} \times 150 \mathrm{~mm} \times 12 \mathrm{~mm}(6 \mathrm{in} . \times 4 \mathrm{in} . \times$ $1 / 2 \mathrm{in}$.) is to be welded to a steel plate by fillet welds along the edges of the 150 mm leg. The angle should support a load of $270 \mathrm{kN}(60,000 \mathrm{lb})$ acting along its length. Determine the lengths of the welds to be specified. The welding electrode used is AWS-AISI E6012 with a tensile strength of $414 \mathrm{MPa}(60 \mathrm{ksi})$. Give your answer as the value of weld_length.

Select the least-expensive alternative out of the candidate materials in Table 8.2 for making a suspension member in the cable car system. The member is 1 m long and is expected to carry a tensile load of 50 kN without yielding. Give your answer as the value of relative_cost_of_member.

Using a drill jig is expected to reduce the drilling time from 30 to 12 min . If a jig is not used, a skilled worker will be needed with an hourly rate of $\$ 12$. Using the jig makes it possible for a less-skilled worker to do the job at an hourly rate of $\$ 10$. The hourly rate for using the drilling machine is $\$ 8$. The cost of designing and manufacturing the jig is estimated as $\$ 1200$. The interest rate is $12 \%$, and the expected life of the jig is 2 years. For the purpose of calculating the total jig cost over its useful life ( $C_{\mathrm{T}}$ ), use the formula $C_{\mathrm{T}}=$ Initial Cost + $\frac{(\text { Initial Cost } \times \text { Annual Interest Rate } \times \text { Life in Years) }}{2}$. It is estimated that the jig will be used for the production of 400 parts during its useful life. Is the use of the jig economically justifiable? How many parts need to be produced for the jig to break even? Give your answer as a tuple: (savings_per_piece, total_jig_cost, jig_cost_per_part, break_even_number_of_parts).

Aluminum alloy 7075-T6 (yield strength $Y_{\text{Al}} = 511$ MPa, density $\rho_{\text{Al}} = 2.7$ g/cm$^3$) and Ti-6Al-4V (yield strength $Y_{\text{Ti}} = 939$ MPa, density $\rho_{\text{Ti}} = 4.5$ g/cm$^3$) are being considered for a 200 mm long tensile member carrying a load of 50 kN. Assume a factor of safety of 1.5.

Which material results in a lighter tensile member, and which material results in a lower embodied-energy impact?

Use the following specific embodied energies:  
Aluminum: 193.5 MJ/kg  
Titanium: 915 MJ/kg

Give your answer as a tuple $(m_{\text{Al}},\ m_{\text{Ti}},\ E_{\text{Al}},\ E_{\text{Ti}})$

The wall thickness of a steel tank is measured monthly, and the loss in thickness is approximately the same each month, $50 \mathrm{mg} / \mathrm{dm}^2 / \mathrm{day}$. What is the useful life of the tank, if the initial thickness is 10 mm and the minimum safe thickness is 6 mm ?

An aluminum 2014 T 6 tube of 75 mm ( 3 in .) outer diameter and 1 mm ( 0.04 in .) thickness is subjected to internal pressure of $8.4 \mathrm{MPa}\left(1200 \mathrm{lb} / \mathrm{in}^2\right)$. What is the factor of safety that was taken against failure by yielding when the pipe was designed?

A structural member is made of steel of mean yield strength of 200 MPa ( 28.6 ksi ) and a standard deviation on strength of $30 \mathrm{MPa}(4.3 \mathrm{ksi})$. The applied stress has a mean value of $150 \mathrm{MPa}(14.3 \mathrm{ksi})$ and a standard deviation of $5 \mathrm{MPa}\left(700 \mathrm{lb} / \mathrm{in}^2{ }^2\right)$. (a) What is the probability of failure? (b) What factor of safety is required if the allowable failure rate is $1 \%$ ? Give your answer as a tuple: (probability_of_failure, factor_of_safety).

The manufacture of a given component requires eight operations in the machine shop. The average setup time per operation is 2.5 h . Average operation time per machine is 7 min . The average nonoperation time needed for handling of workpieces and tools, inspection, delays, and temporary storage is 50 min per operation. Estimate the total time required to get a batch of 100 components through the workshop.
A cubic crystal is loaded with a tensile stress of 2.8 MPa applied along the [210] direction, as shown in the figure. Find the shear stress on the (111) plane in the $[10\bar{1}]$ direction.

Find the principal stresses in a body under the stress state, $\sigma_x = 10$, $\sigma_y = 8$, $\sigma_z = -5$, $\tau_{yz} = \tau_{zy} = 5$, $\tau_{zx} = \tau_{xz} = -4$, and $\tau_{xy} = \tau_{yx} = -8$, where all stresses are in MPa.  Give your answer as a tuple (...) containing values in ascending order.

A body is loaded under stresses, $\sigma_x = 150 \, \text{MPa}$, $\sigma_y = 60 \, \text{MPa}$, $\tau_{xy} = 20 \, \text{MPa}$, $\sigma_z = \tau_{yz} = \tau_{zx} = 0$. Find the largest shear stress in the body.

An element 1 cm long is extended to twice its initial length (2 cm) and then compressed to its initial length (1 cm).
A. Find true strains for the extension and compression.
B. Find engineering strains for the extension and compression.
Give your answer as an ordered tuple (A extension strains, A compression strains, B extension strains, B compression strains).

A bar 10 cm long is elongated by (1) drawing to 15 cm, and then (2) drawing to 20 cm.
A. Calculate the sum of engineering strains for the two steps, and the engineering strain for the overall deformation.
B. Repeat the calculation with true strains.
Give your answer as an ordered tuple (A: the sum of engineering strains for the two steps, A: the engineering strain for the overall deformation, B: the sum of true the two steps, B:  the true strains overall deformation).

A block of initial dimensions $L_{x0}$, $L_{y0}$, $L_{z0}$ is deformed so that the new dimensions are $L_x$, $L_y$, $L_z$. Express the volume strain, $V/V_0$, in terms of the three true strains, $\varepsilon_x$, $\varepsilon_y$, $\varepsilon_z$.

A two-dimensional square body initially 1.00 cm by 1.00 cm was deformed into a rectangle 0.95 cm by 1.10 cm , as shown in the figure.
A. Calculate the strain, $e'_x$, along the diagonal from its initial and final dimensions. Then calculate the strains, $e_x$ and $e_y$, along the edges and use the transformation equation, $e_{ij}=\sum_m \sum_n \ell_{im} \ell_{jn} e_{mn}$, to find the strain along the diagonal. What is the absolute difference between the two values of $e'_{x}$.
B. Repeat A for a $1.00\,\text{cm}$ by $1.00\,\text{cm}$ square deformed into a $0.50\,\text{cm}$ by $2.0\,\text{cm}$ rectangle.
Give your answer as an ordered tuple (A's difference, B's difference).

A capped thin-wall tube having a length $L$, a diameter $D$, and a wall thickness $t$, is loaded by an internal pressure $P$. Find the largest stresses in the wall assuming that $t \ll D$ and that $D \ll L$.
Derive the formula of the torque $T$ acting on the rod of radius $R$ with respect to the internal shear stress $\tau_{xy}(r)$.

A wide sheet (1 mm thick) of steel is bent elastically to a constant radius of curvature, $\rho = 50~\text{cm}$, measured from the axis of bending to the center of the sheet, as shown in the figure. The strain, $\epsilon_y$, and the stress, $\sigma_z$, are zero. Knowing that $E = 208~\text{GPa}$, and $\nu = 0.29$ for steel, find the stress in the surface. Assume that there is no net force in the plane of the sheet. Give your answer as a tuple ($\sigma_x$, $\sigma_y$).
A body is loaded elastically so that $e_y = 0$ and $\sigma_z = 0$. Both $\sigma_x$ and $\sigma_y$ are finite. Derive an expression for $e_z$ in terms of $\sigma_x$, $E$, and $\nu$ only.
a body loaded elastically under balanced biaxial tension, $\sigma_1 = \sigma_2$. Assume $\nu = 0.25$. What is the largest shear strain, $\gamma$?

A glaze is applied on a ceramic body by heating it above $600\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$, which allows it to flow over the surface. On cooling, the glaze becomes rigid at $500\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$. See Figure. The coefficients of thermal expansion of the glaze and the body are $\alpha_g = 4.0 \times 10^{-6}\,{}^{\circ}\mathrm{C}^{-1}$ and $\alpha_b = 5.5 \times 10^{-6}\,{}^{\circ}\mathrm{C}^{-1}$. The elastic constants for the glaze are $E_g = 70\,\mathrm{GPa}$ and $\nu_g = 0.3$. Calculate the stresses in the glaze when it has cooled to $20\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$.

For a hexagonal crystal under hydrostatic pressure, where the stress components are \(\sigma_1 = \sigma_2 = \sigma_3 = -p\), derive an expression for the rate of change of the axial ratio \(c/a\) with respect to pressure, \(\frac{d(c/a)}{dp}\), in terms of the elastic compliance components \(s_{ij}\) and the ratio \(c/a\).
In a tensile test, a material fractured before necking. The true stress and strain at fracture were $630 \text{ MPa}$ and $0.18$, respectively. What is the tensile strength of the material?

Calculate the temperature rise in a tension test of a low-carbon steel after a tensile elongation of 22%. Assume that 95% of the energy goes to heat and remains in the specimen. Also assume that the figure represents the stress-strain curve of the material. For steel, the heat capacity is $447 \, \mathrm{J} / (\mathrm{kg} \cdot \mathrm{K})$, and the density is $7.88 \, \mathrm{Mg} / \mathrm{m}^3$. The engineering stress–strain curve is in the figure.

Consider the accuracy of \( R \) measurement for a material having an \( R \) of about 1.00. Assume the width is about 0.5 in. and can be measured to an accuracy of \( \pm 0.001 \) in., which corresponds to an uncertainty of width strain of \( \pm 0.001 / 0.5 = \pm 0.002 \). The errors in measuring the length strain are much smaller.  
Assuming the transverse strain is approximately half the axial strain (as expected when \( R = 1 \)), estimate the percentage error in finding \( R \) if the total strain was 5%. How would this be reduced if the total strain was 15%? Give your answer in an ordered tuple (the relative error when the total strain was 5%, the  relative error when the total strain was 15%).

In a tensile test, the engineering stress $s = 100 \text{ MPa}$ at an engineering strain of $e = 0.20$. Find the corresponding values of $\sigma$ and $\varepsilon$. At what engineering stress and strain in compression would the values of $|\sigma|$ and $|\varepsilon|$ equal those values of $\sigma$ and $\varepsilon$? Give your answer as an order tuple (engineering stress, engineering strain).

Assume that in a hydraulic bulge test, the bulged surface is a portion of a sphere and that at every point on the thickness of the bulged surface the thickness is the same. (Note: This is not strictly true.) Express the radius of curvature, $\rho$, in terms of the die radius, $r$, and the bulged height, $h$. See the figure.

Consider a torsion test in which the twist is great enough that the entire cross-section is plastic. Assume that the shear yield stress, $\tau$, is a constant. Express the torque, $T$, in terms of the bar radius, $R$, and $\tau$.

Consider a four‐point bend test on a flat sheet of width w and thickness t. The supports are separated by span L, and two equal loads F/2 are applied at points symmetrically spaced so that the pure‐bending region between the loads has constant moment. Assuming the entire thickness yields (fully plastic bending), derive an expression for the material’s yield stress Y in terms of the measured load F, w, t, and L.

Two points on a true stress-strain curve are $\sigma = 222$ MPa at $\varepsilon = 0.05$ and $\sigma = 303$ MPa at $\varepsilon = 0.15$.
A. Find the values of $K$ and $n$ in equation $\sigma=K\varepsilon^n$ that best fit the data. Then using these values of $K$ and $n$, predict the true stress at a strain of $\varepsilon = 0.30$.
B. Find the values of $\sigma_0$ and $A$ in equation $\sigma = \sigma_0[1-\exp(-A\varepsilon)]$ that best fit the data. Then using these values of $\sigma_0$ and $A$, predict the true stress at a strain of $\varepsilon = 0.30$.
Give your answer as an order tuple (true stress  in question A, true stress in question B)

A material’s true stress–true strain curve is σ = Y + A ε.  Using the Considère criterion for the onset of necking (i.e. dσ/dε = σ), find the engineering strain e at which necking begins, in terms of the constants Y and A.

To ensure that the neck in a tensile bar would occur at the middle of the gauge section, the machinist made the bar with a 0.500 in. diameter in the middle of the gauge section and machined the rest of it to a diameter of 0.505 in. After testing, the diameter away from the neck was 0.470 in. Assume that the stress-strain relation follows the power law, $\sigma = K \epsilon^n$. What was the value of $n$?

Under plane stress (σ₃=0), let the principal stresses be σ₁ and σ₂, and define α=σ₂/σ₁.  Using the von Mises yield criterion with uniaxial tensile yield stress Y, determine the maximum possible value of σ₁/Y at yielding and the corresponding α.  Give your answer as (σ₁/Y, α).

Circles were printed on the surface of a part before it was deformed. Examination after deformation revealed that the principal strains in the sheet are $\varepsilon_1 = 0.18$ and $\varepsilon_2 = 0.078$. Assume that the tools did not touch the surface of concern and that the ratio of stresses remained constant during the deformation. Using the von Mises criterion, find the ratio of the principal stresses $\alpha = \sigma_2/\sigma_1$.

The strains measured on the surface of a piece of sheet metal after deformation are $\varepsilon_1 = 0.182$, $\varepsilon_2 = -0.035$. The stress-strain curve in tension can be approximated by $\sigma = 30 + 40\varepsilon$. Assume the von Mises criterion, and assume that the loading was such that the ratio of $\varepsilon_2 / \varepsilon_1$ was constant. Calculate the levels of $\varepsilon_1$ and $\varepsilon_2$ reached before unloading. Give your answer as an ordered tuple ($\varepsilon_1$, $\varepsilon_2$).

A steel sheet that has an $R$ value of 1.75 in all directions in the sheet was stretched in biaxial tension with $\sigma_z = 0$. Strain measurements indicate that throughout the deformation, $\varepsilon_y = 0$. Find the stress ratio, $\alpha = \sigma_y / \sigma_x$, that prevailed according to the Hill yield criterion and with the nonquadratic yield criterion with $a = 8$.

The strain rate dependence of a zinc alloy can be represented by equation $\sigma = C \dot{\varepsilon}^m$ with $m=0.07$. What is the ratio of the flow stress, $\sigma_2 / \sigma_1$, at $\varepsilon=0.10$ for a strain rate of $10^{3} \, \text{s}^{-1}$ to that at $\varepsilon=0.10$ for a strain rate of $10^{-3} \, \text{s}^{-1}$? Repeat for a low-carbon steel with $m=0.01$. Give your answer as an ordered tuple ($\sigma_2 / \sigma_1$ for zinc, $\sigma_2 / \sigma_1$ for steel).

The tensile stress in one region of an HSLA steel sheet ($m = 0.005$) is 1% greater than in another region. What is the ratio of the strain rates in the two regions? Neglect strain hardening. What would be the ratio of the strain rates in the two regions for a titanium alloy ($m = 0.02$)? Give your answer as a tuple (the ratio for HALA steel sheet, the ratio for titanium alloy).

A tensile bar is machined so that the diameter at one location is 1% smaller than the rest of the bar. The bar is tested at high temperature, so strain hardening is negligible, but the strain rate exponent is 0.25. When the strain in the reduced region is 0.20, what is the strain in the larger region?

The figure is a plot of measured strain rate exponents, $m$, for a large number of steels as a function of the flow stress at which they were measured. Find the value of $m'$ in equation $ \nabla \sigma = m' \ln (\dot{\varepsilon}_2/\dot{\varepsilon}_1)$ that best fits the data in the figure.
For an aluminum alloy under constant load, the creep rate increased by a factor of 2 when the temperature was increased from $71\,^\circ\text{C}$ to $77\,^\circ\text{C}$. Find the activation energy.
A tensile stress of $\sigma = 5 \text{ kPa}$ is applied parallel to the [432] direction of a cubic crystal. Find the shear stress, $\tau$, on the $(11\bar{1})$ plane in the [011] direction.

A cubic crystal is subjected to a stress state, $\sigma_x = 15 \text{ kPa}$, $\sigma_y = 0$, $\sigma_z = 7.5 \text{ kPa}$, $\tau_{yz} = \tau_{zx} = \tau_{xy} = 0$, where $x = [100]$, $y = [010]$, and $z = [001]$. What is the shear stress on the $(11\bar{1})[10\bar{1}]$ slip system? See the figure.

If slip occurs on the $(11\bar{1})[101]$ slip system described below, what will be the ratios of the resulting strains, $d\varepsilon_y / d\varepsilon_x$ and $d\varepsilon_z / d\varepsilon_x$, where $x=[100]$, $y=[010]$, and $z=[001]$?

The system: a cubic crystal is subjected to a stress state, $\sigma_x = 15 \text{ kPa}$, $\sigma_y = 0$, $\sigma_z = 7.5 \text{ kPa}$, $\tau_{yz} = \tau_{zx} = \tau_{xy} = 0$, where $x = [100]$, $y = [010]$, and $z = [001]$. Give your answer as a tuple ($\frac{d\varepsilon_y}{d\varepsilon_x}$, $\frac{d\varepsilon_z}{d\varepsilon_x}$).

The number of independent slip systems is the same as the number of the strain components, $\varepsilon_1$, $\varepsilon_2$, $\gamma_{23}$, $\gamma_{31}$, $\gamma_{12}$, that can be accommodated by slip. Find the number of independent slip systems in crystals of the NaCl structure that deform by $\langle 110 \rangle \{1 \overline{1} 0\}$ slip.
Using proper notation, write the Burgers vector for a dislocation corresponding to the shortest repeat distance in a bcc crystal.

Calculate the distance from the core of a screw dislocation at which the energy per volume equals the heat of vaporization, $H_v$, and express this distance in terms of $r/b$. Evaluate this critical value of $r/b$ for copper. $\rho=8.93 \text{ Mg/m}^3$, $G=77 \text{ GPa}$, $b=0.255 \text{ nm}$, $H_v=4.73 \text{ MJ/kg}$.

Consider two parallel dislocations with Burgers vectors $\mathbf{b}_1 = \frac{a}{2}[110]$ and $\mathbf{b}_2 = a[0\overline{1}1]$. Would their interaction and subsequent reaction be energetically favorable? Provide your answer as  1 if favorable or 0 if unfavorable.
Use equation $F_L = -\frac{Gb_1 b_2}{2 \pi r}$ to make an approximate calculation of equilibrium separation of the partial dislocations. Neglect the fact that both partials cannot be pure screws.

Slip was found to first occur in some aluminum crystals when the resolved shear stress reached 1.0 MPa. Assuming that this is the stress necessary to operate the largest Frank-Read sources, calculate the size, $d$, of these sources.

Consider the energy expended as a dislocation with vacancy-producing jogs moves through a crystal. Assume that the distance between jogs is \( d \), the magnitude of the Burgers vector is \( b \), and the energy required to produce a vacancy is \( U_v \). Find the drag force per unit length, \( f_L \), caused by the jogs when the dislocation moves through the crystal.
The distance $\overline{AG}$ in the figure is a little smaller than the normal interatomic distance (e.g., $\overline{AF}$) as shown in the figure. By what percentage is this distance too small?

Evaluate the constants $\sigma_0$ and $K_Y$ in equation $\sigma = \sigma_0 + K_Y d^{-1/2}$ for ferrite using the data in the Figure. 

Give your answer as a tuple ($K_Y$, $\sigma_0$).

A high-strength, low-alloy steel is strengthened by precipitates of VC. If the diameter of the particles is 50 nm, what volume percent particles would be required to increase the strength by 100 MPa? For iron, $G = 80$ GPa, and the atomic diameter is 0.248 nm.

Aluminum alloy 6061 is an age-hardenable alloy containing about 1% Mg and 0.6% Si. The precipitates that form on aging are $\text{Mg}_2\text{Si}$. The yield strength in the solution-treated and quenched condition is 150 MPa. After aging 1 hr at $230\,^\circ\text{C}$, the yield strength is about 250 MPa. In the completely overaged condition, the yield strength is about 75 MPa. In the aged condition, what is the strength increase, $\Delta\sigma$, attributable to particle hardening? What is the strength increase attributable to solid solution hardening by Mg and Si in the solution-treated condition? Give your answer as a tuple (strength increase attributable to particle hardening, strength increase attributable to solid solution).
Calculate the stress concentration at the tip of an elliptical crack having major and minor radii of $a = 100 \, \text{nm}$ and $b = 1 \, \text{nm}$.

A 6-foot-long steel strut, 2.0 in. wide and 0.25 in. thick, is designed to carry an 80,000-lb tension load. Assume that the steel has a toughness of $40 \, \text{ksi} \sqrt{\text{in}}$. What is the longest edge crack that will not cause failure?

Using stress field in equation ($\begin{aligned} \sigma_{\mathrm{x}} & =\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{I}} /(2 \pi r)^{1 / 2} \cos (\theta / 2)[1-\sin (\theta / 2) \sin (3 \theta / 2)] \\ \sigma_{\mathrm{y}} & =\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{I}} /(2 \pi r)^{1 / 2} \cos (\theta / 2)[1+\sin (\theta / 2) \sin (3 \theta / 2)] \\ \tau_{\mathrm{xy}} & =\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{I}} /(2 \pi r)^{1 / 2} \cos (\theta / 2) \sin (\theta / 2) \cos (3 \theta / 2) \\ \sigma_{\mathrm{z}} & =v\left(\sigma_{\mathrm{x}}+\sigma_{\mathrm{y}}\right) \text { for plane strain }\left(\varepsilon_{\mathrm{z}}=0\right) \text { and } \sigma_{\mathrm{z}}=0 \text { for plane stress } \\ \tau_{\mathrm{yz}} & =\tau_{\mathrm{zx}}=0\end{aligned}$) for plane stress $\left(\sigma_{\mathrm{z}}=0\right)$, calculate the values of $r$ at $\theta=0^{\circ}$ and $90^{\circ}$ at which the von Mises yield criterion is satisfied. Express the values of $r$ in terms of $\left(\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{Ic}} / Y\right)$.

Give your answer as a tuple (r_at_theta_0, r_at_theta_90).

A support is to be made from 4340 steel. The steel may be tempered to different yield strengths. The correlation between strength and toughness is shown in the figure. Assume that $f=1.1$ for the support geometry and that nondestructive inspection can detect all edge cracks of size $a=2 \, \text{mm}$ or larger. If a 4340 steel of $K_{\text{Ic}} = 120 \, \text{MPa} \sqrt{\text{m}}$ is used, what is the largest stress that will not cause either yielding or fracture?

A medium carbon steel has a yield strength of 250 MPa and a fracture toughness of 80 MPa $\sqrt{\text{m}}$. How thick would a compact tensile specimen have to be for valid plane-strain fracture testing?
For a given viscoelastic material, the ratio of initial force to equilibrium force is given by $F_0 / F_\infty = 1.02$. Find the relaxation-time ratio $\tau_e / \tau_\sigma$ for this material.
The amplitude of a vibrating member decreases so that the amplitude on the 100th cycle is 13% of the amplitude on the first cycle. Determine logarithmic decrement $\Lambda$.

A piece of metal is subjected to a cyclic stress of 80 MPa. If the phase angle $\delta = 0.1^\circ$ and no heat is transferred to the surroundings, what will be the temperature rise after 100,000 cycles? Data: $E = 80 \text{ GPa}$, $C = 800 \text{ J} / (\text{kg} \cdot \text{K})$, and $\rho = 4.2 \text{ Mg} / \text{m}^3$.

Assume that carbon interstitially dissolved in iron causes Snoek damping and that the frequency (cycles/s) at the peak amplitude is proportional to the diffusivity $(m^2/s)$ of C in Fe. Use the data in the figure to find the activation energy for diffusion of C in Fe.

During creep under constant stress, the strain rate increased by a factor of 1.43 when the temperature was increased from 800 K to 805 K. A. Calculate the activation energy assuming $\dot{\varepsilon} = (A/T) \exp(-Q/RT)$. B. Repeat assuming $\dot{\varepsilon} = B \exp(-Q/RT)$. Give you answer as a tuple (activation energy in A, activation energy in B).

For creep in Ni of grain size, $d = 200 \, \mu\text{m}$, determine the boundary between Coble and Nabarro-Herring creep on a deformation mechanism map using the data in the figure. The activation energy for lattice diffusion and grain boundary diffusion in Ni is $Q_L = 286 \, \text{kJ/mole}$ and $Q_{gb} = 115 \, \text{kJ/mole}$.
Use the initial linear portion of the S-N curve for aluminum alloy 7075 T-6 in the figure to find the values of $A$ and $b$ in the equation $S = AN^{-b}$. Give your answer as tuple (b, A).

A part made from the 7075-T4 aluminum alloy in the figure has been subjected to 200,000 cycles of 250 MPa and 40,000 cycles of 300 MPa stress amplitude. According to Miner's rule, how many additional cycles at 200 MPa can it withstand before failing? Using: $N = \left( \frac{S}{5400} \right)^{-1/0.24}$. $N_{250} = 3.63 \times 10^5$, $N_{300} = 1.70 \times 10^5$, $N_{200} = 10^6$.

Calculate the stress concentration factor for fatigue, $K_f$, for a plate of steel 2 in. wide and 0.25 in. thick with a hole 0.5 in. in diameter in the center. The steel has a tensile strength of 600 MPa.

A round bar of a steel has a yield strength of $40 \text{ ksi}$, a tensile strength of $60 \text{ ksi}$, and an endurance limit of $30 \text{ ksi}$. An elastic analysis indicates that $K_t = 2$. It is estimated that $q = 0.75$. The bar is to be loaded under bending such that a cyclic bending moment of $1500 \text{ in-lb}$ is superimposed on a steady bending moment of $1000 \text{ in-lb}$. The surface has a ground finish. What is the minimum diameter bar that would give an infinite life?
Find the exponent, $m$, and the constant $C$ in equation $\frac{da}{dN} = C(\Delta K_I)^m$ for aluminum alloy 7076-T6 using the data for $R=0$ in the figure. Give your answer as a tuple (m, C).

Consider crack growth in 7076-T6 aluminum. See Figure.
(a) Determine the crack growth rate, $\frac{da}{dN}$, if $\Delta \sigma = 200 \text{ MPa}$ and the crack is initially 1 mm in length. Assume $R=0$ and $f=1$.
(b) Calculate the number of cycles needed for the crack to grow from 1 mm to 10 mm if $\Delta \sigma = 200 \text{ MPa}$.

Give your answer as a tuple (the crack growth rate in (a), number of cycles in (b)).

A bar of copper with randomly oriented grains was deformed in tension to a stress, $\sigma_{\text{av}}$, which caused plastic deformation and then the load was released. Determine whether a grain oriented with [100] parallel to the tensile axis would be left in a state of residual tension or residual compression. For [100], the Taylor factor, $M=2.449$ and $E=66 \text{ GPa}$. For randomly oriented copper, $M=3.067$ and $E=128 \text{ GPa}$. Give your answer as 1 if it would be left in a state of residual tension, 2  if residual compression, and 3 otherwise.

Estimate the magnitude of the residual stresses in zinc after cooling from an annealing treatment. Consider cooling of a bicrystal consisting of crystal A with [0001] parallel to an external direction, $x$, and B, with [0001] perpendicular to $x$ as sketched in Figure. Assume that the cross-sections of A and B normal to $x$ are equal.

For zinc, $\alpha_B = 15 \times 10^{-6} \, {}^{\circ}\text{C}^{-1}$, $\alpha_A = 61.5 \times 10^{-6} \, {}^{\circ}\text{C}^{-1}$, $E_A = 119 \, \text{GPa}$ and $E_B = 34.8 \, \text{GPa}$.

A beam of thickness t made of an elastic–perfectly plastic material (elastic modulus E, yield stress Y) is subjected to a pure bending moment M≥M_plastic so that the entire cross-section yields.  Upon removal of M it elastically springs back.  Determine the residual stress at the outer (convex) and inner (concave) surfaces.

Give your answer as a tuple (the stress at the outer surface, the stress at the inner surface).

A thin layer (25 $\mu$m) was removed from a thin sheet (1.0 mm) of a plastic. After the layer was removed, the sheet became dish shaped with equal radii of curvature, $\rho = 50$ cm. Assume that the elastic modulus of the plastic is 4 GPa and $\nu = 0.4$. Calculate the stress that existed in the layer that was removed.

At a specific location in a material, the level of residual stress is $\sigma_0$. The material is given a stress relief anneal. At the annealing temperature, the material has a plastic creep rate of $\dot{\varepsilon}_p = -C \sigma^5$. Assuming the model of stress relief for which the dimensions remain constant, write an equation that describes how the level of residual stress, $\sigma$, decreases with time. Let the elastic modulus be $E$, and for simplicity, assume a uniaxial stress.

Analysis of 100 tests on a ceramic indicated that 11 specimens failed at 85 MPa or less and that 53 failed at a stress of 100 MPa or less. Estimate the stress that would cause $1$ failure in 10000 tests.

When the porosity of a certain ceramic is reduced from $2.3\%$ to $0.5\%$ the fracture stress drops by $12\%$. Assuming $\sigma \propto \exp(-bP)$, how much would the fracture stress increase above the level for $0.5\%$ porosity if all porosity were removed?

The temperature of the inside wall of a tube is \(200\,^{\circ}\text{C}\), and the outside wall temperature is \(40\,^{\circ}\text{C}\). Calculate the stresses at the outside of the wall if the tube is made from a glass having a coefficient of thermal expansion of \(\alpha = 8 \times 10^{-6} \,{}^{\circ}\text{C}^{-1}\), an elastic modulus of \(10 \times 10^{6} \, \text{psi}\), and a Poisson's ratio of 0.3. Let the reference position be the midwall, where $T = 120\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$.

The glass transition temperature, $T_g$, for an ethylenepropylene copolymer is $-59\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$. Find the temperature at which the toughness in a Charpy test is the same as in slow bending of a Charpy bar at $-59\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$. Assume the deformation in the Charpy test occurs in $1\,\mathrm{ms}$, whereas the slow bend test takes $10\,\mathrm{s}$. Assume that for this copolymer the WLF constants are C₁ = 17.4, C₂ = 51.6 K.

Assuming equation $\sigma = G(\lambda - \lambda^{-2})$, where $\lambda$ is the extension ratio, $\lambda = 1 + e$, where $e$ is the engineering strain, find an expression for Young's modulus, and value of Poisson's ratio at low strains (e=0). Give your answer as a tuple (expression for Young's modulus, Poisson's ratio)

Given the stress--stretch law  
\[
\sigma = G(\lambda - \lambda^{-2}), \quad G = NkT, \quad \lambda = \frac{L}{L_0},
\]
find an expression for the linear coefficient of thermal expansion  
\[
\alpha \equiv \frac{1}{L} \left( \frac{\partial L}{\partial T} \right)_{\sigma}
\]
as a function of \(\lambda\) and \(T\) (i.e., differentiate at constant \(\sigma\)).

von Mises yield criterion is $(\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2+(\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2+(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2+K_1(\sigma_1+\sigma_2+\sigma_3)|\sigma_1+\sigma_2+\sigma_3|=K_2$. Express $K_{1}$ and $K_{2}$ in the equation in terms of the tensile yield strength, $T$, and the absolute magnitude of the compressive yield strength, $C$. Give your answer as a tuple ($K_1$, $K_2$)

The compressive yield strength of a polymer is 15 % greater than its tensile yield strength. Calculate the ratio of the yield strength in balance biaxial tension, $\sigma_b$, to the yield strength in uniaxial tension, $T$, according to equation $(\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2+(\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2+(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2+2(C-T)(\sigma_1+\sigma_2+\sigma_3)=2CT$. Assuming von Mises yield criterion is $(\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2+(\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2+(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2+K_1(\sigma_1+\sigma_2+\sigma_3)|\sigma_1+\sigma_2+\sigma_3|=K_2$.

Evaluate $m$ and $2\tau_u$ in the equation $\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 + m(\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 + \sigma_3) = 2\tau_u$ in terms of $C$ and $T$. Then calculate the ratio of the yield strength in biaxial tension to that in uniaxial tension, $\sigma_{b} / T$, for a polymer for which the compressive yield strength is 15% higher than the tensile yield strength.

A metal composite composed of metal M reinforced by parallel wires of metal W. The volume fraction of W is 50%, and W has a yield stress of 200 MPa and a modulus of 210 GPa. Metal M has a yield stress of 50 MPa and a modulus of 30 GPa. What is the maximum stress that the composite can carry without yielding?

Consider the 16:1 extrusion of a round aluminum billet from a diameter of 24 cm to a diameter of 6 cm. Assume that the flow stress is 15 MPa at the appropriate temperature and extrusion rate. Estimate the extrusion force, assuming an efficiency of 0.50.

Find the horsepower that would be required to cold roll a 48-in.-wide sheet from a thickness of 0.030 in. to 0.025 in. if the exit speed is 80 ft/s and the flow stress is 10,000 psi. Assume a deformation efficiency of 80% and neglect work hardening.
To roll a sheet of 2.0 mm in thickness with 15-cm-diameter rolls, how large a reduction ( % reduction of thickness) would be necessary to ensure deformation resistance ratio $\Delta \geq 1$?
Find the angle between the tensile axis and the local neck form in a tension test on material with a strain ratio $R=2$.

Calculate the number of atoms in 100 g of silver (Ag). The atomic mass of silver is $107.868 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}$ and the Avogadro constant is $6.022 \times 10^{23} \mathrm{atoms} / \mathrm{mol}$.

Scientists are considering 3 nm‐diameter Fe nanoparticles for ultra‐high density data storage.  Given that the density of Fe is 7.8 g·cm⁻³ and its molar mass is 55.85 g·mol⁻¹, estimate the number of Fe atoms in a single spherical nanoparticle.
Given the Pauling electronegativities χ(Si) = 1.90 and χ(O) = 3.44, estimate the fraction of covalent bonding in SiO₂ .

Determine the number of lattice points per cell in the cubic crystal systems. If there is only one atom located at each lattice point, calculate the number of atoms per unit cell. Give your answer as a tuple: (SC_atoms, BCC_atoms, FCC_atoms).

Determine the relationship between the atomic radius and the lattice parameter in SC, BCC, and FCC structures when one atom is located at each lattice point. Give your answer as a tuple (SC, BCC, FCC) where SC, BCC, FCC are the relationships for simple cubic, body-centered cubic, and face-centered cubic structures respectively.

Calculate the packing factor for the FCC unit cell. The volume of one atom is $\frac{4}{3} \pi r^{3}$ and the volume of the unit cell is $a_{0}^{3}$, where $r$ is the radius of the atom and $a_{0}$ is the lattice parameter. For FCC unit cells, $a_{0} = \frac{4r}{\sqrt{2}}$.

Determine the density of BCC iron, which has a lattice parameter of 0.2866 nm. The atomic mass of iron is 55.847 g/mol, and the Avogadro constant is $6.022 \times 10^{23}$ atoms/mol. The volume of the unit cell can be calculated using the formula $V = a_{0}^{3}$, where $a_{0}$ is the lattice parameter. The theoretical density can be calculated using the formula $\rho = \frac{(\text{number of atoms/cell})(\text{atomic mass})}{(\text{volume of unit cell})(\text{Avogadro constant})}$.

Calculate the percent volume change as zirconia transforms from a tetragonal to monoclinic structure. The lattice constants for the monoclinic unit cells are $a=5.156$, $b=5.191$, and $c=5.304 \AA$, respectively. The angle $\beta$ for the monoclinic unit cell is $98.9^{\circ}$. The lattice constants for the tetragonal unit cell are $a=5.094$ and $c=5.304 \AA$.
Determine the Miller indices of directions $A, B$, and $C$ in Figure 3-15. Give your answer as a tuple (A, B, C) where A, B, and C are the Miller indices for directions A, B, and C respectively.

Determine the Miller indices of planes $A, B$, and $C$ in Figure, which illustrates crystallographic planes and intercepts. Give your answer as a tuple (Miller indices of plane A, Miller indices of plane B, Miller indices of plane C).

Calculate the planar density and planar packing fraction for the (010) and (020) planes in simple cubic polonium, which has a lattice parameter of 0.334 nm. The (010) and (020) planes are not equivalent, and no atoms are centered on the (020) planes. Give your answer as a tuple (planar density for (010) plane, planar packing fraction for (010) plane, planar density for (020) plane, planar packing fraction for (020) plane).

Determine the Miller-Bravais indices for planes $A$ and $B$ and directions $C$ and $D$ in Figure. The figure illustrates crystallographic planes and directions in HCP unit cells using a four-axis coordinate system. Give your answer as a tuple (Miller-Bravais indices for plane A, Miller-Bravais indices for plane B, Miller-Bravais indices for direction C, Miller-Bravais indices for direction D).
Calculate the number of octahedral sites that uniauely belong to one FCC unit cell.

For potassium chloride $(\mathrm{KCl})$, (a) calculate the radius ratio of $\mathrm{K}^{+}$ to $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ and (b) calculate the packing factor for the compound. The ionic radii are $r_{\mathrm{K}^{+}}=0.133 \mathrm{~nm}$ and $r_{\mathrm{Cl}^{-}}=0.181 \mathrm{~nm}$. Give your answer as a tuple (radius ratio, packing factor).

Calculate the density of MgO. Reference the radius ratio and coordination number to justify the crystal structure, and use the given ionic radii and atomic masses for your calculations. the ionic radii are $r_{\mathrm{Mg}^{+2}}=0.066 \mathrm{~nm}$ and $r_{\mathrm{O}^{-2}}=0.132 \mathrm{~nm}$, Atomic masses: Mg = 24.31 g mol⁻¹; O = 16.00 g mol⁻¹.

Describe the diamond cubic structure as a lattice and a basis and determine its packing factor. Give your answer as a tuple: (Packing_factor, a₀/r(simplified LaTeX-like notation for the lattice parameter relationship)).

The lattice constant of Si is $5.43 \AA$. Calculate the radius of a silicon atom and the theoretical density of silicon. The atomic mass of Si is $28.09 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}$. Give your answer as a tuple (radius of silicon atom, theoretical density of silicon).

Perovskite is a mineral containing calcium, titanium, and oxygen. The unit cell is cubic and has a calcium atom at each corner, an oxygen atom at each face center, and a titanium atom at the body-centered position. The atoms contribute to the unit cell in the usual way ( $1 / 8$ atom contribution for each atom at the corners, etc.). How many atoms of each type are there per unit cell?

How many carbon and hydrogen atoms are in each unit cell of crystalline polyethylene? There are twice as many hydrogen atoms as carbon atoms in the chain. The density of polyethylene is about $0.9972 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{cm}^3$. Give your answer as a tuple (carbon atoms, hydrogen atoms).

Calculate the concentration of vacancies in copper at room temperature $\left(25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)$. What temperature will be needed to heat treat copper such that the concentration of vacancies produced will be 1000 times more than the equilibrium concentration of vacancies at room temperature? Assume that 20,000 cal are required to produce a mole of vacancies in copper. The lattice parameter of FCC copper is 0.36151 nm. Give your answer as a tuple (concentration, temperature)

Calculate the theoretical density of iron, and then determine the number of vacancies needed for a BCC iron crystal to have a density of $7.874 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{cm}^{3}$. The lattice parameter of iron is $2.866 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{~cm}$. Assumes that there are no imperfections in the crystal. Give your answer as a tuple: (theoretical density, number of vacancies).

In FCC iron, carbon atoms are located at octahedral sites, which occur at the center of each edge of the unit cell at sites such as $(0,0,1 / 2)$ and at the center of the unit cell $(1 / 2,1 / 2,1 / 2)$. In BCC iron, carbon atoms enter tetrahedral sites, such as $(0,1 / 2,1 / 4)$. The lattice parameter is 0.3571 nm for FCC iron and 0.2866 nm for BCC iron. Assume that carbon atoms have a radius of 0.071 nm. (a) Would we expect a greater distortion of the crystal by an interstitial carbon atom in FCC or BCC iron? (b) What would be the atomic percentage of carbon in each type of iron if all the interstitial sites were filled? Give your answer as a tuple (atomic percentage in FCC, atomic percentage in BCC).

Write the appropriate defect reactions for (a) incorporation of magnesium oxide (MgO) in nickel oxide $(\mathrm{NiO})$ and (b) formation of a Schottky defect in alumina $(\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3})$ using the Kröger-Vink notation. Ensure that the reactions maintain charge, mass, and site balance. Give your answer as a tuple (defect_reaction_a, defect_reaction_b).
Write the appropriate defect reactions for the incorporation of calcium oxide $(\mathrm{CaO})$ in zirconia $(\mathrm{ZrO}_{2})$ using the Kröger-Vink notation.
Calculate the length of the Burgers vector in copper. The lattice parameter of copper $(\mathrm{Cu})$ is 0.36151 nm . The close-packed directions.

A sketch of a dislocation in magnesium oxide $(\mathrm{MgO})$, which has the sodium chloride crystal structure and a lattice parameter of 0.396 nm , is shown in Figure. Determine the length of the Burgers vector.

The planar density of the (112) plane in BCC iron is $9.94 \times 10^{14}$ atoms $/ \mathrm{cm}^{2}$. Calculate (a) the planar density of the (110) plane and (b) the interplanar spacings for both the (112) and (110) planes. The lattice parameter of BCC iron is 0.2866 nm or $2.866 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{~cm}$. Give your answer as a tuple (planar density of (110) plane, interplanar spacing of (112) plane, interplanar spacing of (110) plane).

We wish to produce a rod composed of a single crystal of pure aluminum, which has a critical resolved shear stress of 148 psi. We would like to orient the rod in such a manner that, when an axial stress of 500 psi is applied, the rod deforms by slip in a $45^{\circ}$ direction to the axis of the rod and actuates a sensor that detects the overload. Design the rod and a method by which it might be produced. Give your answer as a tuple: (λ_deg, φ_deg).

The yield strength of mild steel with an average grain size of 0.05 mm is $20,000 \mathrm{psi}$. The yield stress of the same steel with a grain size of 0.007 mm is $40,000 \mathrm{psi}$. What will be the average grain size of the same steel with a yield stress of $30,000 \mathrm{psi}$? Assume the Hall-Petch equation is valid and that changes in the observed yield stress are due to changes in grain size.
Suppose we count 16 grains per square inch in a photomicrograph taken at a magnification of 250. What is the ASTM grain size number?

Suppose that interstitial atoms are found to move from one site to another at the rates of $5 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{jumps} / \mathrm{s}$ at $500^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $8 \times 10^{10} \mathrm{jumps} / \mathrm{s}$ at $800^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Calculate the activation energy $Q$ for the process.

One step in manufacturing transistors, which function as electronic switches in integrated circuits, involves diffusing impurity atoms into a semiconductor material such as silicon (Si). Suppose a silicon wafer 0.1 cm thick, which originally contains one phosphorus atom for every 10 million Si atoms, is treated so that there are 400 phosphorous (P) atoms for every 10 million Si atoms at the surface. Calculate the concentration gradient (a) in atomic percent $/ \mathrm{cm}$ and (b) in $\frac{\text { atoms }}{\mathrm{cm}^{3} \cdot \mathrm{~cm}}$. The lattice parameter of silicon is $5.4307 \AA$. The crystal structure of Si is diamond cubic (DC) with 8 atoms per unit cell. Give your answer as a tuple (concentration gradient in atomic percent per cm, concentration gradient in atoms per cm^3 per cm).

A 0.05 cm layer of magnesium oxide $(\mathrm{MgO})$ is deposited between layers of nickel $(\mathrm{Ni})$ and tantalum (Ta) to provide a diffusion barrier that prevents reactions between the two metals (Figure). At $1400^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, nickel ions diffuse through the MgO ceramic to the tantalum. Determine The total number of nickel atoms crossing the $2 \mathrm{~cm} \times 2 \mathrm{~cm}$ interface per second. At $1400^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, the diffusion coefficient of nickel ions in MgO is $9 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{~cm}^{2} / \mathrm{s}$, and the lattice parameter of nickel at $1400^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is $3.6 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{~cm}$.

An impermeable cylinder 3 cm in diameter and 10 cm long contains a gas that includes $0.5 \times 10^{20} \mathrm{~N}$ atoms per $\mathrm{cm}^3$ and $0.5 \times 10^{20} \mathrm{H}$ atoms per $\mathrm{cm}^3$ on one side of an iron membrane (see Figure). Gas is continuously introduced to the pipe to ensure a constant concentration of nitrogen and hydrogen. The gas on the other side of the membrane includes a constant $1 \times 10^{18} \mathrm{~N}$ atoms per $\mathrm{cm}^3$ and $1 \times 10^{18}$ H atoms per $\mathrm{cm}^3$. The entire system is to operate at $700^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, at which iron has the BCC structure. Design an iron membrane that will allow no more than $1 \%$ of the nitrogen to be lost through the membrane each hour, while allowing $90 \%$ of the hydrogen to pass through the membrane per hour.Give your answer as a tuple: (N_total_atoms, Δx_min_cm, Δx_max_cm).

Consider a diffusion couple between pure tungsten and a tungsten alloy containing 1 at $\%$ thorium. After several minutes of exposure at $2000^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, a transition zone of 0.01 cm thickness is established. What is the flux of thorium atoms at this time if diffusion is due to (a) volume diffusion, (b) grain boundary diffusion, and (c) surface diffusion? Refer to Table for the diffusion coefficients and activation energies for thorium in tungsten. The lattice parameter of BCC tungsten is $3.165 \AA$. Give your answer as a tuple (volume diffusion flux, grain boundary diffusion flux, surface diffusion flux).

The surface of a $0.1 \% \mathrm{C}$ steel gear is to be hardened by carburizing. An aluminum rod is to withstand an applied force of 45,000 pounds. The engineering stress-strain curve for the aluminum alloy to be used is shown in Figure. To ensure safety, the maximum allowable stress on the rod is limited to $25,000 \mathrm{psi}$, which is below the yield strength of the aluminum. The rod must be at least 150 in. long but must deform elastically no more than 0.25 in. when the force is applied. Design an appropriate rod.

Reference Context: The engineering stress-strain curve (see Figure) is used to record the results of a tensile test. The curve helps in determining the elastic and plastic deformation characteristics of the material under load.

*Give your answer as the value of minimum_diameter_in.

Calculate the modulus of elasticity of the aluminum alloy for which the engineering stress-strain curve is shown in Figure. Calculate the length of a bar of initial length 50 in. when a tensile stress of $30,000 \mathrm{psi}$ is applied. The modulus of elasticity is closely related to the binding energies of the atoms, indicating that high forces are required to separate the atoms and cause the material to stretch elastically. Thus, the material has a high modulus of elasticity. For metallic materials, the modulus of elasticity is considered a microstructure insensitive property since the value is dominated by the stiffness of atomic bonds.

**Give your answer as a tuple: (modulus_of_elasticity, final_length).*

The aluminum alloy in has a final length after failure of 2.195 in. and a final diameter of 0.398 in at the fractured surface. Original gauge length l₀ = 2.000 in. and original diameter d₀ = 0.505 in. Calculate the ductility of this alloy. The ductility can be measured by the percent elongation and the percent reduction in area, as defined by the equations:

$$\% \text { Elongation }=\frac{l_{f}-l_{0}}{l_{0}} \times 100$$

$$\% \text { Reduction in area }=\frac{A_{0}-A_{f}}{A_{0}} \times 100$$

where $l_{f}$ is the final length after failure, $l_{0}$ is the original length, $A_{f}$ is the final cross-sectional area at the fracture surface, and $A_{0}$ is the original cross-sectional area. Give your answer as a tuple (percent elongation, percent reduction in area).

The flexural strength of a composite material reinforced with glass fibers is $45,000 \mathrm{psi}$, and the flexural modulus is $18 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{psi}$. Determine the force required to fracture the material and the deflection of the sample at fracture, assuming that no plastic deformation occurs. Give your answer as a tuple (force, deflection).

Calculate the elastic modulus and hardness of MgO . Give your answer as a tuple (elastic modulus, hardness).

Assume that an advanced ceramic sialon (acronym for SiAION or silicon aluminum oxynitride), has a tensile strength of $60,000 \mathrm{psi}$. Let us assume that this value is for a flaw-free ceramic. Determine the minimum allowable thickness for a 3-in.-wide plate made of sialon that has a fracture toughness of $9,000 \mathrm{psi} \sqrt{\mathrm{in}}$. The plate must withstand a tensile load of $40,000 \mathrm{lb}$. The part will be nondestructively tested to ensure that no flaws are present that might cause failure. The minimum allowable thickness of the part will depend on the minimum flaw size that can be determined by the available testing technique. Assume that three nondestructive testing techniques are available. An advanced engineered ceramic has a Weibull modulus $m=9$. The flexural strength is 250 MPa at a probability of failure $F=0.4$. What is the level of flexural strength if the probability of failure has to be 0.1 ?

A high-strength steel plate, which has a plane strain fracture toughness of $80 \mathrm{MPa} \sqrt{\mathrm{m}}$ is alternately loaded in tension to 500 MPa and in compression to 60 MPa. The crack growth rate versus the stress intensity factor range for this steel is given by $\frac{d a}{d N}=C(\Delta K)^{n}$, where $C=1.62 \times 10^{-12}$ and $n=3.2$ for the units shown in the figure. 

Give your answer as a tuple: (critical crack size, initial surface flaw size, number of cycles).

Assume good bonding between the fibers and the matrix, ensuring equal strain in both components.
A green wood has a density of $0.86 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{cm}^{3}$ and contains $175 \%$ water. Calculate the density of the wood after it has completely dried.

Calculate the amounts of water, cement, sand, and aggregate in 5 cubic yards of concrete, assuming that we want to obtain a water/cement ratio of 0.4 (by weight) and that the cement/sand/aggregate ratio is 1:2.5:4 (by weight). A "normal" aggregate will be used, containing $1 \%$ water, and the sand contains $4 \%$ water. Assume that no air is entrained into the concrete. Weight of one sack of cement is 94 lb. Assume the following densities: cement $=190 \mathrm{lb} / \mathrm{ft}^3$, dry sand $=160 \mathrm{lb} / \mathrm{ft}^3$, dry aggregate = $170 \mathrm{lb} / \mathrm{ft}^3$, and water $=62.4 \mathrm{lb} / \mathrm{ft}^3$. Give your answer as a tuple: (cement_sacks, sand_weight, gravel_weight, water_volume).

Assuming that all of the valence electrons contribute to current flow, (a) calculate the mobility of an electron in copper and (b) calculate the average drift velocity for electrons in a 100 cm copper wire when 10 V are applied. The lattice parameter of copper is $3.6151 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{~cm}$ and, since copper is FCC, there are four atoms/unit cell. The conductivity $\sigma=5.98 \times 10^{5} \Omega^{-1} \cdot \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$. Give your answer as a tuple (mobility of an electron in copper, average drift velocity for electrons in a 100 cm copper wire when 10 V are applied).

Calculate the electrical conductivity of pure copper at (a) 400 °C and (b) –100 °C.  The resistivity of copper at 25 °C is 1.67×10⁻⁶ Ω·cm, and its temperature‐resistivity coefficient is α=0.0043 /°C. 


 Give your answers in S/cm as (σ₄₀₀, σ₋₁₀₀).

For germanium at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, estimate (a) the number of charge carriers, (b) the fraction of the total electrons in the valence band that are excited into the conduction band, and (c) the constant $n_0$ in Equation ($n=n_i=p_i=n_0 \exp \left(\frac{-E_g}{2 \mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{B}} T}\right)$).
