At T = 300 K, p-type Si has a hole mobility μp ≃ 480 cm²/V·s.  What acceptor concentration NA is required to give a conductivity σ = 100 Ω⁻¹·cm⁻¹?  Compare NA with the atomic density of Si.

Give your answer as a tuple: (acceptor atoms per cm3, dopant atoms per Si atom).

At $1800^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ($T = 2073\ \mathrm{K}$), the diffusion coefficient of $\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$ ions in single–crystal MgO is  
\[
D = 1.0\times10^{-10}\ \mathrm{cm^{2}\,s^{-1}}.
\]  
(a) Estimate the mobility of the $\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$ ions.  
(b) Using the ion concentration that follows from the rock–salt structure (four $\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$ ions per cubic unit cell, lattice parameter $a = 3.96\times10^{-8}\ \mathrm{cm}$), calculate the electrical conductivity of MgO at $1800^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$.  

Useful relations:
\[
\mu = \frac{Z q D}{k_{\mathrm B} T}, \quad \sigma = n Z q \mu,
\]
where $Z = +2$, $q = 1.60\times10^{-19}\ \mathrm{C}$, and $k_{\mathrm B} = 1.38\times10^{-23}\ \mathrm{J\,K^{-1}}$.


Give your answer as a tuple: (mobility Mg2+ ions, electrical conductivity MgO).

Suppose that the average displacement of the electrons relative to the nucleus in a copper atom is $10^{-8} \AA$ when an electric field is imposed on a copper plate. Design a glass window $4 \mathrm{ft} \times 4 \mathrm{ft}$ square that separates a room at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ from the outside at $40^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and allows no more than $5 \times 10^6 \mathrm{cal}$ of heat to enter the room each day. Give your answer as the value of grams_lost_per_hour.

Determine the maximum allowable temperature that will produce a flux of less than 2000 H atoms $/\left(\mathrm{cm}^2 \cdot \mathrm{~s}\right)$ through a BCC iron foil when the concentration gradient is $-5 \times 10^{16} \frac{\text { atoms }}{\mathrm{cm}^3 \mathrm{~cm}}$. (Note the negative sign for the flux.)

Compare the rate at which oxygen ions diffuse in alumina $\left(\mathrm{Al}_2 \mathrm{O}_3\right)$ with the rate at which aluminum ions diffuse in $\mathrm{Al}_2 \mathrm{O}_3$ at $1500^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Explain the difference. Give your answer as a tuple: (oxygen_diffusion_rate, aluminum_diffusion_rate).

A carburizing process is carried out on a $0.10 \% \mathrm{C}$ steel by introducing $1.0 \% \mathrm{C}$ at the surface at $980^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, where the iron is FCC. Calculate the carbon content at 0.01 cm, 0.05 cm, and 0.10 cm beneath the surface after 1 h. Give your answer as a tuple: (carbon_content_0.01cm, carbon_content_0.05cm, carbon_content_0.10cm).

What temperature is required to obtain $0.50 \% \mathrm{C}$ at a distance of 0.5 mm beneath the surface of a $0.20 \%$ C steel in 2 h , when $1.10 \% \mathrm{C}$ is present at the surface? Assume that the iron is FCC.

A $0.02 \%$ C steel is to be carburized at $1200^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ in 4 h , with the carbon content 0.6 mm beneath the surface reaching $0.45 \%$ C. Calculate the carbon content required at the surface of the steel.

A $0.80 \% \mathrm{C}$ steel must operate at $950^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ in an oxidizing environment for which the carbon content at the steel surface is zero. Only the outermost 0.02 cm of the steel part can fall below $0.75 \% \mathrm{C}$. What is the maximum time that the steel part can operate?

What time is required to nitride a $0.002 \% \mathrm{~N}$ steel to obtain $0.12 \% \mathrm{~N}$ at a distance of 0.002 in . beneath the surface at $625^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ? The nitrogen content at the surface is $0.15 \%$.

During freezing of a $\mathrm{Cu}-\mathrm{Zn}$ alloy, we find that the composition is nonuniform. By heating the alloy to $600^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ for 3 h , diffusion of zinc helps to make the composition more uniform. What temperature would be required if we wished to perform this homogenization treatment in 30 minutes?

(a) A force of $100,000 \mathrm{~N}$ is applied to an iron bar with a cross-sectional area of $10 \mathrm{~mm} \times 20 \mathrm{~mm}$ and having a yield strength of 400 MPa and a tensile strength of 480 MPa. calculate the maximum force that a 0.2 -in. diameter rod of $\mathrm{Al}_2 \mathrm{O}_3$, having a yield strength of $35,000 \mathrm{psi}$, can withstand with no plastic deformation.

A polymer bar's dimensions are 1 in . $\times 2 \mathrm{in} . \times 15 \mathrm{in}$. The polymer has a modulus of elasticity of $600,000 \mathrm{psi}$. What force is required to stretch the bar elastically from 15 in. to 15.25 in.? Give your answer as the value of required_force_lb.

A steel cable 1.25 in . in diameter and 50 ft long is to lift a 20 ton load. What is the length of the cable during lifting? The modulus of elasticity of the steel is $30 \times 10^6 \mathrm{psi}$. Give your answer as the value of length_during_lifting_ft.

(a) A 0.4-in.-diameter, 12-in.-long titanium bar has a yield strength of $50,000 \mathrm{psi}$, a modulus of elasticity of $16 \times 10^6 \mathrm{psi}$, and a Poisson's ratio of 0.30. Determine the length and diameter of the bar when a $500-\mathrm{lb}$ load is applied. (b) When a tensile load is applied to a 1.5-cm-diameter copper bar, the diameter is reduced to $1.498-\mathrm{cm}$ diameter. Determine the applied load, using the data in Table. Give your answer as a tuple: (final_length_in, final_diameter_in).

\hline Material & $T_m\left({ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)$ & $E$ (psi) & Poisson's ratio ( $v$ ) \\
\hline Pb & 327 & $2.0 \times 10^6$ & 0.45 \\

\hline
\end{tabular}

A three-point bend test is performed on a block of $\mathrm{ZrO}_2$ that is 8 in . long, 0.50 in . wide, and 0.25 in . thick and is resting on two supports 4 in . apart. When a force of 400 lb is applied, the specimen deflects 0.037 in. and breaks. Calculate (a) the flexural strength and (b) the flexural modulus, assuming that no plastic deformation occurs. Give your answer as a tuple: (flexural_strength_psi, flexural_modulus_psi).

(a) A thermosetting polymer containing glass beads is required to deflect 0.5 mm when a force of 500 N is applied. The polymer part is 2 cm wide, 0.5 cm thick, and 10 cm long. If the flexural modulus is 6.9 GPa , determine the minimum distance between the supports. Assume that no plastic deformation occurs.

A Brinell hardness measurement, using a $10-\mathrm{mm}$-diameter indenter and a 500 kg load, produces an indentation of 4.5 mm on an aluminum plate. Determine the Brinell hardness number $(\mathrm{HB})$ of the metal. Give your answer as the value of Brinell_hardness_number.

A polymer that contains internal flaws 1 mm in length fails at a stress of 25 MPa. Determine the plane strain fracture toughness of the polymer. Assume that $f=1$. Give your answer as the value of plane_strain_fracture_toughness.

A cylindrical tool steel specimen that is 6 in . long and 0.25 in . in diameter rotates as a cantilever beam and is to be designed so that failure never occurs. Assuming that the maximum tensile and compressive stresses are equal, determine the maximum load that can be applied to the end of the beam. (See Figure.) Give your answer as the value of maximum_load_lb.

A cyclical load of 1500 lb is to be exerted at the end of a $10-\mathrm{in}$-long aluminium beam (see figure). The bar must survive for at least $10^6$ cycles. What is the minimum diameter of the bar? Give your answer as the value of minimum_diameter_in.

Suppose that we would like a part produced from the acetal polymer shown in Figure to survive for one million cycles under conditions that provide for equal compressive and tensile stresses. What is the fatigue strength, or maximum stress amplitude, required? What are the maximum stress, the minimum stress, and the mean stress on the part during its use?

Give your answer as a tuple: (fatigue_strength, max_stress, min_stress, mean_stress).

The high-strength steel in Figure, which has a critical fracture toughness of $80 \mathrm{MPa} \sqrt{\mathrm{m}}$, is subjected to an alternating stress varying from -900 MPa (compression) to +900 MPa (tension). It is to survive for $10^5$ cycles before failure occurs. Assume that $f=1$. Calculate (a) the size of a surface crack required for failure to occur; and (b) the largest initial surface crack size that will permit this to happen. Give your answer as a tuple: (surface_crack_size_failure_mm, largest_initial_crack_size_mm).
Calculate the constants $C$ and $n$ in Equation ($\frac{d a}{d N}=C(\Delta K)^n$)for the crack growth rate of an acrylic polymer. (See Figure.) Give your answer as a tuple: (C, n).

When a stress of $20,000 \mathrm{psi}$ is applied to a material heated to $900^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, rupture occurs in $25,000 \mathrm{~h}$. If the activation energy for rupture is $35,000 \mathrm{cal} / \mathrm{mol}$, determine the rupture time if the temperature is reduced to $800^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Give your answer as the value of rupture_time_h.
A 1-in-diameter bar of an iron-chromium-nickel alloy is subjected to a load of 2500 lb. How many days will the bar survive without rupturing at $980^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$? (See Figure(a).)
An iron-chromium-nickel alloy is to withstand a load of 1500 lb at $760^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ for 6 years. Calculate the minimum diameter of the bar. (See Figure(a).)
A $1 \mathrm{in} . \times 2$ in. ductile cast-iron bar must operate for 9 years at $650^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. What is the maximum load that can be applied? (See Figure(b).)

Consider the tensile stress-strain curves in Figure labeled 1 and 2 and answer the following questions. These curves are typical of metals. Consider each part as a separate question that has no relationship to previous parts of the question. (a) Which material has the larger workhardening exponent? How do you know? (b) Samples 1 and 2 are identical except that they were tested at different strain rates. Which sample was tested at the higher strain rate? How do you know? (c) Assume that the two stress-strain curves represent successive tests of the same sample. The sample was loaded, then unloaded before necking began, and then the sample was reloaded. Which sample represents the first test: 1 or 2 ? How do you know? Give your answer as a tuple: (larger_workhardening_exponent, higher_strain_rate_sample, first_test_sample).

Figure shows a plot of the natural log of the true stress versus the natural $\log$ of the true strain for a $\mathrm{Cu}-30 \% \mathrm{Zn}$ sample tested in tension. Only the plastic portion of the stress-strain curve is shown. Determine the strength coefficient $K$ and the work-hardening exponent $n$. Give your answer as the value of work_hardening_exponent_n.
A 0.25 -in.-diameter copper bar is to be cold worked $63 \%$ in tension. Find the final diameter. Give your answer as the value of final_diameter_in.

A 3105 aluminum plate is reduced from 1.75 in . to 1.15 in . Determine the final properties of the plate. Note 3105 designates a special composition of aluminum alloy. (See Figure.) Give your answer as a tuple: (tensile_strength_psi, yield_strength_psi, elongation_percent).

We want a $\mathrm{Cu}-30 \% \mathrm{Zn}$ brass plate originally 1.2 in. thick to have a yield strength greater than $50,000 \mathrm{psi}$ and a $\%$ elongation of at least $10 \%$. What range of final thicknesses must be obtained? (See Figure.) Give your answer as a tuple: (min_final_thickness_in, max_final_thickness_in).

A 3105 aluminum plate previously cold worked $20 \%$ is 2 in . thick. It is then cold worked further to 1.3 in . Calculate the total percent cold work and determine the final properties of the plate. (Note: 3105 designates a special composition of aluminum alloy.) (See Figure.) Give your answer as a tuple: (total_percent_cold_work, tensile_strength_psi, yield_strength_psi, elongation_percent).
We want to draw a 0.3 -in.-diameter copper wire having a yield strength of $20,000 \mathrm{psi}$ into a $0.25-\mathrm{in}$. diameter wire. Calculate the draw force, assuming no friction.

Using the data in Table, plot the recrystallization temperature versus the melting temperature of each metal, using absolute temperatures (kelvin). Measure the slope and compare with the expected relationship between these two temperatures. Is our approximation a good one? Give your answer as the value of slope.

\hline Metal & Melting Temperature ( ${ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ) & Recrystallization Temperature ( ${ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ) \\
\hline Sn & 232 & -4 \\
\hline Pb & 327 & -4 \\
\hline Zn & 420 & 10 \\
\hline Al & 660 & 150 \\
\hline Mg & 650 & 200 \\
\hline Ag & 962 & 200 \\

\hline Fe & 1538 & 450 \\
\hline Ni & 1453 & 600 \\
\hline Mo & 2610 & 900 \\
\hline W & 3410 & 1200 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

Suppose that liquid nickel is undercooled until homogeneous nucleation occurs. Calculate (a) the critical radius of the nucleus required and (b) the number of nickel atoms in the nucleus. Assume that the lattice parameter of the solid FCC nickel is 0.356 nm. Give your answer as a tuple: (critical_radius_Å, number_of_atoms).

Suppose that solid nickel was able to nucleate homogeneously with an undercooling of only $22^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. How many atoms would have to group together spontaneously for this occur? Assume that the lattice parameter of the solid FCC nickel is 0.356 nm. Give your answer as the value of number_of_atoms.

Calculate the fraction of solidification that occurs dendritically when iron nucleates (a) at $10^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ undercooling; (b) at $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ undercooling; and (c) homogeneously. The specific heat of iron is $5.78 \mathrm{~J} /\left(\mathrm{cm}^3 .{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)$. Give your answer as a tuple: (fraction_at_10C_undercooling, fraction_at_100C_undercooling, fraction_homogeneously).

Analysis of a nickel casting suggests that $28 \%$ of the solidification process occurred in a dendritic manner. Calculate the temperature at which nucleation occurred. The specific heat of nickel is $4.1 \mathrm{~J} /\left(\mathrm{cm}^3 \cdot{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)$. Give your answer as the value of nucleation_temperature.

A 5 -cm-diameter sphere solidifies in 1050 s . Calculate the solidification time for a 0.3 cm $\times 10 \mathrm{~cm} \times 20 \mathrm{~cm}$ plate cast under the same conditions. Assume that $n=2$. Give your answer as the value of solidification_time_s.

Figure shows a cylindrical riser attached to a casting. Compare the solidification times for each casting section and the riser and determine whether the riser will be effective. Give your answer as a tuple: (V_A_riser, V_A_thick, V_A_thin).

A 4-in.-diameter sphere of liquid copper is allowed to solidify, producing a spherical shrinkage cavity in the center of the casting. Compare the volume and diameter of the shrinkage cavity in the copper casting to that obtained when a 4 in. sphere of liquid iron is allowed to solidify. Give your answer as a tuple: (diameter_copper_in, diameter_iron_in).

A $2 \mathrm{~cm} \times 4 \mathrm{~cm} \times 6 \mathrm{~cm}$ magnesium casting is produced. After cooling to room temperature, the casting is found to weigh 80 g. Determine (a) the volume of the shrinkage cavity at the center of the casting and (b) the percent shrinkage that must have occurred during solidification. Give your answer as a tuple: (shrinkage_cavity_volume_cm3, percent_shrinkage).

Liquid magnesium is poured into a 2 cm $\times 2 \mathrm{~cm} \times 24 \mathrm{~cm}$ mold and, as a result of directional solidification, all of the solidification shrinkage occurs along the 24 cm length of the casting. Determine the length of the casting immediately after solidification is completed. Give your answer as the value of final_length_cm.

From Figure, find the solubility of hydrogen in liquid aluminum just before solidification begins when the partial pressure of hydrogen is 1 atm. Determine the solubility of hydrogen (in $\mathrm{cm}^3 / 100 \mathrm{~g}$ Al) at the same temperature if the partial pressure were reduced to 0.01 atm.

How many grams of nickel must be added to 500 grams of copper to produce an alloy that has a liquidus temperature of $1350^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$? What is the ratio of the number of nickel atoms to copper atoms in this alloy? [See Figure.] Give your answer as a tuple: (grams_of_nickel, Ni_to_Cu_atom_ratio).
How many grams of MgO must be added to 1 kg of NiO to produce a ceramic that has a solidus temperature of $2200^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ? [See Figure.]

We would like to produce a solid $\mathrm{MgO}-\mathrm{FeO}$ ceramic that contains equal mol percentages of MgO and FeO at $1200^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Determine the $\mathrm{wt} \% \mathrm{FeO}$ in the ceramic. (See Figure.) Give your answer as the value of wt_percentage_FeO.

A Nb-W alloy held at $2800^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is partly liquid and partly solid. (a) If possible, determine the composition of each phase in the alloy, and (b) if possible, determine the amount of each phase in the alloy. (See Figure.) Give your answer as a tuple: (composition_liquid_phase_wt_percent_W, composition_solid_phase_wt_percent_W).

Suppose a 1200 lb bath of a $\mathrm{Nb}-40 \mathrm{wt} \% \mathrm{~W}$ alloy is held at $2800^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. How many pounds of tungsten can be added to the bath before any solid forms? How many pounds of tungsten must be added to cause the entire bath to be solid? (See Figure.) Give your answer as a tuple: (tungsten_added_before_solid_forms_lb, tungsten_added_for_entire_bath_solid_lb).

A Nb-35\% W alloy is allowed to solidify. Determine (a) the composition of the first solid to form, and (b) the composition of the last liquid to solidify under equilibrium conditions. (See Figure.) Give your answer as a tuple: (first_solid_composition_W_percent, last_liquid_composition_W_percent).

An intermetallic compound is found for $38 \mathrm{wt} . \% \mathrm{Sn}$ in the $\mathrm{Cu}-\mathrm{Sn}$ phase diagram. Determine the formula for the compound. Give your answer as the value of compound_formula.

An intermetallic compound is found for $10 \mathrm{wt} . \% \mathrm{Si}$ in the $\mathrm{Cu}-\mathrm{Si}$ phase diagram. Determine the formula for the compound. Give your answer as the value of compound_formula.
A Pb-Sn alloy contains $45 \% \alpha$ and $55 \% \beta$ at $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. What is the percentage of Sn in the alloy?

A Pb-Sn alloy contains $23 \%$ primary $\alpha$ and $77 \%$ eutectic microconstituent immediately after the eutectic reaction has been completed. Determine the composition of the alloy. Give your answer as the value of alloy_composition_percent_Sn.
Observation of a microstructure shows that there is $28 \%$ eutectic and $72 \%$ primary $\beta$ in an $\mathrm{Al}-\mathrm{Li}$ alloy. Calculate the percentage of Li composition in the alloy.

Calculate the total amount of $\alpha$ and $\beta$ and the amount of each microconstituent in a $\mathrm{Pb}-50 \% \mathrm{Sn}$ alloy at $182^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. What fraction of the total $\alpha$ in the alloy is contained in the eutectic microconstituent? Give your answer as a tuple: (total_alpha_percent, total_beta_percent, primary_alpha_percent, eutectic_percent, fraction_eutectic_alpha).

Determine the constants c and n in Equation 12-2 ($f=1-\exp \left(-\mathrm{c} t^{\mathrm{n}}\right)$) that describe the rate of crystallization of polypropylene at $140^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. (See Figure.) Give your answer as a tuple: (c, n).
A steel contains $8 \%$ cementite and $92 \%$ ferrite at room temperature. Estimate the carbon content of the steel.
A steel contains $18 \%$ pearlite and $82 \%$ primary ferrite at room temperature. Estimate the carbon content of the steel.
A steel contains $55 \% \alpha$ and $45 \% \gamma$ at $750^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Estimate the carbon content of the steel. Give your answer as the value of carbon_content_percent.

A steel is heated until $40 \%$ austenite, with a carbon content of $0.5 \%$, forms. Estimate the temperature and the overall carbon content of the steel. Give your answer as a tuple: (temperature, overall_carbon_content).

An isothermally transformed eutectoid steel is found to have a yield strength of 410 MPa. Estimate (a) the transformation temperature and (b) the interlamellar spacing in the pearlite. Give your answer as a tuple: (transformation_temperature_C, interlamellar_spacing_cm).
A eutectoid steel has been heated to $800^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ for 1 h , quenched to $350^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and held for 750 s , and finally quenched to room temperature. What is the hardness?
A eutectoid steel has been heated to $800^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, quenched to $300^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and held for 10 s , and finally quenched to room temperature. What is the hardness?

A steel microstructure contains $75 \%$ martensite and $25 \%$ ferrite; the composition of the martensite is $0.6 \% \mathrm{C}$. Using Figure, determine (a) the temperature from which the steel was quenched and (b) the carbon content of the steel. Give your answer as a tuple: (quenching_temperature_C, carbon_content_percent).

A steel containing $0.8 \% \mathrm{C}$ is quenched to produce all martensite. Estimate the volume change that occurs, assuming that the lattice parameter of the austenite is $3.6 \AA$.  What is the volume change percent?

Describe the complete heat treatment required to produce a quenched and tempered eutectoid steel having an HRC hardness of less than 50. Include appropriate temperatures. Give your answer as a tuple: (austenitizing_temperature_C, tempering_temperature_C).

A 1030 steel is given an improper quench and temper heat treatment, producing a final structure composed of $60 \%$ martensite and $40 \%$ ferrite. Estimate the carbon content of the martensite and the austenitizing temperature that was used. What austenitizing temperature would you recommend? Give your answer as a tuple: (carbon_content_percent, austenitizing_temperature_used_C, recommended_austenitizing_temperature_C).

We would like to obtain a hardness of HRC 38 to 40 in a quenched steel. What range of cooling rates would we have to obtain for the following steels? Are some steels inappropriate? (a) 4340 (b) 8640 (c) 9310 (d) 4320 (e) 1050 (f) 1080. Give your answer as a tuple: (cooling_rate_range_9310, cooling_rate_range_1050).

A part produced from a 4320 steel has a hardness of HRC 35 at a critical location after quenching. Determine (a) the cooling rate at that location, and (b) the microstructure and hardness that would be obtained if the part were made of a 1080 steel. Give your answer as the value of cooling_rate_C_per_s.

A steel bar is to be quenched in agitated water. Determine the maximum diameter of the bar that will produce a minimum hardness of HRC 40 if the bar is (a) 1050 (b) 1080 (c) 4320 (d) 8640 (e) 4340. Give your answer as a tuple: (max_diameter_1050_in, max_diameter_4320_in).

You would like to produce a gray iron casting that freezes with no primary austenite or graphite. If the carbon content in the iron is $3.5 \%$, what percentage of silicon must you add? Give your answer as the value of percentage_silicon.

Suppose, by rapid solidification from the liquid state, that a supersaturated $\mathrm{Al}-7 \%$ Li alloy can be produced and subsequently aged. Compare the amount of $\beta$ that will form in this alloy with that formed in a 2090 alloy. Give your answer as a tuple: (beta_in_Al_7Li, beta_in_2090_alloy).

Suppose a 24 -in.-long round bar is to support a load of 400 lb without any permanent deformation. Calculate the minimum diameter of the bar if it is made from (a) AZ80A-T5 magnesium alloy and (b) 6061-T6 aluminum alloy. Calculate the weight of the bar and the approximate cost (based on pure Mg and $\mathrm{Al})$ in each case. Give your answer as a tuple: (min_diameter_AZ80A_in, weight_AZ80A_lb, cost_AZ80A_USD, min_diameter_6061_in, weight_6061_lb, cost_6061_USD).

Compare the percent increase in the yield strength of commercially pure annealed aluminum, magnesium, and copper by strain hardening. Explain the differences observed. Give your answer as a tuple: (percent_increase_Al, percent_increase_Mg, percent_increase_Cu).

Determine the specific strength of the strongest $\mathrm{Al}, \mathrm{Mg}, \mathrm{Cu}, \mathrm{Ti}$, and Ni alloys. Use the densities of the pure metals, in $\mathrm{lb} / \mathrm{in} .^3$, in your calculations. Give your answer as a tuple: (specific_strength_Al, specific_strength_Cu, specific_strength_Ni).

Silicon carbide ( SiC ) has a specific gravity of $3.1 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{cm}^3$. A sintered SiC part is produced, occupying a volume of $500 \mathrm{~cm}^3$ and weighing 1200 g . After soaking in water, the part weighs 1250 g . Calculate the bulk density, the true porosity, and the volume fraction of the total porosity that consists of closed pores. Give your answer as a tuple: (bulk_density_g_cm3, true_porosity_percent, closed_pores_fraction).

How many grams of BaO can be added to 1 kg of $\mathrm{SiO}_2$ before the $\mathrm{O}: \mathrm{Si}$ ratio exceeds 2.5 and glass-forming tendencies are poor? Compare this with the case when $\mathrm{Li}_2 \mathrm{O}$ is added to $\mathrm{SiO}_2$. Give your answer as a tuple: (BaO_grams, Li2O_grams).

A stress of 1000 psi is applied to a polymer that operates at a constant strain; after six months, the stress drops to 850 psi. For a particular application, a part made of the same polymer must maintain a stress of 900 psi after 12 months. What should be the original stress applied to the polymer for this application? Give your answer as the value of original_stress_psi.

Data for the rupture time of polyethylene are shown in Figure. At an applied stress of 600 psi , the figure indicates that the polymer ruptures in 0.2 h at $90^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ but survives $10,000 \mathrm{~h}$ at $65^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Assuming that the rupture time is related to the viscosity, calculate the activation energy for the viscosity of the polyethylene and estimate the rupture time at $23^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Give your answer as the value of activation_energy_cal_per_mol.

Nickel containing $2 \mathrm{wt} \%$ thorium is produced in powder form, consolidated into a part, and sintered in the presence of oxygen, causing all of the thorium to produce $\mathrm{ThO}_2$ spheres 80 nm in diameter. Calculate the number of spheres per $\mathrm{cm}^3$. The density of $\mathrm{ThO}_2$ is $9.69 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{cm}^3$. Give your answer as the value of number_of_spheres_per_cm3.

Yttria $\left(\mathrm{Y}_2 \mathrm{O}_3\right)$ particles $750 \AA$ in diameter are introduced into tungsten by internal oxidation. Measurements using an electron microscope show that there are $5 \times 10^{14}$ oxide particles per $\mathrm{cm}^3$. Calculate the $\mathrm{wt} \% \mathrm{Y}$ originally in the alloy. The density of $\mathrm{Y}_2 \mathrm{O}_3$ is $5.01 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{cm}^3$. Give your answer as the value of wt_percent_Y.

Calculate the density of a cemented carbide, or cermet, based on a titanium matrix if the composite contains $50 \mathrm{wt} \%$ WC, $22 \mathrm{wt} \% \mathrm{TaC}$, and $14 \mathrm{wt} \% \mathrm{TiC}$. (densities of the carbides = $11.5 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{cm}^3$) Give your answer as the value of density.

An electrical contact material is produced by infiltrating copper into a porous tungsten carbide (WC) compact. The density of the final composite is $12.3 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{cm}^3$. Assuming that all of the pores are filled with copper, calculate (a) the volume fraction of copper in the composite, (b) the volume fraction of pores in the WC compact prior to infiltration, and (c) the original density of the WC compact before infiltration. Give your answer as a tuple: (volume_fraction_copper, volume_fraction_pores, original_density_WC).

How much clay must be added to 10 kg of polyethylene to produce a low-cost composite having a modulus of elasticity greater than $120,000 \mathrm{psi}$ and a tensile strength greater than 2000 psi ? The density of the clay is $2.4 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{cm}^3$ and that of the polyethylene is $0.92 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{cm}^3$. (See Figure.) Give your answer as a tuple: (clay_mass_range_kg).

Five kilograms of continuous boron fibers are introduced in a unidirectional orientation into 8 kg of an aluminum matrix. Calculate (a) the density of the composite, (b) the modulus of elasticity parallel to the fibers, and (c) the modulus of elasticity perpendicular to the fibers. Give your answer as a tuple: (density_g_per_cm3, modulus_parallel_psi, modulus_perpendicular_psi).

An epoxy matrix is reinforced with $40 \mathrm{vol} \%$ E-glass fibers to produce a $2-\mathrm{cm}$ diameter composite that is to withstand a load of $25,000 \mathrm{~N}$ that is applied parallel to the fiber length. Calculate the stress acting on each fiber. The elastic modulus of the epoxy is $0.4 \times 10^6 \mathrm{psi}$. Give your answer as the value of stress_on_fiber_MPa.

Short, but aligned, $\mathrm{Al}_2 \mathrm{O}_3$ fibers with a diameter of $20 \mu \mathrm{~m}$ are introduced into a 6,6 -nylon matrix. The strength of the bond between the fibers and the matrix is estimated to be 1000 psi . Calculate the critical fiber length and compare with the case when $1 \mu \mathrm{~m}$ alumina whiskers are used instead of the coarser fibers. What is the minimum aspect ratio in each case? Give your answer as a tuple: (critical_fiber_length_20um_cm, aspect_ratio_20um, critical_fiber_length_1um_cm, aspect_ratio_1um).

A microlaminate, Arall, is produced using five sheets of 0.4 -mm-thick aluminum and four sheets of 0.2 -mm-thick epoxy reinforced with unidirectionally aligned Kevlar ${ }^{\text {TM }}$ fibers. The volume fraction of $K^K \operatorname{Karlar}^{\text {TM }}$ fibers in these intermediate sheets is $55 \%$. The elastic modulus of the epoxy is $0.5 \times 10^6 \mathrm{psi}$. Calculate the modulus of elasticity of the microlaminate parallel and perpendicular to the unidirectionally aligned Kevlar ${ }^{\mathrm{TM}}$ fibers. What are the principle advantages of the Arall material compared with those of unreinforced aluminum? Give your answer as a tuple: (modulus_parallel_psi, modulus_perpendicular_psi).

A 0.01-cm-thick sheet of a polymer with a modulus of elasticity of $0.7 \times 10^6 \mathrm{psi}$ is sandwiched between two 4 -mm-thick sheets of glass with a modulus of elasticity of $12 \times 10^6$ psi. Calculate the modulus of elasticity of the composite parallel and perpendicular to the sheets. Give your answer as a tuple: (modulus_parallel_psi, modulus_perpendicular_psi).

Calculate the density of a honeycomb structure composed of the following elements: The two 2 -mm-thick cover sheets are produced using an epoxy matrix prepreg containing $55 \mathrm{vol} \% \mathrm{E}$-glass fibers. The aluminum honeycomb is 2 cm thick; the cells are in the shape of 0.5 cm squares and the walls of the cells are 0.1 mm thick. The density of the epoxy is $1.25 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{cm}^3$. Compare the weight of a $1 \mathrm{~m} \times 2 \mathrm{~m}$ panel of the honeycomb with a solid aluminum panel of the same dimensions. at $100 \%$ water content. Give your answer as a tuple: (density_g_per_cm3, honeycomb_weight_kg, solid_aluminum_weight_kg).

A sample of wood with the dimensions $3 \mathrm{in} . \times 4 \mathrm{in} . \times 12 \mathrm{in}$. has a dry density of $0.35 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{cm}^3$. (a) Calculate the number of gallons of water that must be absorbed by the sample to contain $120 \%$ water. (b) Calculate the density after the wood absorbs this amount of water. Give your answer as a tuple: (water_volume_gal, final_density_g_per_cm3).

We have been asked to prepare $100 \mathrm{yd}^3$ of normal concrete using a volume ratio of cement-sand-coarse aggregate of $1: 2: 4$. The water-cement ratio (by weight) is to be 0.5 . The sand contains $6 \mathrm{wt} \%$ water, and the coarse aggregate contains $3 \mathrm{wt} \%$ water. No entrained air is expected. (a) Determine the number of sacks of cement that must be ordered, the tons of sand and aggregate required, and the amount of water needed.(b) Calculate the total weight of the concrete per cubic yard. (c) What is the weight ratio of cement-sand-coarse aggregate? 

Give your answer as a tuple: (sacks_of_cement, tons_of_sand, tons_of_aggregate, water_needed_gal, weight_per_cubic_yard_lb).

A current of 10 A is passed through a $1-\mathrm{mm}$ diameter wire 1000 m long. Calculate the power loss if the wire is made from (a) aluminum (Conductivity $\left(\mathrm{ohm}^{-1} \cdot \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}\right)$ = $3.77 \times 10^5$) and (b) silicon (Conductivity $\left(\mathrm{ohm}^{-1} \cdot \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}\right)$ = $4 \times 10^{-6}$). Give your answer as a tuple: (power_loss_aluminum_W, power_loss_silicon_W).

A current density of $100,000 \mathrm{~A} / \mathrm{cm}^2$ is applied to a gold wire 50 m in length. The resistance of the wire is found to be 2 ohm. Give your answer as a tuple: (diameter_cm, voltage_V).

We apply 10 V to an aluminum wire 2 mm in diameter and 20 m long. If $10 \%$ of the valence electrons carry the electrical charge, calculate the average drift velocity of the electrons in $\mathrm{km} / \mathrm{h}$ and miles $/ \mathrm{h}$. Give your answer as a tuple: (average_drift_velocity_km_per_h, average_drift_velocity_miles_per_h).

Calculate the electrical conductivity of nickel at $-50^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and at $+500^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Give your answer as a tuple: (electrical_conductivity_at_minus_50C, electrical_conductivity_at_plus_500C).

After finding the electrical conductivity of cobalt at $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, we decide we would like to double that conductivity. To what temperature must we cool the metal? Give your answer as the value of required_temperature_degC.

The electrical resistivity of a beryllium alloy containing $5 \mathrm{at} \%$ of an alloying element is found to be $50 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{ohm} \cdot \mathrm{cm}$ at $400^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Determine the contributions to resistivity due to temperature and due to impurities by finding the expected resistivity of pure beryllium at $400^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, the resistivity due to impurities, and the defect resistivity coefficient. What would be the electrical resistivity if the beryllium contained $10 \mathrm{at} \%$ of the alloying element at $200^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$? Give your answer as a tuple: (resistivity_pure_Be_400C, resistivity_impurities, defect_resistivity_coefficient, resistivity_10at_percent_200C).

For germanium and silicon, compare, at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, the number of charge carriers per cubic centimeter, the fraction of the total electrons in the valence band that are excited into the conduction band, and the constant $n_0$. Give your answer as a tuple: (charge_carriers_per_cm3_Ge, fraction_excited_Ge, n0_Ge).

Determine the electrical conductivity of silicon when $0.0001 \mathrm{at} \%$ antimony is added as a dopant and compare it to the electrical conductivity when $0.0001 \mathrm{at} \%$ indium is added. Give your answer as a tuple: (electrical_conductivity_Sb, electrical_conductivity_In).

When a voltage of 5 mV is applied to the emitter of a transistor, a current of 2 mA is produced. When the voltage is increased to 8 mV , the current through the collector rises to 6 mA . By what percentage will the collector current increase when the emitter voltage is doubled from 9 mV to 18 mV ? Give your answer as the value of percentage_increase.

Calculate the displacement of the electrons or ions for the following conditions: (a) electronic polarization in nickel of $2 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{C} / \mathrm{m}^2$; (b) electronic polarization in aluminum of $2 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{C} / \mathrm{m}^2$ (c) ionic polarization in NaCl of $4.3 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{C} / \mathrm{m}^2$; and (d) ionic polarization in ZnS of $5 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{C} / \mathrm{m}^2$. Give your answer as a tuple: (displacement_nickel_m, displacement_NaCl_m).

Suppose we are able to produce a polarization of $5 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{C} / \mathrm{m}^2$ in a cube ( 5 mm side) of barium titanate. Assume a dielectric constant of 3000 . What voltage is produced? Give your answer as the value of voltage_V.

Calculate the capacitance of a parallelplate capacitor containing five layers of mica for which each mica sheet is $1 \mathrm{~cm} \times$ $2 \mathrm{~cm} \times 0.005 \mathrm{~cm}$. The layers are connected in parallel. Give your answer as the value of capacitance_microfarad.

Calculate and compare the maximum magnetization we would expect in iron, nickel, cobalt, and gadolinium. There are seven electrons in the $4 f$ level of gadolinium. Compare the calculated values with the experimentally observed values. Give your answer as a tuple: (Fe_magnetization_Oe, Co_magnetization_Oe).

An $\mathrm{Fe}-49 \% \mathrm{Ni}$ alloy has a maximum permeability of 64,000 when a magnetic field of 0.125 oersted is applied. What inductance is obtained and what current is needed to obtain this inductance in a 200-turn, 3-cm-long coil? Give your answer as a tuple: (inductance_G, current_mA).

A material has a linear absorption coefficient of $591 \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$ for photons of a particular wavelength. Determine the thickness of the material required to absorb $99.9 \%$ of the photons. Give your answer as the value of required_thickness_cm.

An alumina insulator for an electrical device is also to serve as a heat sink. The atomic radii of \(\mathrm{K}^{+}\) and \(\mathrm{Br}^{-}\) ions are 0.138 and 0.196 nm , respectively.

A piece of copper originally 305 mm long is pulled in tension with a stress of 276 MPa. If the deformation is entirely elastic, what will be the resultant elongation?

For some applications, it is necessary to harden the surface of a steel (or iron-carbon alloy) above that of its interior. One way this may be accomplished is by increasing the surface concentration of carbon in a process termed carburizing; the steel piece is exposed, at an elevated temperature, to an atmosphere rich in a hydrocarbon gas, such as methane \(\left(\mathrm{CH}_{4}\right)\). Consider one such alloy that initially has a uniform carbon concentration of \(0.25 \mathrm{wt} \%\) and is to be treated at \(950^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\left(1750^{\circ} \mathrm{F}\right)\). If the concentration of carbon at the surface is suddenly brought to and maintained at \(1.20 \mathrm{wt} \%\), how long will it take to achieve a carbon content of \(0.80 \mathrm{wt} \%\) at a position 0.5 mm below the surface? The diffusion coefficient for carbon in iron at this temperature is \(1.6 \times 10^{-11} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}\); assume that the steel piece is semi-infinite.

A relatively large plate of a glass is subjected to a tensile stress of 40 MPa. If the specific surface energy and modulus of elasticity for this glass are 0.3 J/m² and 69 GPa, respectively, determine the maximum length of a surface flaw that is possible without fracture.

The clear plastic bottles used for carbonated beverages (sometimes also called soda, pop, or soda pop) are made from poly(ethylene terephthalate) (PET). The "fizz" in pop results from dissolved carbon dioxide \(\left(\mathrm{CO}_{2}\right)\); because PET is permeable to \(\mathrm{CO}_{2}\), pop stored in PET bottles will eventually go "flat" (i.e., lose its fizz). A 20 -oz. bottle of pop has a \(\mathrm{CO}_{2}\) pressure of about 400 kPa inside the bottle, and the \(\mathrm{CO}_{2}\) pressure outside the bottle is 0.4 kPa.
(a) Given that the permeability coefficient of \(\mathrm{CO}_{2}\) through PET is \(0.23 \times 10^{-13}\left(\mathrm{~cm}^{3} \mathrm{STP}\right)(\mathrm{cm}) /\left(\mathrm{cm}^{2} \cdot \mathrm{~s} \cdot \mathrm{~Pa}\right)\). Assuming conditions of steady state, calculate the diffusion flux of \(\mathrm{CO}_{2}\) through the wall of the bottle.
(b) If the bottle must lose \(750\left(\mathrm{~cm}^{3} \mathrm{STP}\right)\) of \(\mathrm{CO}_{2}\) before the pop tastes flat, what is the shelf life for a bottle of pop?
Note: Assume that each bottle has a surface area of 500 cm^2 and a wall thickness of 0.05 cm.

Give your answer as a tuple (diffusion flux in (a), shelf life in (b)).

A continuous and aligned glass fiber-reinforced composite consists of 40 vol% glass fibers having a modulus of elasticity of 69 GPa and 60 vol% polyester resin that, when hardened, displays a modulus of 3.4 GPa. Compute the elastic modulus of the composite material,  but assume that the stress is applied perpendicular to the direction of fiber alignment.

Computation of Critical Nucleus Radius and Activation Free Energy

(a) For the solidification of pure gold, calculate the critical radius \(r^{*}\) and the activation free energy \(\Delta G^{*}\) if nucleation is homogeneous. Values for the latent heat of fusion and surface free energy are \(-1.16 	imes 10^{9} \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{m}^{3}\) and \(0.132 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{m}^{2}\), respectively. Use the supercooling value in Table 10.1.
(b) Now, calculate the number of atoms found in a nucleus of critical size. Assume a lattice parameter of 0.413 nm for solid gold at its melting temperature.
Give your answer as a tuple (critical radius, activation free energy, number of atoms).

Zinc undergoes corrosion in an acidic solution according to the reaction:
\[
\mathrm{Zn} + 2\mathrm{H}^{+} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Zn}^{2+} + \mathrm{H}_{2}
\]

The rates of both oxidation and reduction half-reactions are controlled by activation polarization.

(a) Compute the rate of oxidation of Zn (in $\mathrm{mol/cm^{2}\cdot s}$), given the following activation polarization data:

\[
\begin{array}{|c|c|}
    \hline
    \textbf{For Zn} & \textbf{For Hydrogen} \\
    \hline
    V_{\left(\mathrm{Zn}/\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\right)} = -0.763\,\mathrm{V} & V_{\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}/\mathrm{H}_{2}\right)} = 0\,\mathrm{V} \\
    \hline
    i_{0} = 10^{-7}\,\mathrm{A/cm^{2}} & i_{0} = 10^{-10}\,\mathrm{A/cm^{2}} \\
    \hline
    \beta = +0.09 & \beta = -0.08 \\
    \hline
\end{array}
\]


(b) Compute the value of the corrosion potential.

Give your answer as a tuple (rate of oxidation in (a), the value of the corrosion potential in (b)).

For intrinsic gallium arsenide, the room-temperature electrical conductivity is $3 \times 10^{-7}(\Omega \cdot \mathrm{~m})^{-1}$; the electron and hole mobilities are, respectively, 0.80 and $0.04 \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{V} \cdot \mathrm{s}$. Compute the intrinsic carrier concentration $n_{i}$ at room temperature.
Given the two figures, calculate the electrical conductivity of intrinsic silicon at $150^{\circ}C$ (423 K).

The electrical conductivity and electron mobility for aluminum are $3.8 \times 10^{7}(\Omega \cdot \mathrm{~m})^{-1}$ and $0.0012 \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{V} \cdot \mathrm{s}$, respectively. Calculate the Hall voltage for an aluminum specimen that is 15 mm thick for a current of 25 A and a magnetic field of 0.6 tesla (imposed in a direction perpendicular to the current).

A brass rod is to be used in an application requiring its ends to be held rigid. If the rod is stress-free at room temperature \(\left[20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\left(68^{\circ} \mathrm{F}\right)\right]\), what is the maximum temperature to which the rod may be heated without exceeding a compressive stress of 172 MPa ( $25,000 \mathrm{psi})$? Assume a modulus of elasticity of $100 \mathrm{GPa}\left(14.6 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{psi}\right)$ for brass.
The fraction of nonreflected light that is transmitted through a 200-mm thickness of glass is 0.98. Calculate the absorption coefficient of this material.

Calculate the saturation magnetization for \(\mathrm{Fe}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}\), given that each cubic unit cell contains \(8 \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}\) and \(16 \mathrm{Fe}^{3+}\) ions and that the unit cell edge length is 0.839 nm.

(a) Compute the density of totally crystalline polyethylene. The orthorhombic unit cell for polyethylene is shown in ; also, the equivalent of two ethylene repeat units is contained within each unit cell.
(b) Using the answer to part (a), calculate the percent crystallinity of a branched polyethylene that has a density of 0.925 g/cm³. The density for the totally amorphous material is 0.870 g/cm³.
Give your answer as a tuple (density of totally crystalline polyethylene, percent crystallinity of branched polyethylene).

Question: A continuous and aligned glass fiber-reinforced composite consists of 40 vol% glass fibers having a modulus of elasticity of 69 GPa and 60 vol% polyester resin that, when hardened, displays a modulus of 3.4 GPa.
(a) Compute the modulus of elasticity of this composite in the longitudinal direction.
(b) If the cross-sectional area is 250 mm² and a stress of 50 MPa is applied in this longitudinal direction, compute the magnitude of the load carried by each of the fiber and matrix phases.
(c) Determine the strain that is sustained by each phase when the stress in part (b) is applied. Give your answer as a tuple (modulus of elasticity, load by fiber, load by matrix, strain by fiber, strain by matrix).

High-purity silicon is doped with $10^{23}\,\text{m}^{-3}$ arsenic atoms. 
(a) Calculate the room-temperature (298K) electrical conductivity of this material.
(b) Compute the conductivity at $100^{\circ}\text{C}$ (373 K).

Give your answer as a tuple (room temp conductivity, conductivity at 373K)

Question: One-half of an electrochemical cell consists of a pure nickel electrode in a solution of \(\mathrm{Ni}^{2+}\) ions; the other half is a cadmium electrode immersed in a \(\mathrm{Cd}^{2+}\) solution.
(a) If the cell is a standard one, write the spontaneous overall reaction and calculate the voltage that is generated.
(b) Compute the cell potential at \(25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\) if the \(\mathrm{Cd}^{2+}\) and \(\mathrm{Ni}^{2+}\) concentrations are 0.5 and \(10^{-3} M\), respectively.
Give your answer as a tuple (voltage for part a, cell potential for part b).

Consider a parallel-plate capacitor having an area of $6.45 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~m}^{2} (1 \mathrm{in} .^{2})$ and a plate separation of $2 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}(0.08 \mathrm{in}$.) across which a potential of 10 V is applied. If a material having a dielectric constant of 6.0 is positioned within the region between the plates, compute the following: (a) The capacitance (b) The magnitude of the charge stored on each plate (c) The dielectric displacement $D$ (d) The polarization. Give your answer as a tuple (capacitance, charge stored on each plate, dielectric displacement, polarization).

Nickel has density ρ = 8.90 g/cm³ and each Ni atom carries a magnetic moment of 0.60 μB.  (Bohr magneton μB = 9.274×10⁻²⁴ A·m², Ni atomic weight A = 58.71 g/mol, NA = 6.022×10²³ mol⁻¹.)  Calculate:  
(a) the saturation magnetization Ms (in A/m),  
(b) the saturation flux density Bs = μ₀Ms (in T).

Give your answer as a tuple (saturation magnetization, saturation flux density).

For copper, estimate the critical radius, $r^*$, and the activation energy, $\Delta G^*$, at $0.01^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, 1.0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, and $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ below $T_{\mathrm{m}}$. For copper, $T_{\mathrm{m}}=1083^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, \Delta H_{\mathrm{f}}=205 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{kg}$, $\rho=8.57 \times 10^3 \mathrm{kg} / \mathrm{m}^3$, and $\gamma_{\mathrm{LS}}=0.177 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{m}^2$.  Give your answer as a tuple: (critical_radius_0.01, critical_radius_1.0, critical_radius_100, activation_energy_0.01, activation_energy_1.0, activation_energy_100).

Suppose a melt is somehow undercooled by $0.18 T_{\mathrm{m}}$ before nucleation occurs and that as freezing occurs, release of the latent heat raises the temperature of the melt without heat transfer to the surroundings. Calculate the fraction, $f$, of the melt that freezes before the temperature of the liquid raises back to $T_{\mathrm{m}}$. Assume a typical value for heat capacity, $C=25 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol}$ and that $\Delta H_{\mathrm{f}}=T_{\mathrm{m}} \Delta S_{\mathrm{i}}$, where $\Delta S_{\mathrm{f}}=10 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol} \mathrm{K}$. 
Give your answer as the value of f.

Consider the freezing of pure aluminum. Estimate the temperature gradient in the solid near the liquid-solid interface if the interface moves at $5 \mathrm{~mm} / \mathrm{min}$. For aluminum, the thermal conductivity $k=4.8 \mathrm{Jm} /(\mathrm{Ks})$, the latent heat of freezing is $397 \mathrm{~kJ} /$ kg , and the density is $2.7 \mathrm{mg} / \mathrm{m}^3$. Give your answer as the value of temperature_gradient.

Estimate the length of dendrites in the freezing of copper alloys containing, (a) $5 \% \mathrm{Zn}$, (b) $5 \% \mathrm{Sn}$, and (c) $0.05 \% \mathrm{Sn}$. In each case, assume a thermal gradient of $G=0.5^{\circ} \mathrm{C} / \mathrm{mm}$ and assume the temperatures at the tips and bases of the dendrites are the equilibrium for liquidus and solidus temperatures the alloy. Assume the freezing ranges for the alloys are: (a) $5 \% \mathrm{Zn}, 6^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$; (b) $5 \% \mathrm{Sn}, 130^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$; and (c) $0.05 \% \mathrm{Sn}, 1.3^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Give your answer as a tuple: (dendrite_length_5pct_Zn, dendrite_length_5pct_Sn, dendrite_length_0.05pct_Sn).

Determine the eutectic morphology (platelets, rods, and isolated spheres) that minimizes the total interphase area as a function of the volume fraction, $f$, of minor phase and the separation distance, $\lambda$, assuming simple geometric arrangements. Give your answer as a tuple: (sphere_volume_fraction_upper_bound, rod_volume_fraction_upper_bound, platelet_volume_fraction_upper_bound).

Given the activation energy  Qgb=110.9 kJ/mol and pre‐exponential factor for grain‐boundary diffusion R=8.314 J/mol·K for Ag.
A. If the grain size of silver were doubled, by what factor would the net diffusion by grain boundary diffusivity change?
B. At what temperature would the net transport by grain boundary diffusion be the same as the grain boundary diffusional transport with the original grain size at 1000 K ?

Give your answer as a tuple: (diffusion change factor, temperature K).

It is known that in a certain carburizing atmosphere it takes 
\(
t_1 = 8\ \mathrm{h}
\)
at 
\(
T_1 = 900\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}
\)
to obtain a carbon concentration of 0.75 at a depth
\(
x_1 = 0.020\ \mathrm{in}\,.
\)
At a higher temperature
\(
T_2 = 950\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C},
\)
how long \(t_2\) is required to reach the same concentration at 
\(
x_2 = 0.030\ \mathrm{in}\,?
\)
Assume the diffusion coefficient follows
\(
D(T) \;=\; D_0 \exp\Bigl(-\tfrac{Q}{R\,T}\Bigr),
\)
with
\(
D_0 = 2\times 10^{-5}\ \mathrm{m}^2/\mathrm{s},
Q = 140\ \mathrm{kJ/mol}.
\)
Give your answer for \(t_2\) in hours.

A steel containing $0.20 \% \mathrm{C}$ is to be carburized in an atmosphere that maintains a carbon concentration of $1.20 \%$ at the surface.
A. After 10 h at $870^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, at what depth below the surface would you find a concentration of $0.40 \% \mathrm{C}$ ? (For diffusion of C in austenite, $D_{\mathrm{o}}=2.0 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~m}^2 / \mathrm{s}$ and $Q=140 \times 10^3 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol}$ ).
B. How long would it take, still at $870^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, to double the depth (part A) at which the concentration is $0.40 \%$ ?
C. What carburizing time at $927^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ gives the same results as 10 h at $870^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ?
Give your answer as a tuple: (depth, time_to_double_depth, carburizing_time_at_927C).

Estimate the ratios of the free surface energies of $\{111\},\{100\}$, and $\{110\}$ surfaces in fcc crystals when normalized to γ₁₀₀=1. 

Give your answer as a tuple: (gamma_111_ratio, gamma_100_ratio, gamma_110_ratio).
Determine how the free surface energy of a two-dimensional square lattice depends on the orientation of the surface. Give your answer as the value of surface_energy_per_area.
Calculate the electron-to-atom ratio in a copper-base alloy containing $15 \%$ zinc and $3 \%$ silicon. Give your answer as the value of electron_to_atom_ratio.
Find the solubility of b in a at $860^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, if $\Omega=-30,000 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol}$. Given universal gas constant is $R=8.134$.
Calculate the value of $s$ for the two-dimensional solutions shown in Figure 5.5a and $b$.(An a-b solid solution with $N_{\mathrm{a}}=N_{\mathrm{b}}=50$.) Give your answer as a tuple: (s_a, s_b).

Equation $w=\frac{\mathrm{G} \gamma^2}{2}=\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) \frac{\mathrm{Gb}^2}{(2 \pi r)^2}$ gives the energy/volume as a function of $r$. Calculate the distance from the core of a screw dislocation at which the energy/volume equals the heat of vaporization, $H_v$, and express this distance in terms of $r / \mathrm{b}$. Evaluate this critical value of $r / \mathrm{b}$ for copper. $\rho=8.93 \mathrm{mg} / \mathrm{cm}^3, G=77 \mathrm{GPa}, \mathrm{b}=0.255 \mathrm{~nm}, H_{\mathrm{v}}=4.73 \mathrm{MJ} / \mathrm{kg}$.

Give your answer as a tuple: (critical $r/b$, critical r of copper).
Using proper notation, write the Burgers vector for a dislocation corresponding to the shortest repeat distance in a bcc crystal. Give your answer as the value of burgers_vector.

Consider the reaction of two parallel dislocations of Burgers vectors $\mathbf{b}_1=(a / 2)[101]$ and $\mathbf{b}_2=a[0 \overline{1} 1]$. If they reacted, what would be the Burgers vector of the product dislocation?

When an $(a / 2)[1 \overline{1} 0]$ dissociates into $(a / 6)[\overline{2} 11]+(a / 6)[\overline{1} 2 \overline{1}]$, on which $\{111\}$ plane must the partial dislocations lie? Give your answer as the value of plane_normal.

Into which other pair of $(a / 6)\langle 211\rangle$ partials could the $(a / 2)[\overline{1} 10]$ dissociate? On what plane would they lie? Give your answer as a tuple: (partial_1, partial_2, plane).

Use Equation 8.13($f_{\mathrm{L}}=-\frac{G \mathbf{b}_1 \cdot \mathbf{b}_2}{2 \pi r}$) to make an approximate calculation of equilibrium separation of the partial dislocations. Neglect the fact that both partials cannot be pure screws. Give your answer as the value of equilibrium_separation.
Using the figure, find the activation energy for recrystallization of copper. Give your answer as the value of activation_energy.
Find the value of $n$ in the Avrami expression $f=1-\exp \left(-b t^\eta\right)$ that best fits the data for $135^{\circ}$ in Figure 9.10. Give your answer as the value of n.

Consider two neighboring grains, $A$ and $B$, in zinc as shown in Figure 14.2. Assume that the dimensions in the $x$ direction remain equal in both grains as the temperature is changed and the grains have the same cross-sectional area. Calculate $\sigma_x$ for a $1^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ temperature change.

The coefficients of thermal expansion of zinc are $\alpha_A=61.5 \times 10^{-60} \mathrm{C}^{-1}$ parallel to the c-axis and $\alpha_B=15 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{C}^{-1}$ perpendicular to the $c$-axis. The values of Young's moduli parallel and perpendicular to $c$ are $E_{\mathrm{A}}=35 \mathrm{GPa}$ and $E_{\mathrm{B}}=120 \mathrm{GPa}$.

Give your answer as the value of stress_x.

$\alpha$-titanium at $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ has an hcp crystal structure with $a=0.2950 \mathrm{~nm}$ and $c=0.4683 \mathrm{~nm}$ at $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. At $900^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, it transforms to $\beta$-titanium with a bcc crystal structure with $a=0.332$. The average coefficient of thermal expansion of $\alpha$-titanium is $8.4 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~K}^{-1}$. Calculate the $\%$ volume change as $\beta$-titanium transforms to $\alpha$-titanium on cooling.

C₀ (eutectoid composition) ≃ 0.77 wt % C  
Cα (ferrite solubility) ≃ 0.02 wt % C  
Cθ (cementite Fe₃C) ≃ 6.67 wt % C  

Calculate the relative widths of the ferrite and carbide platelets using the lever law. Give the value of thickness ratio.

Calculate the eutectoid temperature and composition for a 4140 steel, containing $0.40 \% \mathrm{C}, 0.83 \% \mathrm{Mn}, 0.30 \% \mathrm{Si}, 1.0 \% \mathrm{Ni}$, and $0.20 \% \mathrm{Mo}$. For plain carbon steel, the eutectoid temperature is approximately $723^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and the eutectoid composition is $0.77 \% \mathrm{C}$. The approximate change in eutectoid temperature ( ${ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ) per $1 \mathrm{wt} \%$ of alloying element is: Mn: $-15, \mathrm{Si}:+25, \mathrm{Ni}:-14, \mathrm{Mo}:+92$. The approximate change in eutectoid composition (\% C) per 1 wt\% of alloying element is: Mn: -0.054, Si: -0.108, Ni: -0.03, Mo: -0.337. Give your answer as a tuple: (eutectoid_temperature, eutectoid_composition)

Calculate the ideal diameter of a steel containing $0.45 \% \mathrm{C}, 0.5 \% \mathrm{Mn}, 0.2 \% \mathrm{Si}$, and $0.35 \% \mathrm{Cr}$ if the ASTM grain size number is 6 . The multiplying factors for $0.5 \% \mathrm{Mn}, 0.2 \% \mathrm{Si}$, and $0.35 \% \mathrm{Cr}$ are 2.667, 1.140, and 1.756.

Figure 20.8 shows that a stainless steel containing $14 \% \mathrm{Cr}$ requires $14 \% \mathrm{Ni}$ to be sure that no martensite can be formed. In a steel containing $12 \% \mathrm{Cr}, 1.0 \% \mathrm{Si}$, and $0.05 \%$ C, how much nickel is necessary to be sure that no martensite can be formed? Give your answer as the value of required_nickel_percentage.
